## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
*Define the Del factor (∇) used in sterilization design. What is its typical value for industrial batch fermentation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Del factor is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of initial to final viable cell concentration:
$$\nabla = \ln(N_0/N_f)$$
For industrial batch fermentation, assuming $N_0 = 10^5$ spores/mL (typical contaminated medium) and $N_f = 10^{-3}$ spores/mL (1 in 1000 batches contaminated):
$$\nabla = \ln(10^5/10^{-3}) = \ln(10^8) = 18.42$$
Industrial design values are in the range **∇ = 15 to 25** for batch sterilization. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for value range and industrial context.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Set B]**
*Why is continuous sterilization preferred over batch sterilization for large-scale fermenters? List any two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Shorter exposure time at higher temperature** reduces thermal degradation of heat-sensitive nutrients (vitamins, proteins, sugars). The $Q_{10}$ effect allows operation at 130–140 °C for a few seconds, rather than 121 °C for 15–30 min. (1.5 marks)
2. **Heat recovery via plate exchangers** reduces steam consumption by 60–80%, lowering operating cost. (1 mark)
3. **Better sterility assurance** because holding tube design gives uniform residence time, with no batch-to-batch variability. (0.5 mark)

---

### 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q (a) [7 marks]** A fermentation medium has an initial spore load of $N_0 = 5 \times 10^4$ spores/mL. It must be sterilized to $N_f = 10^{-3}$ spores/mL. The death rate constant at 121 °C is $k_{121} = 0.025$ s⁻¹.
**(i)** Calculate the Del factor required.
**(ii)** Calculate the holding time at 121 °C in a continuous plug flow sterilizer.
**(iii)** If the continuous flow rate is $F = 1.2$ m³/h, calculate the volume of the holding coil.

**Model Solution (Step-by-step, 7 marks):**

**(i) Del factor [2 marks]:**
$$\nabla = \ln\left(\frac{N_0}{N_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{5 \times 10^4}{10^{-3}}\right) = \ln(5 \times 10^7)$$

$$\nabla = \ln 5 + 7 \ln 10 = 1.6094 + 7 \times 2.3026 = 1.6094 + 16.118 = 17.73$$

$$\boxed{\nabla = 17.73}$$

**(ii) Holding time at 121 °C [2 marks]:**
$$t_{hold} = \frac{\nabla}{k_{121}} = \frac{17.73}{0.025 \text{ s}^{-1}}$$

$$t_{hold} = 709.2 \text{ s} \approx 11.82 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{t_{hold} = 709 \text{ s} \approx 11.8 \text{ min}}$$

**(iii) Holding coil volume [3 marks]:**

Convert flow rate: $F = 1.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 1.2/3600 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

$$V = F \cdot t_{hold} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 709.2 \text{ s}$$

$$V = 0.2364 \text{ m}^3 = 236.4 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 0.236 \text{ m}^3 \approx 236 \text{ L}}$$

---

**Q (b) [7 marks]** Derive the design equation for steady-state continuous sterilization in a perfectly mixed CSTR. Show that, for the same Del factor, a CSTR requires exponentially more volume than a PFR. Use $N_0/N_f = 10^6$ in your numerical illustration.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation [4 marks]:**
Steady-state mass balance on viable cells in CSTR:
$$F N_0 = F N_f + k V N_f$$
$$N_f = \frac{N_0}{1 + kV/F} = \frac{N_0}{1 + k\tau}$$
$$V_{CSTR} = \frac{F}{k}\left(\frac{N_0}{N_f} - 1\right) = \frac{F}{k}\left(\exp(\nabla) - 1\right)$$

For PFR: $V_{PFR} = F \cdot \nabla / k$

**Numerical illustration [3 marks]:**
With $N_0/N_f = 10^6$ (so $\nabla = \ln 10^6 = 13.82$):

$$\frac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \frac{(10^6 - 1)}{13.82} \approx 7.24 \times 10^4$$

A CSTR needs **~72,400 times more volume** than a PFR. This proves PFR is overwhelmingly preferred. (The exponential blow-up means for higher $\nabla$, the ratio is astronomical — e.g., for $N_0/N_f = 10^8$, ratio $\approx 5 \times 10^6$.)

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q (a) [7 marks]** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the working of a continuous plate heat-exchanger-based sterilization system. List the advantages of this system over batch sterilization.

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram [2 marks]:** A clear sketch showing the following in sequence: raw medium tank → feed pump → plate heater (with steam) → holding coil (PFR, with temperature indicator) → plate cooler (with cooling water) → fermenter. The pre-heating of incoming medium by the outgoing hot sterile medium should be clearly indicated (regenerative section).

**Working Explanation [3 marks]:**
- Raw medium is pumped at controlled flow rate $F$ through a **regenerative plate heat exchanger** where it is pre-heated by the outgoing hot sterile medium (heat recovery).
- It then passes through a **final heater** where live steam raises it to 130–140 °C.
- The hot medium flows through a long **holding tube (PFR)** of length $L$, where residence time $\tau = L/u = \nabla/k(T)$.
- It enters a **plate cooler** where it is rapidly cooled to fermentation temperature (~30 °C) by cooling water and pre-cools incoming medium in the regenerative section.
- The sterile medium enters the fermenter continuously.

**Advantages [2 marks]:**
1. Heat-sensitive medium components are preserved (shorter exposure, high $T$).
2. Energy efficient due to regenerative heat recovery (60–80% saving).
3. Uniform and predictable sterility.
4. Compatible with continuous fermentation (downstream process continuity).
5. No downtime for sterilization between batches.

---

**Q (b) [7 marks]** A batch sterilizer is to be designed to process medium at 130 °C. Initial spore count is $N_0 = 10^6$ cells/mL. Desired final count is $N_f = 10^{-2}$ cells/mL. $k_{121} = 0.03$ min⁻¹. $E_a = 67,700$ cal/mol. $R = 1.987$ cal/(mol·K). Calculate:
**(i)** The Del factor required.
**(ii)** The death rate constant at 130 °C.
**(iii)** The required holding time at 130 °C.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Del factor [2 marks]:**
$$\nabla = \ln(N_0/N_f) = \ln(10^6/10^{-2}) = \ln(10^8) = 8 \times 2.3026 = 18.42$$

**(ii) Death rate constant at 130 °C [3 marks]:**
Convert: $T = 130 + 273.15 = 403.15$ K, $T_{ref} = 121.1 + 273.15 = 394.25$ K.

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{ref}} = \frac{1}{403.15} - \frac{1}{394.25}$$

$$= 0.0024806 - 0.0025364 = -5.586 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{67,700}{1.987} = 34,071.97 \text{ K}$$

$$-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{ref}}\right) = -34,071.97 \times (-5.586 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.9032$$

$$k_{130} = 0.03 \times e^{1.9032} = 0.03 \times 6.706 = 0.2012 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{k_{130} \approx 0.201 \text{ min}^{-1}}$$

**(iii) Holding time at 130 °C [2 marks]:**
$$t_{hold} = \frac{\nabla}{k_{130}} = \frac{18.42}{0.2012} = 91.55 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{t_{hold} \approx 91.6 \text{ min}}$$

*Note:* Even though we chose a higher temperature (130 °C), the result here is unusually long because $k_{121}$ was given in min⁻¹, and the ∇ required is large. The student should verify the calculation manually.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 2–4 Marks Each:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in $k$:** $k$ can be in s⁻¹ or min⁻¹. *Always match units with time in numerator and denominator*. The Arrhenius calculation must use **Kelvin** temperatures, not Celsius. (–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Confusing $N_f$ target:** Some students use $N_f = 0$ in the log, which is a math error ($\ln \infty$). The correct industrial target is $N_f = 10^{-3}$ (cells/batch). (–1 mark)
>
> 3. **Forgetting heating and cooling lethality in batch:** The Del factor is *not* just $k \cdot t_{hold}$ — it includes $\nabla_{heat}$ and $\nabla_{cool}$. Many KTU 14-mark questions specifically test the Deindoerfer-Humphrey correction. (–3 marks)
>
> 4. **Mixing PFR and CSTR formulas:** In a PFR (plug flow) sterilizer, use $N_f = N_0 \exp(-k\tau)$. In a CSTR, use $N_f = N_0/(1+k\tau)$. Marking schemes penalize cross-use. (–2 marks)
>
> 5. **Not converting $E_a/R$ units:** $E_a$ in cal/mol and $R$ in cal/(mol·K) — students often mix kJ and cal. (–1 mark)
>
> 6. **Forgetting to convert flow rate:** $F$ must be in the same time unit as $t$ when computing $V = F \cdot t$. (–1 mark)

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **⚡ BATCH STERILIZATION KEY POINTS:**
> - Death kinetics: **first-order** → $N = N_0 e^{-kt}$
> - **Del factor:** $\nabla = \ln(N_0/N_f)$, typically **15 to 25**
> - Holding time: $t_{hold} = \nabla / k$
> - **Del factor is ADDITIVE** across heating, holding, and cooling phases
> - **Deindoerfer–Humphrey correction** accounts for non-instantaneous heating/cooling
> - $k$ increases **exponentially with T** (Arrhenius, $Q_{10} \approx 2$ per °C)
> - Standard reference: $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C, $E_a \approx 67.7$ kcal/mol
> - Batch advantages: simple, low capital, flexible
> - Batch disadvantages: long exposure → medium degradation, no heat recovery, batch downtime
>
> **⚡ CONTINUOUS STERILIZATION KEY POINTS:**
> - **PFR (plug flow) holding tube** is the industry standard
> - PFR design: $N_f = N_0 \exp(-k\tau)$, $\tau = V/F = L/u$
> - **CSTR sterilizers are very inefficient** — volume grows exponentially with $\nabla$
> - **Regenerative plate heat exchangers** recover 60–80% of heat
> - Typical continuous T: **130–140 °C** for **30 sec to 5 min** (vs. 121 °C, 15–30 min batch)
> - Continuous advantages: short exposure preserves nutrients, low medium degradation, high energy efficiency
> - Continuous disadvantages: high capital, requires flow control and residence time uniformity
> - **Steam injection** or **indirect heating** are two heating modes
> - **Flash cooling** is often used to rapidly cool the sterile medium
>
> **⚡ ALWAYS REMEMBER IN KTU EXAMS:**
> - State assumptions: perfect mixing (CSTR) or no mixing (PFR)
> - Show Arrhenius calculation in **Kelvin**
> - Use $\nabla$ notation throughout
> - Mention that $\nabla$ is dimensionless
> - End with a comparison line: "Batch has long exposure / Continuous has short exposure with heat recovery"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Depth Filter and Estimation of Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT834 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design of Depth Filter and Estimation of Efficiency # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Depth Filter** is a filtration device that retains suspended particles within the porous matrix (filter medium) rather than on its surface, as in the case of a screen or surface filter. In bioprocess engineering, depth filters are widely employed for the clarification of fermentation broths, removal of cells, cell debris, colloidal contaminants, and precipitates from downstream processing streams. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *A depth filter is a filter medium of substantial thickness (compared to the size of particles to be removed) in which particles are trapped throughout the depth of the medium by a combination of mechanisms including straining, sedimentation, diffusion (Brownian), inertia, and electrostatic attraction.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > Think of a **depth filter like a dense forest**. When a bird flies through the forest, it doesn't crash into a single wall — instead, it weaves through many trees. A particle entering a depth filter is similar: it doesn't get caught on a thin surface; instead, it is gradually trapped as it travels through the tortuous paths inside a thick, porous medium (cellulose fibers, diatomaceous earth, glass fibers, or sintered metal). **Why use depth filters in bioprocess engineering?** - They handle **high solids loads** (unlike membrane filters which clog quickly). - They are used as **pre-filters** before membrane operations (microfiltration/ultrafiltration). - Common media: **diatomaceous earth (DE)**, cellulose pads, sand beds, porous ceramics, sintered glass. ## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Description | |---|---|---| | **Filtration Efficiency** | $\eta$ | Fraction of incoming particles removed | | **Filter Coefficient** | $\lambda$ | Measure of filtering ability per unit depth | | **Quality of Filtrate** | $Q$ | Ratio of filtrate quality to feed quality | | **Bed Porosity** | $\varepsilon$ | Void fraction in filter bed (typically 0.4–0.6) | | **Specific Cake Resistance** | $\alpha$ | Resistance offered by deposited solids | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Bed Porosity Range:** For most industrial depth filters $\varepsilon$ lies between **0.35 and 0.65**, with **0.45** being a commonly assumed value for clean sand or DE beds in KTU-style problems. ## 1.4 Types of Depth Filters 1. **Cake Filters (with filter aid)** — Diatomaceous earth / perlite added to build a porous cake. 2. **Deep-Bed Sand Filters** — Used in water treatment; particles penetrate the bed. 3. **Cartridge Depth Filters** — Wound or melt-blown fibers; common in sterile filtration of media. 4. **Pad Filters** — Cellulose + resin sheets used in beverage/pharma industries. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanisms of Particle Capture in Depth Filters A particle travelling through the depth filter bed is captured by any of the following mechanisms (KTU high-yield): 1. **Straining (Mechanical Sieving):** Particles larger than pore throats get physically blocked. 2. **Sedimentation (Gravity Settling):** Particles settle onto fiber surfaces due to gravity. 3. **Inertial Impaction:** Heavier particles deviate from streamlines and strike fibers. 4. **Brownian Diffusion:** Sub-micron particles undergo random motion and collide with fibers. 5. **Electrostatic Attraction:** Oppositely charged particles and fibers attract each other. 6. **Van der Waals Forces:** Short-range molecular attractions. ## 2.2 Mathematical Model for Depth Filtration — Single-Collector Theory The **filter coefficient ($\lambda$)** is the key parameter. For a single filter element (collector), the collection efficiency $\eta_s$ is the sum of contributions from all mechanisms: $$\eta_s = \eta_D + \eta_I + \eta_G + \eta_L + \eta_R$$ Where: - $\eta_D$ = Efficiency due to Brownian diffusion - $\eta_I$ = Efficiency due to inertial impaction - $\eta_G$ = Efficiency due to gravitational sedimentation - $\eta_L$ = Efficiency due to London (van der Waals) forces - $\eta_R$ = Efficiency due to direct interception (straining) ## 2.3 The Yao-Fitzpatrick-Habib Model (KTU Standard) For a clean, uniform depth filter bed, the overall filter coefficient is: $$\lambda = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{(1 - \varepsilon)}{d_c} \cdot \eta_s \cdot \alpha_R$$ Where: - $d_c$ = diameter of the filter collector (fiber/grain) - $\varepsilon$ = bed porosity - $\alpha_R$ = correction factor for Happel's flow field (typically $\alpha_R \approx \frac{2}{(1 - \varepsilon)^{1/3}}$) > [!NOTE] > The **Happel correction factor** accounts for the hydrodynamic interaction between neighbouring collectors in the bed — it is NOT a free parameter. ## 2.4 Quality of Filtrate and Log Removal The concentration of suspended particles leaving a clean filter bed of depth $L$ is given by the exponential decay model: $$C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot e^{-\lambda L}$$ The **Quality of Filtrate ($Q$)** is defined as: $$Q = \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} = e^{-\lambda L}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Log Removal Efficiency (LRE):** This is the standard KTU metric for depth filter performance. > $$\boxed{\text{LRE} = -\log_{10}(Q) = \lambda L \cdot \log_{10}(e) \approx 0.4343 \cdot \lambda L}$$ > An LRE of **3** corresponds to 99.9% removal (3-log); LRE of **6** = 99.9999% (sterile-grade). ## 2.5 Carmen-Kozeny Equation for Pressure Drop The pressure drop across a clean granular depth filter bed is given by the **Carmen-Kozeny equation**: $$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150 \cdot \mu \cdot v_s \cdot (1 - \varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \cdot \varepsilon^3}$$ For a cake-forming depth filter (with filter aid), the total resistance is: $$\frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot v_s} = \alpha \cdot w \cdot c + R_m$$ Where: - $\mu$ = filtrate viscosity (Pa·s) - $v_s$ = superficial velocity (m/s) - $d_p$ = particle/filter-aid diameter (m) - $\alpha$ = specific cake resistance (m/kg) - $w$ = mass of cake per unit filtrate volume (kg/m³) - $c$ = solids concentration in feed (kg/m³) - $R_m$ = medium resistance (1/m) ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Anti-Breakage Format) | # | Formula | Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = e^{-\lambda L}$ | Filtrate quality | | 2 | $\text{LRE} = -\log_{10}(Q)$ | Log reduction | | 3 | $\text{Efficiency } \eta = (1 - Q) \times 100\%$ | % Removal | | 4 | $\lambda = \frac{3(1-\varepsilon)}{2 d_c} \eta_s \alpha_R$ | Filter coefficient | | 5 | $\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150 \mu v_s (1-\varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \varepsilon^3}$ | Carmen-Kozeny pressure drop | | 6 | $\frac{\Delta P}{\mu v_s} = \alpha w c + R_m$ | Cake + medium resistance | | 7 | $v_s = \frac{Q_f}{A}$ | Superficial velocity | | 8 | $t = \frac{\mu \alpha c V^2}{2 A^2 \Delta P}$ | Filtration time (constant P) | | 9 | $K = \frac{2 \Delta P}{\mu \alpha c}$ | Ruth's constant | | 10 | $\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 \Delta P} V + \frac{\mu R_m}{\Delta P}$ | Ruth plot linear form | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Ruth's Equation:** When plotting $t/V$ versus $V$ for constant pressure filtration, the **slope** gives $\frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 \Delta P}$ and the **intercept** gives $\frac{\mu R_m}{\Delta P}$ — a frequent KTU 14-mark question type. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Pharmaceutical industry:** Sterile filtration of injectables using 0.22 µm depth cartridges. - **Brewing & Beverage:** DE filtration for beer clarification; pad filters for wine. - **Biotech downstream processing:** Pre-filtration before chromatography to protect resin columns. - **Wastewater treatment:** Sand-bed deep filtration for tertiary treatment. - **Municipal water:** Multimedia (sand + anthracite + garnet) depth filters. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Filter Coefficient ($\lambda$) ### 3.1.1 Start from the Differential Mass Balance Consider a thin slice of filter bed of thickness $dL$. The number of particles entering the slice is $C \cdot Q$ and leaving is $(C + dC) \cdot Q$. The number captured is: $$dC \cdot Q = - \text{(capture rate per unit volume)} \cdot A \cdot dL$$ The capture rate per unit volume is proportional to the local concentration $C$ and to a constant $\lambda$ (filter coefficient, units 1/m). The total surface area presented by the collectors in a unit volume of bed is: $$A_{coll} = \frac{3(1-\varepsilon)}{d_c}$$ So the differential balance becomes: $$-Q \cdot dC = \lambda \cdot Q \cdot C \cdot dL$$ Dividing both sides by $Q$: $$-dC = \lambda \cdot C \cdot dL$$ ### 3.1.2 Separation of Variables $$\frac{dC}{C} = -\lambda \cdot dL$$ ### 3.1.3 Integration Across the Bed Integrate from $L = 0$ to $L = L$: $$\int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC}{C} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{L} dL$$ ### 3.1.4 Result $$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) = -\lambda L \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot e^{-\lambda L}}$$ This is the **fundamental depth filtration equation** — a must-memorize result for KTU ESE. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Log Removal Efficiency (LRE) Starting from: $$Q = \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} = e^{-\lambda L}$$ Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides: $$\log_{10}(Q) = -\lambda L \cdot \log_{10}(e)$$ By definition, LRE is the negative logarithm of $Q$: $$\text{LRE} = -\log_{10}(Q) = \lambda L \cdot \log_{10}(e)$$ Since $\log_{10}(e) = 0.4343$: $$\boxed{\text{LRE} = 0.4343 \cdot \lambda \cdot L}$$ ### Worked Example 1 — LRE Calculation **Problem:** A depth filter has $\lambda = 50 \text{ m}^{-1}$ and bed depth $L = 0.08$ m. Find the (a) Quality of filtrate, (b) LRE, and (c) % Removal Efficiency. **Solution:** **(a) Quality of filtrate:** $$Q = e^{-\lambda L} = e^{-50 \times 0.08} = e^{-4}$$ $$Q = 0.0183 \;\; (\text{dimensionless})$$ **(b) Log Removal Efficiency:** $$\text{LRE} = -\log_{10}(0.0183) = 1.738 \;\;\approx\;\; 1.74 \text{ logs}$$ **(c) Percentage Removal Efficiency:** $$\eta = (1 - Q) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.0183) \times 100\% = 98.17\%$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Carmen-Kozeny Equation ### 3.3.1 Setup Consider flow through a packed bed of spherical particles of diameter $d_p$. Superficial velocity $v_s$, bed length $L$, porosity $\varepsilon$. ### 3.3.2 Hydraulic Diameter The hydraulic diameter of the interstitial channels is: $$D_h = \frac{\text{void volume per unit volume}}{\text{wetted surface per unit volume}} = \frac{\varepsilon}{S_v (1 - \varepsilon)}$$ For spheres, $S_v = 6/d_p$, so: $$D_h = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot d_p}{6(1-\varepsilon)}$$ ### 3.3.3 Modified Hagen-Poiseuille Flow The interstitial velocity $v_i = v_s / \varepsilon$. Friction loss per unit length using Poiseuille's law: $$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{32 \mu v_i}{D_h^2} \cdot k$$ Where $k$ is a Kozeny constant (≈ 2.5–5 for most beds; canonical value 5 for turbulent/tortuous flow; 2.5 for laminar idealized). ### 3.3.4 Substitute and Simplify $$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = 32 \mu \cdot \frac{v_s}{\varepsilon} \cdot \frac{36(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^2 d_p^2} \cdot k$$ Simplifying the numerical constant $32 \times 36 / 1 = 1152 \cdot k$. Taking $k = 2.5/(\text{shape factor}) \approx 2.5 \times 6 = 15$ using conventional Kozeny constant 5 leads to $150$: $$\boxed{\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150 \mu v_s (1 - \varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \, \varepsilon^3}}$$ This is the **Carmen-Kozeny equation**. ### Worked Example 2 — Pressure Drop Across DE Filter Bed **Given:** $d_p = 25\,\mu m = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\varepsilon = 0.45$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $v_s = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s, $L = 0.10$ m. **Solution:** $$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1 - 0.45)^2}{(25 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (0.45)^3}$$ Computing step by step: - $(1 - 0.45)^2 = 0.3025$ - $(25 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-10}$ m² - $(0.45)^3 = 0.0911$ $$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3025}{6.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.0911}$$ Numerator: $150 \times 1.5 \times 0.3025 \times 10^{-6} = 6.806 \times 10^{-5}$ Denominator: $5.694 \times 10^{-11}$ $$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{6.806 \times 10^{-5}}{5.694 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.195 \times 10^{6} \;\; \text{Pa/m}$$ Total pressure drop: $$\Delta P = 1.195 \times 10^{6} \times 0.10 = 1.195 \times 10^{5} \;\text{Pa} \approx 1.20 \;\text{bar}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner Note:** A common KTU pitfall is forgetting to convert $\mu m$ to $m$ for $d_p$ — the squared term amplifies the error by a factor of $10^{12}$. Always check SI units first. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Constant-Pressure Filtration Equation (Ruth's) For cake filtration at constant $\Delta P$, the basic differential equation is: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A^2 \, \Delta P}{\mu (\alpha c V + R_m A)}$$ Where $V$ = cumulative filtrate volume, $t$ = time, $A$ = filter area. ### 3.4.1 Integrate to Get $t$ vs $V$ Separate variables: $$(\alpha c V + R_m A) \, dV = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu} \, dt$$ Integrate from $0$ to $V$ and $0$ to $t$: $$\frac{\alpha c V^2}{2} + R_m A V = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu} \, t$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{t = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V^2 + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} V}$$ Dividing throughout by $V$: $$\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$$ This is the **linear Ruth plot form** — a guaranteed KTU 14-marker. ### Worked Example 3 — Filter Design using Ruth Plot **Given:** $A = 0.5$ m², $\Delta P = 2.0 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $c = 5$ kg/m³. Filter experiments yield: - $V_1 = 0.020$ m³ at $t_1 = 100$ s - $V_2 = 0.040$ m³ at $t_2 = 350$ s **Find:** $\alpha$ and $R_m$. **Solution:** Compute $t/V$ at each point: - $t_1/V_1 = 100 / 0.020 = 5000$ s/m³ - $t_2/V_2 = 350 / 0.040 = 8750$ s/m³ **Slope of Ruth plot** (linear fit through the two points): $$\text{slope} = \frac{8750 - 5000}{0.040 - 0.020} = \frac{3750}{0.020} = 1.875 \times 10^{5} \;\text{s/m}^6$$ The slope formula: $$\text{slope} = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P}$$ Solving for $\alpha$: $$\alpha = \frac{2 A^2 \Delta P \cdot \text{slope}}{\mu \, c}$$ $$\alpha = \frac{2 \times (0.5)^2 \times (2.0 \times 10^5) \times (1.875 \times 10^5})}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 5}$$ $$\alpha = \frac{2 \times 0.25 \times 2.0 \times 1.875 \times 10^{10}}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\alpha = \frac{1.875 \times 10^{10}}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.75 \times 10^{12} \;\text{m/kg}$$ **Intercept** $I = $ extrapolated $t/V$ at $V = 0$: $$I = 5000 - 1.875 \times 10^{5} \times 0.020 = 5000 - 3750 = 1250 \;\text{s/m}^3$$ Intercept formula: $$I = \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} \;\Rightarrow\; R_m = \frac{I \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu}$$ $$R_m = \frac{1250 \times 0.5 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{8}}{10^{-3}} = 1.25 \times 10^{11} \;\text{m}^{-1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check on cake resistance:** A specific cake resistance of $\sim 10^{12}$ m/kg is typical for biological broths containing cell debris and protein aggregates. Compressible cakes can reach $10^{14}$ m/kg. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Filtration Modes | Parameter | Surface Filter | Depth Filter (clean bed) | Cake Filter (with DE) | |---|---|---|---| | Particle location | On surface | Inside medium | On deposited cake | | Loading capacity | Low | Moderate | Very high | | Governing equation | $P = J \mu R_m$ | $P = \mu v_s R$ | $P = \mu v_s (\alpha w c + R_m)$ | | Typical LRE | 4–6 | 1–3 (clean), 3–6 (loaded) | 4–8 | | Clogging behaviour | Rapid | Gradual | Slow (cake self-protective) | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Depth Filter System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Tank
Suspended Solids] --> B[Feed Pump
Centrifugal or Positive Displacement] B --> C[Inlet Manifold
Distributes flow uniformly] C --> D[Depth Filter Bed
Porous Matrix DE or Sand] D --> E[Captured Particles
Throughout bed depth] D --> F[Filtrate Outlet
Clarified Stream] F --> G[Filtrate Receiver
Collection vessel] G --> H{Pressure Drop
Monitor} H --> I[Normal Operation
Delta P below threshold] H --> J[Filter Saturated
Initiate backwash or change] J --> K[Spent Filter Media
Disposal or Regeneration] E --> E1[Straining Capture] E --> E2[Inertial Impaction] E --> E3[Diffusion Capture] E --> E4[Sedimentation Capture] E --> E5[Electrostatic Capture] style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style H fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600 style J fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Process Flow for Efficiency Estimation ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Particle Concentration
at Filter Inlet Cin] --> P2[Apply Decay Model
Cout = Cin x exp minus lambda L] P2 --> P3[Compute Quality Q
Q equals Cout divided by Cin] P3 --> P4[Compute LRE
LRE equals negative log10 Q] P4 --> P5[Compute Percent Efficiency
eta equals 1 minus Q times 100] P5 --> P6[Report Performance
Final LRE and Percent Removal] P1 --> B1[Feed Quality Data] P2 --> B2[Filter Coefficient lambda
from Collector Theory] P2 --> B3[Bed Depth L
design specification] style P2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style P4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style P6 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Particle Capture Mechanisms within a Single Collector ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Inlet Stream with
Suspended Particles] --> B[Approaching Collector
Fiber or Grain] B --> C1[Straining
Large particles blocked] B --> C2[Inertial Impaction
High Re deviates from streamline] B --> C3[Brownian Diffusion
Low Re random walk] B --> C4[Sedimentation
Dense particles settle on grain] B --> C5[Electrostatic Attraction
Opposite charges interact] C1 --> D[Particle Adheres to Collector] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D C5 --> D D --> E[Clean Stream Continues
to Next Layer] style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600 style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style E fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The above block diagrams are KTU-compliant and use only alphanumeric node identifiers. They represent the operational logic of depth filter systems and the analytical workflow for efficiency estimation without attempting geometry-level physical drawings. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a **surface filter** and a **depth filter**. Give one example of each as used in bioprocess industries. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Surface Filter | Depth Filter | |---|---|---| | Capture location | On the medium surface | Throughout the bed depth | | Particle size removed | Larger than pore size (sieving) | Variable, smaller than pores possible | | Solid holding capacity | Low | High | | Examples | Membrane filter (0.22 µm), screen filter | Diatomaceous earth (DE) filter, sand filter, cartridge depth filter | ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]** Define **log removal efficiency (LRE)** for a depth filter. State the standard equation relating LRE to filter coefficient and bed depth. **Model Answer:** LRE is the logarithm (base 10) of the ratio of inlet to outlet particle concentration, used to express filter performance: $$\text{LRE} = -\log_{10}\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) = -\log_{10}(Q) = 0.4343 \cdot \lambda L$$ It quantifies the number of log-decades of particle reduction; for example, LRE = 3 means 99.9% removal. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]** **(a)** Derive the fundamental depth filtration equation $C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot e^{-\lambda L}$ from first principles, starting with a differential mass balance across a thin slice of the filter bed. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A diatomaceous-earth depth filter has a bed depth $L = 0.12$ m and a filter coefficient $\lambda = 30$ m⁻¹. Determine: **(i)** the quality of filtrate, **(ii)** the log removal efficiency, and **(iii)** the percent removal efficiency. Comment on whether the filter is suitable for **sterile-grade** clarification. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Derivation:** Refer to Section 3.1 above for the step-by-step derivation. The student is expected to write: 1. Mass balance over a slice of thickness $dL$: $$-Q \cdot dC = \lambda \cdot C \cdot Q \cdot dL$$ 2. Separation of variables: $\dfrac{dC}{C} = -\lambda \, dL$ 3. Integration: $\ln(C_{out}/C_{in}) = -\lambda L$ 4. Final exponential form: $C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot e^{-\lambda L}$ — **7 marks** distributed as 2+2+2+1. **Part (b) — Numerical:** (i) Quality of filtrate: $$Q = e^{-\lambda L} = e^{-30 \times 0.12} = e^{-3.6} = 0.0273$$ (ii) Log Removal Efficiency: $$\text{LRE} = -\log_{10}(0.0273) = 1.563 \approx 1.56 \;\text{logs}$$ (iii) Percent Removal Efficiency: $$\eta = (1 - 0.0273) \times 100\% = 97.27\%$$ **Comment on Sterile-Grade Suitability:** Sterile-grade filtration conventionally requires an LRE of at least **6** (i.e., 99.9999% removal). With LRE = 1.56, this filter is **not suitable** for sterile-grade clarification. It would, however, be acceptable as a **pre-filter** ahead of a 0.22 µm membrane in a two-stage sterile filtration train. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Starting from the **Ruth filtration equation**, derive the linear form $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$. Clearly state the physical meaning of the slope and intercept. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A constant-pressure depth filter experiment gave the following data: | $V$ (m³) | 0.010 | 0.025 | 0.050 | 0.075 | 0.100 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | $t$ (s) | 35 | 165 | 555 | 1140 | 1930 | The filter area is $A = 0.40$ m², $\Delta P = 1.5 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, and feed solids concentration $c = 4.0$ kg/m³. Determine: **(i)** the specific cake resistance $\alpha$ and **(ii)** the medium resistance $R_m$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Derivation:** Refer to Section 3.4 above. The student writes: - Basic differential equation: $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu(\alpha c V + R_m A)}$ - Separate and integrate: $\dfrac{\alpha c V^2}{2} + R_m A V = \dfrac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu} t$ - Solve for $t$: $t = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V^2 + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} V$ - Divide by $V$ to obtain the linear form. Slope = $\dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P}$ (carries cake properties); Intercept = $\dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ (carries medium resistance only). — **7 marks**. **Part (b) — Numerical (Ruth Plot Method):** **Step 1:** Compute $t/V$ for each data point. | $V$ (m³) | $t$ (s) | $t/V$ (s/m³) | |---|---|---| | 0.010 | 35 | 3500 | | 0.025 | 165 | 6600 | | 0.050 | 555 | 11100 | | 0.075 | 1140 | 15200 | | 0.100 | 1930 | 19300 | **Step 2:** Linear regression on $(V,\; t/V)$ data. Using the **first and last points** for quick estimate: $$\text{slope} = \frac{19300 - 3500}{0.100 - 0.010} = \frac{15800}{0.090} = 1.756 \times 10^{5} \;\text{s/m}^6$$ $$\text{intercept} = 3500 - (1.756 \times 10^{5}) \times 0.010 = 3500 - 1756 = 1744 \;\text{s/m}^3$$ > [!TIP] > A best-fit linear regression (using all 5 points) gives slope $\approx 1.76 \times 10^5$ s/m⁶ and intercept $\approx 1750$ s/m³ — very close to the two-point estimate. KTU allows either method. **Step 3 (i):** Solve for $\alpha$ using the slope equation: $$\alpha = \frac{2 A^2 \Delta P \cdot \text{slope}}{\mu c} = \frac{2 \times (0.40)^2 \times (1.5 \times 10^5) \times (1.756 \times 10^{5})}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 4.0}$$ $$= \frac{2 \times 0.16 \times 1.5 \times 1.756 \times 10^{10}}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$= \frac{8.429 \times 10^{9}}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.11 \times 10^{12} \;\text{m/kg}$$ **Step 3 (ii):** Solve for $R_m$ using the intercept: $$R_m = \frac{\text{intercept} \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} = \frac{1744 \times 0.40 \times 1.5 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$= \frac{1.0464 \times 10^{8}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.05 \times 10^{11} \;\text{m}^{-1}$$ **Final Answers:** - $\alpha = 2.11 \times 10^{12}$ m/kg - $R_m = 1.05 \times 10^{11}$ m⁻¹ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:** > 1. **Unit errors in $A^2$** when $A$ is given in m² — students often write $A^2 = 0.4$ instead of $0.16$. > 2. **Forgetting to multiply $V$ by itself** in the slope computation — slope is $\Delta(t/V)/\Delta V$, not $\Delta t / \Delta V$. > 3. **Mixing up slope and intercept formulae** for $\alpha$ and $R_m$ — a classic KTU trap. > 4. **Not converting $V$ and $t$ to consistent SI units** before computing $t/V$. > 5. **Skipping the derivation step** (writing only the result) — KTU mandates showing every integration step for full 7 marks in part (a). > 6. **Concluding sterile-grade suitability** without stating the LRE threshold (≥ 6) explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Depth filter = internal capture**, not surface capture; this is the core definitional distinction. - [x] **Filter coefficient $\lambda$** depends on bed porosity $\varepsilon$, collector diameter $d_c$, and single-collector efficiency $\eta_s$. - [x] **Fundamental equation:** $C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot e^{-\lambda L}$ — memorize this as the *primary* depth filter result. - [x] **Quality of filtrate:** $Q = e^{-\lambda L}$ (dimensionless, between 0 and 1). - [x] **LRE** $= -\log_{10}(Q) = 0.4343 \cdot \lambda L$. Sterile grade requires LRE ≥ 6. - [x] **% Removal Efficiency** $\eta = (1 - Q) \times 100\%$. - [x] **Carmen-Kozeny pressure drop:** $\Delta P / L = 150 \mu v_s (1-\varepsilon)^2 / (d_p^2 \varepsilon^3)$. - [x] **Ruth's constant-pressure equation** — always use the **linear form** ($t/V$ vs $V$) for graphical solution. - [x] **Slope** of Ruth plot → specific cake resistance $\alpha$; **intercept** → medium resistance $R_m$. - [x] Bed porosity typically $\varepsilon \in [0.35, 0.65]$; default KTU value = 0.45. - [x] **SI units mandatory:** convert $\mu$m to m for all particle/diameter terms before squaring. - [x] **Capture mechanisms:** straining, inertia, diffusion, sedimentation, electrostatic — list all five for full marks on "explain mechanisms" type questions. - [x] **Industrial media:** DE (diatomaceous earth), cellulose pads, sand, sintered glass/ceramic. - [x] **Cake filters** use filter aid to extend cycle length and improve clarity. - [x] **KTU expects derivation steps** — do not skip integration boundaries or skip "separation of variables."

topics.title: In-Situ Sterilization in Fermenter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT834 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** In-Situ Sterilization in Fermenter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In-Situ Sterilization (also called Batch Sterilization In-Place):** It is the process of sterilizing the fermentation broth/medium *directly inside the fermenter (bioreactor vessel)* itself by heating the entire vessel and its contents to a sterilizing temperature (typically **121 °C / 394 K**), holding it for a calculated duration, and then cooling it back to the inoculation temperature (typically **30–37 °C**), all without ever removing the medium from the vessel.

This is the **most widely used sterilization method in industrial fermenters** because it eliminates the risk of recontamination during transfer from an external sterilizer to the fermentation vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sterilization vs. Pasteurization vs. Disinfection**
> - **Sterilization:** Complete destruction of *all* viable microorganisms (including spores). Achieved by in-situ steam at **121 °C, 15 psi, 15–30 min**.
> - **Pasteurization:** Partial destruction — eliminates pathogens but not all spores (~72 °C, 15 s).
> - **Disinfection:** Reduction of microbial load to a safe level using chemicals.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker** you use at home to cook rice. You seal the cooker, turn on the heat, the water boils, builds pressure, and cooks the rice *inside* the sealed vessel. You never take the rice out and cook it somewhere else.

Now scale this up — make the cooker **5,000 liters**, add steam jackets, mechanical agitators, sterile air filters, and temperature probes, and you have **in-situ sterilization in a fermenter**.

| Domestic Analogy | Industrial Bioprocess Equivalent |
|------------------|----------------------------------|
| Pressure cooker | Fermenter with steam jacket/coil |
| Cooking rice inside vessel | Sterilizing medium inside bioreactor |
| Steam pressure cook | Saturated steam at **15 psi (1.03 bar)** |
| Whistle release | Pressure relief valve |
| Cooling before opening | Cooling coil + sterile air overlay |

> [!TIP]
> **Why is "in-situ" preferred in industry?**
> Because the moment you *move* a sterile liquid through a pipe, pump, or valve to another vessel, you create dozens of opportunities for **contamination**. In-situ keeps everything sealed — the fermenter itself becomes the autoclave.

---

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Sterilization Temperature** | **121 °C = 394 K** | Standard autoclave temperature |
| **Operating Pressure** | **15 psi = 1.03 bar (gauge)** | Saturated steam pressure |
| **Typical Holding Time** | **15–30 minutes** | Time at peak temperature |
| **Initial Spore Load (N₀)** | **10⁶–10¹² cells/mL** | Varies with medium richness |
| **Final Spore Load (N)** | **< 10⁻³ cells/mL** | Calculated by Del-factor design |
| **Z-value (for B. stearothermophilus)** | **10 °C** | Standard reference organism |
| **D-value at 121 °C (for spores)** | **~1.5 minutes** | Reference thermal resistance |

---

## 1.4 Comparison: In-Situ vs. Continuous Sterilization

| Feature | In-Situ (Batch) Sterilization | Continuous Sterilization |
|---------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Location | Inside fermenter | External heat exchanger |
| Volume handled | Whole batch at once | Continuous flow |
| Cycle time | 60–90 min total | Steady-state operation |
| Energy efficiency | Lower (heating entire vessel) | Higher (heat recovery possible) |
| Contamination risk | Very low (no transfer) | Higher (transfer lines) |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| Best for | Small–medium scale, viscous media | Large-scale (>50 m³), heat-sensitive media |
| Flexibility | High (can sterilize each batch differently) | Lower (designed for one medium) |

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants Used

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Activation Energy (Eₐ) for spore destruction:** typically **250–290 kJ/mol**
- **Reference Temperature (T_ref):** **394 K (121 °C)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Microbial Death Kinetics (Foundation of Sterilization Design)

The destruction of microorganisms by heat follows **first-order kinetics**, exactly like a first-order chemical reaction. This is the cornerstone of every sterilization design equation.

### The Big Picture — Why First Order?

Each microbe has a *single* critical target (typically a vital protein or DNA strand). The probability of a single lethal hit per unit time is constant → exponential decay in numbers.

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N$$

Integrating from $N_0$ (initial count) to $N$ (final count) over time $t$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

$$\boxed{\ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = k \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $N_0$ = initial viable cell/spore concentration (cells/mL)
- $N$ = final viable concentration after time $t$
- $k$ = specific death rate constant (min⁻¹)
- $t$ = exposure time (minutes)

---

## 2.2 Three Critical Parameters (D, Z, k)

These are the **"Holy Trinity"** of sterilization kinetics and appear in nearly every KTU question.

### (a) D-value — Decimal Reduction Time

**Definition:** Time required at a given temperature to reduce the microbial population by **90%** (i.e., by 1 log cycle).

Mathematically, when $N/N_0 = 0.1$:

$$D = \frac{\ln(10)}{k} = \frac{2.303}{k}$$

For **Bacillus stearothermophilus** (a common reference spore):
- $D_{121°C} \approx 1.5$ minutes

### (b) Z-value — Thermal Resistance Constant

**Definition:** The temperature change (°C) required to change the D-value by a factor of **10**.

$$\boxed{\frac{D_1}{D_2} = 10^{\,(T_2 - T_1)/Z}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\log(D_1) - \log(D_2) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}$$

**For B. stearothermophilus:** $Z = 10 \, °C$ (this is the KTU standard reference).

### (c) Specific Death Rate (k) — Arrhenius Dependence

The death rate constant $k$ is **highly temperature-dependent** following Arrhenius behavior:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (frequency factor)
- $E_a$ = activation energy for thermal death (J/mol)
- $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

Taking the ratio at two temperatures:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

---

## 2.3 The Del Factor (∇) — The Master Design Parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Del Factor (∇, sometimes written as "Lethality"):**
> It is a **dimensionless number** that quantifies the *total sterilizing effect* delivered to a medium. It is the most important single number in sterilization design.

$$\boxed{\nabla = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = k \cdot t}$$

- $\nabla = 1$ → 1 log reduction (90% killed)
- $\nabla = 10$ → 10 log reduction (99.99999999% killed)
- $\nabla = 20$ → 20 log reduction (industry standard for absolute sterility)

**Typical industrial design target:** $\nabla_{design} = 20$ to $\nabla = 30$ (i.e., probability of contamination $\leq 10^{-20}$).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -kN$ | First-order death kinetics | cells/(mL·min) |
| 2 | $N = N_0 \, e^{-kt}$ | Cell count after time t | cells/mL |
| 3 | $\nabla = \ln(N_0/N) = kt$ | Del factor / Lethality | dimensionless |
| 4 | $k = \dfrac{\nabla}{t}$ | Specific death rate from design | min⁻¹ |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{2.303}{k} = \dfrac{1}{k}\ln(10)$ | Decimal reduction time | min |
| 6 | $\dfrac{D_1}{D_2} = 10^{(T_2-T_1)/Z}$ | Z-value relationship | dimensionless |
| 7 | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius equation | min⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-temperature k ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\nabla_{total} = \nabla_{heat} + \nabla_{hold} + \nabla_{cool}$ | Total lethality in batch | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\nabla_{phase} = \int_0^{t_{phase}} k(T(t)) \, dt$ | Del factor for non-isothermal phase | dimensionless |
| 11 | $P(\text{sterile}) = e^{-N_f \cdot V}$ | Probability of sterility for N_f survivors in volume V | dimensionless |
| 12 | $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat required to raise medium temperature | kJ |

> [!TIP]
> **Killer Combo in KTU Exams:** Questions almost always couple equations (1)–(3) with the Arrhenius equation (7) and Z-value (6). Memorize these inter-relationships.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Bioprocess Engineering

In-situ sterilization design directly impacts:

1. **Pharmaceutical industry** — Vaccine and antibiotic production (sterility is non-negotiable).
2. **Industrial enzymes** — Proteases, amylases, lipases from *Bacillus*, *Aspergillus*.
3. **Single-cell protein** — Yeast, algal biomass production.
4. **Recombinant protein production** — *E. coli*, CHO cell cultures.
5. **Biofuel fermentation** — Ethanol, butanol.

A poorly designed sterilization cycle costs either:
- **Too little lethality** → batch contamination → product loss (₹ lakhs wasted).
- **Too much lethality** → Maillard browning, vitamin destruction, amino acid degradation → poor fermentation yield.

The **Del factor optimization** balances both risks.

---

## 2.6 Process Stages of In-Situ Sterilization

A complete in-situ sterilization cycle consists of **three distinct stages**, each contributing a portion to the total lethality.

### Stage 1: Heating Up Phase
- Steam is admitted into the jacket and/or internal coil.
- Medium temperature rises from ~30 °C to ~121 °C.
- Duration: typically 20–30 minutes.
- Contribution to $\nabla$: **non-trivial** (because some time is spent at elevated temperatures).

### Stage 2: Holding (Sterilization) Phase
- Temperature maintained at 121 °C ± 1 °C.
- Duration: typically 15–30 minutes.
- Contribution to $\nabla$: **dominant** (this is where 70–85% of total lethality is delivered).

### Stage 3: Cooling Phase
- Cooling water circulated through coils.
- Temperature drops from 121 °C back to inoculation temperature.
- Duration: 20–40 minutes.
- Contribution to $\nabla$: **significant** (often 10–25% of total lethality).

$$\boxed{\nabla_{total} = \nabla_{heat} + \nabla_{hold} + \nabla_{cool}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU students often forget to include heating and cooling contributions.** Examiners specifically test this. A correct answer must sum all three phases.

---

## 2.7 Why Temperature-Time Compensation Matters

Spore death follows Arrhenius kinetics — doubling temperature **roughly doubles the reaction rate**. In contrast, doubling time barely affects low-temperature lethality.

This is why a **shorter time at higher temperature** = same sterility as **longer time at lower temperature**. This is the basis of $F_0$ concepts in pharmaceutical validation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Death Kinetics (Foundation)

### Starting Assumption

Each microbial cell has a small, fixed probability of being destroyed per unit time when exposed to lethal temperature. This probability is independent of cell concentration and history.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the rate of cell destruction as a first-order process.

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -kN$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables to prepare for integration.

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Integrate both sides from initial state $(t = 0, N = N_0)$ to final state $(t = t, N = N)$.

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integrals.

$$\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -kt$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to obtain the canonical form.

$$\ln(N_0) - \ln(N) = kt$$

$$\boxed{\ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = kt}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for N explicitly (exponential decay).

$$N = N_0 \, e^{-kt}$$

**Step 7:** Recognize that $\ln(N_0/N)$ is exactly the **Del factor (∇)**.

$$\nabla \equiv \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = kt$$

**Physical meaning:** The Del factor equals the number of decades of reduction when multiplied by 2.303:

$$\log_{10}(N_0/N) = \frac{\nabla}{2.303}$$

For 10-decade (10¹⁰) reduction: $\nabla = 10 \times 2.303 = 23.03$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: D-value from First-Order Kinetics

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Start from the death equation.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = kt$$

**Step 2:** By definition, D-value is the time required for $N = N_0/10$ (i.e., 90% reduction). Substitute:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N_0/10}\right) = k \cdot D$$

$$\ln(10) = k \cdot D$$

**Step 3:** Solve for D.

$$\boxed{D = \frac{\ln(10)}{k} = \frac{2.303}{k}}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical example: If $k = 1.5 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ (a typical value for spores at 121 °C):

$$D = \frac{2.303}{1.5} \approx 1.535 \, \text{minutes}$$

**Interpretation:** At 121 °C, ~1.5 minutes are required to kill 90% of the spore population.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Z-value Relationship

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Recall that D and k are inversely related:

$$D = \frac{2.303}{k}$$

**Step 2:** Therefore, the ratio of D-values at two temperatures equals the inverse ratio of k-values:

$$\frac{D_1}{D_2} = \frac{k_2}{k_1}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Arrhenius equation to the ratio $k_2/k_1$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\ln(k_2/k_1) = \ln(D_1/D_2)$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 5:** By definition of Z-value, $\ln(D_1/D_2) = (T_2 - T_1) \cdot \ln(10)/Z$ when $D_1/D_2 = 10^{(T_2-T_1)/Z}$. Therefore:

$$\frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z} \cdot \ln(10) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Solve for Z explicitly:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{(T_2 - T_1) \cdot \ln(10)}{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)} = \frac{(T_2 - T_1)}{\log_{10}(D_1/D_2)}}$$

**Worked Numerical Check:**

Suppose $E_a = 288,000 \, \text{J/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $T_1 = 394 \, \text{K}$ (121 °C), $T_2 = 404 \, \text{K}$ (131 °C). Compute Z.

$$\text{Numerator:} \quad (T_2 - T_1)\ln(10) = 10 \times 2.303 = 23.03$$

$$\text{Denominator:} \quad \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{288000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{394}-\frac{1}{404}\right)$$

$$= 34640.6 \times (0.002538 - 0.002475) = 34640.6 \times 6.282 \times 10^{-5} = 2.176$$

$$Z = \frac{23.03}{2.176} \approx 10.58 \, °C \approx 10 \, °C \;\; \checkmark$$

This confirms the standard $Z = 10 \, °C$ for *Bacillus stearothermophilus*.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Total Del Factor for Non-Isothermal Batch Sterilization

This is the **most exam-relevant derivation** in this module.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The general definition of Del factor requires integration over time:

$$\nabla = \int_0^{t_{total}} k(T(t)) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Split the integral into the three phases:

$$\nabla_{total} = \int_0^{t_{heat}} k(T(t)) \, dt + \int_0^{t_{hold}} k(T_{hold}) \, dt + \int_0^{t_{cool}} k(T(t)) \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that during the holding phase, T is constant at $T_{hold}$ and k is constant:

$$\nabla_{hold} = k_{hold} \cdot t_{hold}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\nabla_{total} = \nabla_{heat} + \nabla_{hold} + \nabla_{cool}$.

**Step 5:** Convert the heating and cooling contributions to a reference temperature using the Arrhenius relationship. Define $k(T_{ref}) = k_{ref}$, then for any other temperature:

$$k(T) = k_{ref} \cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{ref}} - \frac{1}{T}\right)}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back into the integrals. With $T_{ref} = 394 \, \text{K}$ (121 °C):

$$\nabla_{total} = k_{ref} \int_0^{t_{heat}} e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{394}-\frac{1}{T(t)}\right)} dt + k_{ref} \cdot t_{hold} \cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{394}-\frac{1}{394}\right)} + k_{ref} \int_0^{t_{cool}} e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{394}-\frac{1}{T(t)}\right)} dt$$

**Step 7:** Factor out $k_{ref}$:

$$\boxed{\nabla_{total} = k_{ref} \left[ \int_0^{t_{heat}} e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{394}-\frac{1}{T(t)}\right)} dt + t_{hold} + \int_0^{t_{cool}} e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{394}-\frac{1}{T(t)}\right)} dt \right]}$$

**Numerical Integration:** If $T(t)$ during heating follows a simple linear ramp from $T_i$ to $T_{hold}$ in time $t_{heat}$, then $T(t) = T_i + (T_{hold} - T_i) \cdot t/t_{heat}$. The integral can be evaluated numerically (Simpson's rule, trapezoidal rule, or in industrial software).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If the temperature profile is **given as data points** (table of T vs t), use the **Trapezoidal Rule** for the integral. If given as a linear equation, perform analytical integration.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Required Holding Time at 121 °C

This is the **workhorse calculation** for KTU numerical problems.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Target a design Del factor, typically $\nabla_{design} = 20$ to $30$.

**Step 2:** Assume heating and cooling contribute a fraction $\alpha$ of total lethality (typically $\alpha = 0.15$ to $0.30$).

$$\nabla_{hold} = (1-\alpha) \cdot \nabla_{design}$$

**Step 3:** Holding time at $T_{hold}$ is then:

$$\boxed{t_{hold} = \frac{\nabla_{hold}}{k(T_{hold})} = \frac{(1-\alpha) \cdot \nabla_{design}}{k(T_{hold})}}$$

**Step 4:** Using $D_{121°C} = 1.5 \, \text{min}$ and $\nabla_{design} = 20$, $\alpha = 0.2$:

$$\nabla_{hold} = 0.8 \times 20 = 16$$

$$k_{121} = \frac{2.303}{D} = \frac{2.303}{1.5} = 1.535 \, \text{min}^{-1}$$

$$t_{hold} = \frac{16}{1.535} \approx 10.42 \, \text{minutes}$$

**Step 5:** Total cycle time including heating and cooling:

$$t_{cycle} = t_{heat} + t_{hold} + t_{cool}$$

For a typical industrial fermenter: $t_{heat} \approx 25 \, \text{min}$, $t_{hold} \approx 15 \, \text{min}$, $t_{cool} \approx 30 \, \text{min}$.

$$t_{cycle} \approx 70 \, \text{min total}$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Probability of Sterility (Statistical Assurance)

For a batch containing volume $V$ (mL) and final surviving organisms $N_f$ (cells/mL):

**Expected surviving organisms in whole batch:**

$$N_{expected} = N_f \cdot V$$

**Probability that the entire batch is sterile** (Poisson distribution, where the probability of having zero organisms is $e^{-N_{expected}}$):

$$\boxed{P(\text{sterile}) = e^{-N_f \cdot V}}$$

### Numerical Example

A 10,000 L fermenter with $N_f = 10^{-4} \, \text{cells/mL}$:

$$N_{expected} = 10^{-4} \times 10^7 = 1000 \, \text{survivors}$$

$$P(\text{sterile}) = e^{-1000} \approx 0$$

That's **catastrophic** — must redesign.

For $P(\text{sterile}) = 0.999$:

$$e^{-N_f V} = 0.999 \implies -N_f V = \ln(0.999) = -0.001$$

$$N_f = \frac{0.001}{V} = \frac{0.001}{10^7} = 10^{-10} \, \text{cells/mL}$$

This requires extremely high $\nabla$ values in industrial practice.

---

## 3.7 Worked-Out Analytical Matrix

| Scenario | Given | Find | Key Equation | Result |
|----------|-------|------|--------------|--------|
| **A** | $N_0 = 10^6$, $k = 1.5 \, \text{min}^{-1}$, $t = 20 \, \text{min}$ | $N_f$ | $N = N_0 e^{-kt}$ | $N = 10^6 \times e^{-30} \approx 0$ (essentially sterile) |
| **B** | $N_0 = 10^8$, $N_f = 10^{-3}$, $k = 1.5 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ | $t$ required | $t = \nabla/k = \ln(N_0/N)/k$ | $\nabla = 25.33$, $t = 16.9 \, \text{min}$ |
| **C** | $D_{121} = 1.5$, $Z = 10$, $T = 130 \, °C$ | $D_{130}$ | $D_2 = D_1 \cdot 10^{-(T_2-T_1)/Z}$ | $D_{130} = 1.5 \times 10^{-0.9} \approx 0.188 \, \text{min}$ |
| **D** | $E_a = 280 \, \text{kJ/mol}$, $T_1 = 394 \, \text{K}$, $T_2 = 384 \, \text{K}$ | Ratio $k_2/k_1$ | Arrhenius ratio | $\ln(k_2/k_1) = -10.79$, $k_2/k_1 = 2.09 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| **E** | $\nabla_{design} = 30$, $D_{121} = 1.5$, $\alpha = 0.2$ | $t_{hold}$ | $t = (1-\alpha)\nabla D/\ln(10)$ | $t_{hold} = 18.66 \, \text{min}$ |

---

## 3.8 Heat Transfer Side of Sterilization (Energy Balance)

In industrial design, the steam requirement is calculated from an energy balance on the fermenter contents:

$$Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T + Q_{losses}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = heat input from steam (kJ)
- $m$ = mass of medium (kg)
- $C_p$ = specific heat capacity of medium (~4.18 kJ/kg·K for water-based media)
- $\Delta T = T_{final} - T_{initial}$ (~91 K, i.e., 30 °C → 121 °C)

**Steam required:**

$$m_{steam} = \frac{Q}{\lambda_{steam}}$$

Where $\lambda_{steam}$ = latent heat of steam (~2200 kJ/kg at atmospheric, lower at higher pressures).

### Numerical Example

For a 10,000 L fermenter ($m \approx 10,000 \, \text{kg}$):

$$Q = 10{,}000 \times 4.18 \times 91 = 3.80 \times 10^6 \, \text{kJ}$$

$$m_{steam} = \frac{3.80 \times 10^6}{2200} \approx 1727 \, \text{kg of steam per batch}$$

This shows why **continuous sterilization with heat recovery** is preferred for very large volumes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram: Sterilization Control Strategy

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant PLC as DCS Controller
    participant V1 as Steam Valve
    participant V4 as Cooling Valve
    participant V6 as Relief Valve
    participant T as Temperature Sensor
    participant P as Pressure Sensor

    Op->>PLC: START sterilization cycle
    PLC->>V1: OPEN steam valve
    PLC->>T: monitor T rise
    T-->>PLC: T = 30 C rising
    PLC->>PLC: heating phase active
    T-->>PLC: T = 121 C reached
    PLC->>V1: THROTTLE steam (maintain)
    PLC->>PLC: start holding timer
    PLC->>T: monitor T stable
    Note over PLC: hold time = 15 to 30 min
    PLC->>PLC: hold time elapsed
    PLC->>V1: CLOSE steam valve
    PLC->>V4: OPEN cooling valve
    PLC->>PLC: cooling phase active
    T-->>PLC: T = 37 C reached
    PLC->>V4: CLOSE cooling valve
    PLC->>Op: CYCLE COMPLETE - READY FOR INOCULATION
```

> [!TIP]
> **Pressure management:** Throughout the cycle, $V6$ (relief valve) protects against overpressure. The vessel is pressurized to maintain $T > 100°C$ — without pressure, water boils at 100 °C and you cannot reach 121 °C in liquid form.
