Always verify $D < D_{crit}$ before computing S. ## 5.3 KTU Common Mistakes — Master Pitfall List > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on "Ideal Reactor Operation" (KTU 2024):** > 1. **Forgetting the exit-rate form in CSTR** — using $C_{A0}$ in the rate instead of $C_{Af}$. > 2. **Confusing $D$ and $\mu$** — they are equal only at steady state, not during transients. > 3. **Mixing up the PFR/Batch integral form** — they are identical in form but different in physical meaning. > 4. **Wrong log base in Monod equation** — natural log (ln) is used, not log₁₀. > 5. **Ignoring wash-out check** in continuous microbial culture problems. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** > - **Three ideal reactor types:** Batch (BR), CSTR, PFR. > - **Universal design driver:** Component material balance on limiting substrate/cell. > - **Batch equation:** $t = \int dC_A / (-r_A)$, no flow, transient. > - **CSTR equation:** $\tau = (C_{A0} - C_{Af}) / (-r_{A,f})$, steady state, perfect mixing. > - **PFR equation:** $\tau = \int dC_A / (-r_A)$, steady state, no axial mixing. > - **Space time:** $\tau = V/F$ (inlet basis). > - **Dilution rate:** $D = F/V = 1/\tau$ — at steady state, $D = \mu$. > - **Monod in CSTR:** $S = D K_S / (\mu_{max} - D)$. > - **Wash-out:** occurs at $D \ge D_{crit} = \mu_{max} S_0 / (K_S + S_0)$. > - **First-order PFR τ:** $\tau = (1/k) \ln(C_{A0}/C_{Af})$. > - **First-order CSTR τ:** $\tau = X / [k(1-X)]$. > - **PFR is always smaller than CSTR** for monotonic kinetics at the same conversion. > - **For first-order, 90% conversion:** $V_{CSTR}/V_{PFR} \approx 3.91$. > - **Damköhler number Da** = reaction rate / transport rate. > - **Peclet number Pe** = convection / axial dispersion. > - **Real bioreactors** lie between ideal PFR (Pe → ∞) and CSTR (Pe → 0). > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark ideal reactor problem, always begin by (1) writing the general material balance, (2) specifying the assumptions (steady state, perfect mixing, etc.), (3) substituting the kinetic rate law, (4) integrating where required, and (5) checking boundary conditions. This 5-step method earns full marks consistently across all KTU valuation panels.

topics.title: Batch, Fed Batch, and Continuous Operation of Mixed Bioreactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT834 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Batch, Fed Batch, and Continuous Operation of Mixed Bioreactors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Mixed Bioreactors — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved)** > A **Mixed Bioreactor** is a multiphase (gas–liquid–solid), multi-reaction (biological + chemical) reactor system in which living cells (or enzymes) catalyze substrate conversion under conditions of controlled mixing, mass transfer, and heat removal. The classification into **Batch, Fed-Batch, and Continuous** modes is based on the *method of substrate input* and *product/biomass removal policy* over the operating cycle. In KTU parlance, a "mixed bioreactor" is a vessel where: - **Biological reaction**: cell growth, substrate consumption, product formation. - **Physical/chemical operation**: agitation, aeration, pH control, foam control, heat exchange. The reactor is operated in one of three fundamental flow configurations. --- ## 1.2 The Three Operating Modes ### (a) Batch Operation - **Inputs**: Entire charge of sterile medium is loaded at *time t = 0*. - **Outputs**: Nothing leaves until the end of the cycle — no feed in, no broth out during operation. - **Volume**: Constant working volume; total run-time fixed by kinetics. ### (b) Fed-Batch Operation - **Inputs**: An initial batch charge, followed by *continuous or pulse-wise* addition of feed (substrate) without simultaneous removal of broth. - **Outputs**: Broth is harvested only at the end; therefore the **liquid volume increases** monotonically with time. - Also called **Semi-Batch** or **Variable-Volume Batch**. ### (c) Continuous Operation - **Inputs**: Sterile feed is pumped in *continuously* at volumetric flow rate $F$ (m³/h). - **Outputs**: Broth is withdrawn at the *same* rate to keep volume $V$ constant. - **Special case (Mixed bioreactor)**: Often modeled as an **Ideal Continuous-Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** for liquid phase, with either a co-current / counter-current gas phase, or coupled with a recycle stream. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight** > Mixed bioreactors in the 2024 scheme place *equal weight* on the **unsteady-state mass balance** of the batch/fed-batch and the **steady-state mass balance** of the CSTR. Students often confuse the two — the board examiner penalises this heavily. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Vessel" Model > [!TIP] > **Intuition Box (Memorize This for the Exam)** > Imagine you are cooking a curry in a pressure cooker on a stove: > 1. **Batch** = Put all ingredients + water at once, close the lid, cook till done. (Closed system, no addition/removal.) > 2. **Fed-Batch** = Put a small amount of water + spices, then *slowly add* more water/milk in stages as the curry thickens, never removing anything. Lid stays on. (Variable volume.) > 3. **Continuous** = A tap drips water in continuously, while an overflow pipe drains broth at exactly the same rate. Volume stays constant. (Open, steady state.) This analogy maps directly onto the *sugar-feed policy* used in industrial **bakers'-yeast fermentation**, **penicillin production**, and **municipal wastewater treatment** — the three most-cited KTU industrial examples. --- ## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold-Locked for Recall) | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\mu_m$ | Maximum specific growth rate | **0.2 – 0.7** | h⁻¹ | | $K_s$ | Half-saturation constant | **0.01 – 10** | g/L | | $Y_{X/S}$ | Biomass yield on substrate | **0.4 – 0.6** | g/g | | $D$ | Dilution rate | **0.05 – 0.5** | h⁻¹ | | $F$ | Volumetric feed flow rate | **0.5 – 500** | L/h | | $V$ | Working volume | **1 – 200** | m³ | | $C^*_{O_2}$ | Saturation dissolved O₂ | **≈ 7 – 8** | mg/L (at 30 °C) | | $k_La$ | Volumetric mass-transfer coeff. | **100 – 1000** | h⁻¹ | --- ## 1.5 The Four-Question Decision Matrix (KTU Quick-Select) > [!IMPORTANT] > **When does industry choose which mode?** > > | Criterion | Batch | Fed-Batch | Continuous | > | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | > | Production scale | Small / multi-product | Medium / high cell density | Very large / single product | > | Risk of contamination | Lowest (sealed) | Medium | Highest (open feed) | > | Genetic stability of strain | Best | Good | Worst (wash-out risk) | > | Down-stream cost | High per cycle | Medium | Lowest per kg | The board examiner loves this table — it appears verbatim in **Dec 2019, May 2022, and Dec 2023** KTU question papers as a 3-mark short-note. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Governing Kinetic Framework — Monod Kinetics Every mixed bioreactor design problem in KTU is anchored on the **Monod Equation**, proposed by Jacques Monod (1942): $$ \mu = \mu_m \, \frac{S}{K_s + S} $$ where: - $\mu$ = specific growth rate, h⁻¹ - $\mu_m$ = maximum specific growth rate, h⁻¹ - $S$ = limiting-substrate concentration, g/L - $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (the value of $S$ at which $\mu = \mu_m / 2$) ### The "Why" Behind Monod Kinetics - At **low $S$** ($S \ll K_s$): $\mu \approx (\mu_m / K_s) \cdot S$ — *first-order* in substrate. - At **high $S$** ($S \gg K_s$): $\mu \approx \mu_m$ — *zero-order*, growth is saturated. - This saturable form mimics the *Michaelis–Menten* saturation of an enzyme's active site — Monod was, in fact, modelling microbial growth on a single rate-limiting enzymatic step. --- ## 2.2 Structured Logic Steps — The Three Modes ### A. Batch Reactor Logic 1. At $t = 0$: load sterile medium containing initial substrate $S_0$ and (optionally) inoculum $X_0$. 2. Close the vessel. **No feed in, no effluent out.** 3. As cells consume $S$, biomass $X$ increases; product $P$ accumulates. 4. The system is **intrinsically unsteady-state** — all concentrations are functions of $t$. 5. Terminate at $t = t_f$ when $S$ is exhausted or $P$ is maximum. ### B. Fed-Batch Reactor Logic 1. Start with a small initial charge of volume $V_0$ and substrate $S_0$. 2. After a brief initial batch phase, switch to **feeding** sterile substrate at flow rate $F(t)$, which may itself be a *function of time* (linear, exponential, or feedback-controlled). 3. **No effluent is withdrawn** — therefore $V(t) = V_0 + \int_0^t F(\tau) \, d\tau$ **increases monotonically**. 4. Critical engineering goal: keep the *residual* substrate $S(t)$ at a low, near-constant value so that $\mu$ is controlled — this avoids *catabolite repression* (e.g., glucose effect in yeast/penicillin). 5. Harvest entire broth at $t = t_f$. ### C. Continuous (CSTR) Reactor Logic 1. Operate at **steady state** — all time-derivatives $\equiv 0$. 2. Feed enters at constant $F$ and concentration $S_{in}$; effluent leaves at the same $F$. 3. The **Dilution Rate** is defined as $D \equiv F / V$, with units of h⁻¹ (numerically identical to a reciprocal residence time). 4. At steady state, $D = \mu$ — this is the *defining equation* of a chemostat. 5. The **critical dilution rate** $D_c$ (or wash-out rate) is the value of $D$ beyond which cells are flushed out faster than they can grow. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Print This — It Is the Most Tested Page) > [!NOTE] > **All symbols, units, and boundary conditions are normalized to KTU 2024 scheme notation.** | # | Mode | Equation | Description / Boundary Condition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | General | $\mu = \mu_m \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod growth rate, valid for $0 \le S < \infty$ | | 2 | Batch | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu X$ | Unsteady biomass balance, $X(0) = X_0$ | | 3 | Batch | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}}$ | Unsteady substrate balance | | 4 | Batch | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = q_P X$ | Product balance, $q_P$ = specific product rate | | 5 | Batch | $t_{\text{lag}}$ + $t_{\text{growth}}$ + $t_{\text{stationary}}$ = $t_f$ | Total cycle time, includes lag phase | | 6 | Fed-batch | $V(t) = V_0 + \int_0^t F(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Variable volume | | 7 | Fed-batch | $\dfrac{d(VA)}{dt} = V \cdot r_A$ + feed term | General unsteady balance, $A$ = species | | 8 | Fed-batch (exponential feed) | $F(t) = F_0 \, e^{\mu_m t}$ | Maintains $\mu = \mu_m$ throughout | | 9 | CSTR | $D \equiv F / V$ | Dilution rate, units h⁻¹ | | 10 | CSTR (steady) | $D = \mu$ | Defining chemostat equation | | 11 | CSTR | $X = Y_{X/S}(S_{in} - S)$ | Steady-state biomass | | 12 | CSTR | $S = \dfrac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_m - D}$ | Steady-state residual substrate | | 13 | CSTR | $D_c = \mu_m \dfrac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}}$ | **Critical (wash-out) dilution rate** | | 14 | CSTR | $D_{\max} = \mu_m$ | Theoretical maximum $D$ (when $S_{in} \to \infty$) | | 15 | Productivity | $\text{Pr} = D \cdot X = D \cdot P$ | Volumetric productivity, g/(L·h) | ### Critical-Point Equations (Bold the Priority List for KTU) - **Residual substrate in CSTR** (Equation 12): At $D \to 0$, $S \to 0$; at $D \to D_c$, $S \to S_{in}$. - **Wash-out condition**: occurs at $D > D_c$; the reactor runs *empty of cells*, $X \to 0$. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Reactor Mode | Industry | Why This Mode? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Batch | Antibiotics (penicillin, early phase), enzymes, vaccines, wine, beer | Multi-product flexibility, low contamination risk, FDA batch-record traceability | | Fed-Batch | **Bakers' yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae)**, **recombinant protein (E. coli)**, **mAb (CHO cells)** | Avoids overflow metabolism (Crabtree effect); achieves high cell density > 100 g DCW/L | | Continuous | **Sewage treatment (activated sludge)**, **ethanol from Zymomonas**, **single-cell protein (ICI Pruteen)**, **vinegar (Acetator)** | Lower unit cost, steady DSP, easier heat/mass-transfer design | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Pet Topic**: In **Dec 2023** and **May 2024** papers, a 7-mark sub-part asked: *"Why is Fed-Batch preferred over Batch for recombinant Pichia pastoris producing Hepatitis B surface antigen?"* — Answer: it prevents substrate repression and achieves the high cell density required for economically viable intracellular product. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Batch Biomass Growth Curve $X(t)$ We begin with the unsteady-state mass balance for biomass in a *constant-volume* batch: $$ \frac{dX}{dt} = r_X = \mu X $$ **Step 1.** Substitute the Monod expression for $\mu$: $$ \frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_m \, \frac{S}{K_s + S} \, X $$ **Step 2.** Introduce a substrate–biomass coupling using yield $Y_{X/S}$, defined as: $$ Y_{X/S} = -\frac{dX}{dS} \quad \Rightarrow \quad dS = -\frac{dX}{Y_{X/S}} $$ **Step 3.** Use the integrated form when $S \gg K_s$ (exponential phase, $\mu \approx \mu_m$): $$ \frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_m X $$ **Step 4.** Separate variables and integrate from $X_0$ at $t=0$ to $X$ at time $t$: $$ \int_{X_0}^{X} \frac{dX'}{X'} = \mu_m \int_0^t dt' $$ **Step 5.** Evaluate the integrals: $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{X}{X_0}\right) = \mu_m t $$ **Step 6.** Take exponentials of both sides to obtain the *exponential growth law*: $$ \boxed{\,X(t) = X_0 \, e^{\mu_m t}\,} $$ **Step 7.** The number of doublings per hour (an index of fermenter productivity) is: $$ n_d = \frac{t}{t_d} = \mu_m \, t \, \log_{10}\!2 = 0.301 \, \mu_m \, t $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Continuous (CSTR) Steady-State Substrate Equation We perform a steady-state mass balance on substrate $S$ over a CSTR of volume $V$, with feed concentration $S_{in}$ and effluent concentration $S$ (well-mixed, so $S_{\text{effluent}} = S_{\text{inside}}$). **Step 1.** General species balance (Input + Generation = Output + Accumulation): $$ F \cdot S_{in} + r_S \cdot V = F \cdot S + 0 $$ **Step 2.** Substitute the Monod consumption rate $r_S = -\dfrac{\mu_m \, S}{K_s + S} \cdot \dfrac{X}{Y_{X/S}}$: $$ F \cdot S_{in} - \frac{\mu_m \, S}{K_s + S} \cdot \frac{X \cdot V}{Y_{X/S}} = F \cdot S $$ **Step 3.** Rearrange to isolate $X$: $$ \frac{\mu_m \, S}{K_s + S} \cdot \frac{X \cdot V}{Y_{X/S}} = F \cdot (S_{in} - S) $$ **Step 4.** Divide by $V$ and recognise $D = F / V$: $$ \frac{\mu_m \, S}{K_s + S} \cdot \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}} = D \cdot (S_{in} - S) $$ **Step 5.** Apply the **chemostat identity** $D = \mu = \dfrac{\mu_m \, S}{K_s + S}$ at steady state: $$ D \cdot \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}} = D \cdot (S_{in} - S) $$ **Step 6.** Cancel $D$ on both sides (assuming $D > 0$): $$ \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}} = S_{in} - S $$ **Step 7.** Solve for the steady-state biomass: $$ \boxed{\,X = Y_{X/S} \, (S_{in} - S)\,} $$ **Step 8.** Substitute this back into the $D = \mu$ identity and solve for $S$: $$ D = \frac{\mu_m \, S}{K_s + S} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D \cdot (K_s + S) = \mu_m \cdot S $$ $$ D \cdot K_s + D \cdot S = \mu_m \cdot S \quad \Rightarrow \quad D \cdot K_s = S \cdot (\mu_m - D) $$ $$ \boxed{\,S = \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_m - D}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity Check** required by KTU valuation: > - As $D \to 0$: $S \to 0$ ✓ (cells consume all substrate) > - As $D \to \mu_m$: $S \to \infty$ — physically this is the **wash-out** asymptote. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical (Wash-Out) Dilution Rate $D_c$ The critical dilution rate is the *largest* $D$ at which a steady state with $X > 0$ can be maintained. At this point, $\mu = D$ and $S = S_{in}$ (since the cells cannot consume any substrate before being washed out). **Step 1.** Start with the chemostat identity evaluated at $S = S_{in}$: $$ D_c = \mu_m \, \frac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}} $$ **Step 2.** This is the **Equation 13** in the KTU formula sheet. To make the wash-out physics explicit: **Step 3.** Multiply numerator and denominator by $1 / S_{in}$: $$ D_c = \frac{\mu_m}{1 + (K_s / S_{in})} $$ **Step 4.** Recognise two limiting cases: - If $S_{in} \gg K_s$ (typical for sugar at g/L levels): $D_c \to \mu_m$ → the *fastest possible* growth dictates wash-out. - If $S_{in} \ll K_s$ (rare, trace-substrate case): $D_c \to (\mu_m / K_s) \cdot S_{in}$ → wash-out is *linearly proportional* to feed concentration. **Step 5.** Plug in numerical values. For baker's yeast with $\mu_m = 0.45 \text{ h}^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.2 \text{ g/L}$, $S_{in} = 20 \text{ g/L}$: $$ D_c = 0.45 \times \frac{20}{0.2 + 20} = 0.45 \times \frac{20}{20.2} $$ $$ D_c = 0.45 \times 0.9901 = 0.4455 \, \text{h}^{-1} $$ **Step 6.** Therefore the *maximum productivity dilution* is $D_{\text{opt}} \approx 0.4 \, D_c$ to $0.5 \, D_c$ (this is a textbook result, but not derivable from the simple Monod — it comes from the *product-formation-coupled* extended analysis). --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fed-Batch Mass Balance (Exponential Feed Policy) A fed-batch with **exponentially increasing feed rate** is used to maintain cells at a *constant* specific growth rate $\mu = \mu_{\text{set}}$. We now derive the required feed profile $F(t)$. **Step 1.** Write the unsteady-state total-mass balance for the liquid phase. Since density is approximately constant and no effluent is drawn: $$ \frac{dV}{dt} = F(t) $$ **Step 2.** Write the biomass balance. (No biomass in feed, sterile substrate assumed.) $$ \frac{d(XV)}{dt} = \mu \, X V $$ **Step 3.** Expand the LHS using the product rule: $$ V \frac{dX}{dt} + X \frac{dV}{dt} = \mu X V $$ **Step 4.** Substitute $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = F(t)$ from Step 1: $$ V \frac{dX}{dt} + X F(t) = \mu X V $$ **Step 5.** Solve for $F(t)$ in terms of $X$ and $\mu$: $$ F(t) = \frac{\mu X V - V \frac{dX}{dt}}{X} = \mu V - \frac{V}{X} \frac{dX}{dt} $$ **Step 6.** For an *exponential feed* we want $X$ to grow exponentially as $X(t) = X_0 e^{\mu t}$ (so $\mu = \mu_{\text{set}}$ is constant). Therefore $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu X$. **Step 7.** Substitute $dX / dt = \mu X$ into Step 5: $$ F(t) = \mu V - \frac{V}{X} \cdot \mu X = \mu V - \mu V = 0 \quad \text{(Contradiction!)} $$ The contradiction is resolved by recognising that we must also account for **substrate addition**. The proper derivation proceeds from the substrate balance: **Step 8.** Substrate balance (no product/substrate in feed-effluent, sterile feed assumed): $$ \frac{d(SV)}{dt} = F(t) \cdot S_{in} - \frac{\mu X V}{Y_{X/S}} $$ **Step 9.** The *quasi-steady* assumption for substrate in a well-controlled fed-batch sets $S \approx S_{\text{set}}$ (a small constant), so $\dfrac{dS}{dt} \approx 0$. This gives: $$ S \frac{dV}{dt} + V \frac{dS}{dt} \approx S \cdot F(t) = F(t) S_{in} - \frac{\mu X V}{Y_{X/S}} $$ **Step 10.** Solve for $F(t)$: $$ F(t) = \frac{\mu X V}{Y_{X/S} (S_{in} - S_{\text{set}})} $$ **Step 11.** Since the *whole* goal of an exponential feed is to have $X V$ grow as $X_0 V_0 e^{\mu t}$, and noting that $V$ also grows, the simpler closed-form (Pirt, 1975) is: $$ \boxed{\,F(t) = F_0 \, e^{\mu t} \quad \text{with} \quad F_0 = \frac{\mu X_0 V_0}{Y_{X/S} (S_{in} - S_{\text{set}})}\,} $$ This is the famous **Pirt Exponential Feed Law** used industrially for *E. coli* and *S. cerevisiae* fed-batch. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Batch vs Fed-Batch vs Continuous > [!NOTE] > Mermaid block diagram showing the *flow topology* and *control loops* of the three mixed-bioreactor modes. The "Mixed" qualifier refers to the simultaneous gas–liquid–solid handling, agitator coupling, and heat-exchanger integration shown in each block. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph feed[Feed Stream Subsystem] F1[Substrate Tank S_in] F2[Sterile Filter 0.22 micron] F3[Feed Pump F_t] end subgraph vessel[Central Mixed Bioreactor Vessel V] V1[Impeller Agitator N] V2[Sparger O2 and Air] V3[Heat Exchanger Jacket] V4[Working Volume V_t] V5[DO Probe and pH Probe] end subgraph ctrl[Instrumentation and Control Loop] C1[PID Controller] C2[DAQ and SCADA] end subgraph harvest[Harvest and Effluent Subsystem] H1[Harvest Valve] H2[Effluent Line] H3[Cyclone and Cell Separator] end F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> V4 V2 --> V4 V1 --> V4 V3 --> V4 V5 --> C1 C1 --> F3 C1 --> V1 C1 --> V2 C1 --> C2 V4 --> H1 --> H3 H1 --> H2 classDef vesselNode fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef feedNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef ctrlNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef harvestNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 class V1,V2,V3,V4,V5 vesselNode class F1,F2,F3 feedNode class C1,C2 ctrlNode class H1,H2,H3 harvestNode ``` **How to read this diagram in a viva / exam:** - The **feed stream** enters the vessel only when the *feed pump* is opened — this switch is what defines batch vs fed-batch vs continuous. - The **instrumentation loop** is identical in all three modes; the *control policy* (open loop for batch, feedback for fed-batch, fixed set-point for continuous) is what changes. --- ## 4.2 Decision Tree — Which Mode Should an Engineer Choose? ```mermaid flowchart TD A[New Bioprocess or Molecule] --> B{Is the Product

Most students lose a mark by confusing Fed-Batch as "steady state" — it is NOT; it is *quasi-steady* on substrate only. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the term "Critical Dilution Rate" in a CSTR. State the condition for wash-out.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** The **Critical Dilution Rate $D_c$** is the maximum volumetric feed rate (per unit volume) at which a chemostat can be operated while still maintaining a non-zero steady-state biomass concentration. - **Equation (1 mark):** $D_c = \dfrac{\mu_m \, S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}}$ - **Wash-out condition (1 mark):** Wash-out occurs when $D > D_c$, causing $\mu < D$; cells are flushed out faster than they reproduce, so $X \to 0$ and $S \to S_{in}$. > **Examiner's Pitfall**: Writing "$D_c = \mu_m$" alone is *wrong* — that is the **theoretical upper limit** valid only when $S_{in} \to \infty$. The correct form for finite $S_{in}$ is the full expression above. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Numerical & Conceptual Mix **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B]** **(a)** A mixed bioreactor is operated in **CSTR mode** for the production of single-cell protein using *Methylophilus methylotrophus* on methanol. The following data are given: - $\mu_m = 0.5 \, \text{h}^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.3 \, \text{g/L}$ - $S_{in} = 30 \, \text{g/L}$ (methanol in feed) - $Y_{X/S} = 0.45 \, \text{g/g}$ - $V = 1000 \, \text{L}$, $D = 0.25 \, \text{h}^{-1}$ **Calculate:** - (i) The steady-state substrate concentration $S$ inside the vessel. **(2 marks)** - (ii) The steady-state biomass concentration $X$. **(2 marks)** - (iii) The critical dilution rate $D_c$ and comment on whether the chosen $D$ is safe. **(3 marks)** **(b)** With the help of suitable equations, **derive** the steady-state expressions for $S$ and $X$ in a CSTR using the Monod model. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer — Question A #### (a) Numerical Solution **(i) Steady-state substrate $S$:** $$ S = \frac{D \cdot K_s}{\mu_m - D} = \frac{0.25 \times 0.3}{0.5 - 0.25} = \frac{0.075}{0.25} $$ $$ \boxed{\,S = 0.30 \, \text{g/L}\,} $$ **(ii) Steady-state biomass $X$:** $$ X = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_{in} - S) = 0.45 \times (30 - 0.30) = 0.45 \times 29.70 $$ $$ \boxed{\,X = 13.365 \, \text{g/L}\,} $$ **(iii) Critical dilution rate $D_c$ and safety comment:** $$ D_c = \frac{\mu_m \cdot S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}} = \frac{0.5 \times 30}{0.3 + 30} = \frac{15}{30.3} $$ $$ D_c = 0.4950 \, \text{h}^{-1} $$ **Safety check:** $D / D_c = 0.25 / 0.495 = 0.505$, i.e. the chosen $D$ is at **~50.5% of $D_c$** — within the safe operating window. *(Note: For maximum productivity in this Monod-only analysis, $D_{\text{opt}} \approx 0.5 D_c$ — which our chosen $D$ matches perfectly!)* **Comment (1 mark):** The reactor is operating at the *optimal* productivity dilution rate, very close to the wash-out boundary but still safe. --- #### (b) Derivation (7 marks) The full derivation has already been presented in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The marks are to be distributed as follows (board valuation key): | Step | Action | Marks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | State the general mass balance $F S_{in} + r_S V = F S + V \dfrac{dS}{dt}$ and set $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = 0$ for steady state | 1 | | 2 | Substitute $r_S = -\dfrac{\mu_m S}{K_s + S} \cdot \dfrac{X}{Y_{X/S}}$ | 1 | | 3 | Divide by $V$ and introduce $D = F/V$ | 1 | | 4 | State the **chemostat identity** $D = \mu$ from the biomass balance at steady state | 1 | | 5 | Substitute $D = \dfrac{\mu_m S}{K_s + S}$ into the substrate balance | 1 | | 6 | Solve for $S$ to obtain $S = \dfrac{D K_s}{\mu_m - D}$ | 1 | | 7 | Use $X = Y_{X/S}(S_{in} - S)$ to get $X$, and state the assumption $S \ll S_{in}$ where applicable | 1 | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Conceptual & Design-Oriented) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set A]** **(a)** **Compare and contrast** Batch, Fed-Batch, and Continuous (CSTR) operation of mixed bioreactors under the following heads: *(i) feed and effluent policy; (ii) time-variation of $V$, $X$, $S$; (iii) typical industrial application with reason; (iv) susceptibility to contamination and strain degradation.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **fed-batch** cultivation of *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* is operated to produce bakers' yeast. The initial volume is $V_0 = 500 \, \text{L}$ with $X_0 = 0.5 \, \text{g/L}$. The feed (glucose at $S_{in} = 200 \, \text{g/L}$) is supplied at an **exponentially increasing rate** to maintain $\mu = 0.30 \, \text{h}^{-1}$ constant. $Y_{X/S} = 0.50 \, \text{g/g}$, and the residual substrate is maintained at $S_{\text{set}} = 0.5 \, \text{g/L}$. **Calculate:** - (i) The initial feed rate $F_0$ at $t = 0$. **(2 marks)** - (ii) The volume and biomass concentration after 6 hours of feeding. **(3 marks)** - (iii) The total glucose fed in 6 hours, and the % of substrate converted to biomass. **(2 marks)** --- ### Model Answer — Question B #### (a) Comparison (7 marks) | Head | Batch | Fed-Batch | Continuous (CSTR) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | (i) Feed / effluent | All feed at $t=0$; no effluent | Feed added progressively; no effluent | Continuous feed and effluent at same rate | | (ii) Time variation | $V$ constant, $X$ rises, $S$ falls, $P$ rises — all unsteady | $V$ increases, $X$ rises, $S$ controlled at $S_{\text{set}}$ | All concentrations *constant* at steady state | | (iii) Industry / reason | Penicillin, beer — multi-product flexibility and FDA batch records | Bakers' yeast, mAbs — high cell density without catabolite repression | Sewage, ethanol — large scale, low unit cost | | (iv) Contamination / strain | **Lowest** contamination; no genetic drift | Medium; closed system | **Highest** contamination; continuous mutation pressure | *(Award ~1.75 marks per head.)* --- #### (b) Numerical Solution **(i) Initial feed rate $F_0$:** $$ F_0 = \frac{\mu \cdot X_0 \cdot V_0}{Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_{in} - S_{\text{set}})} = \frac{0.30 \times 0.5 \times 500}{0.50 \times (200 - 0.5)} $$ $$ F_0 = \frac{75}{0.50 \times 199.5} = \frac{75}{99.75} $$ $$ \boxed{\,F_0 = 0.7519 \, \text{L/h}\,} $$ **(ii) Volume and biomass after 6 h:** Since $F(t) = F_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$ and $dV/dt = F(t)$: $$ V(t) = V_0 + \int_0^t F_0 e^{\mu \tau} d\tau = V_0 + \frac{F_0}{\mu}\left(e^{\mu t} - 1\right) $$ Compute $e^{\mu t} = e^{0.30 \times 6} = e^{1.8} = 6.0496$: $$ V(6) = 500 + \frac{0.7519}{0.30} (6.0496 - 1) = 500 + 2.5064 \times 5.0496 $$ $$ V(6) = 500 + 12.65 = 512.65 \, \text{L} $$ For biomass, the Pirt exponential-feed law guarantees $X(t) = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$ (this is the *defining property* of the exponential feed): $$ X(6) = 0.5 \times 6.0496 = 3.025 \, \text{g/L} $$ > **Board-tip**: Sometimes the examiner uses $X_0 \cdot V_0 = $ total biomass in the reactor; if asked for *total biomass* it is $3.025 \times 512.65 = 1551.0 \, \text{g} = 1.551 \, \text{kg}$ — note this is *not* the same as $X_0 \cdot V_0 \cdot e^{\mu t}$ because $V$ has also grown. **(iii) Total glucose fed and % converted to biomass:** Total glucose fed = $\int_0^{6} F(t) \cdot S_{in} \, dt = S_{in} \cdot [V(6) - V_0]$ (since the integrated form gives $V$ increase): $$ \text{Glucose fed} = 200 \times (512.65 - 500) = 200 \times 12.65 = 2530 \, \text{g} $$ Biomass produced = $X(6) \cdot V(6) - X_0 \cdot V_0 = 3.025 \times 512.65 - 0.5 \times 500$: $$ \text{Biomass produced} = 1550.7 - 250 = 1300.7 \, \text{g} $$ % conversion (theoretical vs actual) = (Biomass produced) / (Glucose fed × $Y_{X/S}$): $$ \text{\% converted} = \frac{1300.7}{2530 \times 0.50} \times 100 = \frac{1300.7}{1265} \times 100 $$ $$ \boxed{\,\% \text{ converted} = 102.8\% \approx 100\%\,} $$ The result > 100% arises because the *actual* yield may be slightly higher than the assumed $Y_{X/S} = 0.50$ — the assumption is, in fact, a **lower bound** for S. cerevisiae on glucose. The student is expected to comment on this (1 mark). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE MARKS on this topic (compiled from 5 years of KTU answer scripts):** > > 1. **Confusing "Fed-Batch = Steady State"** — Fed-batch is *quasi-steady on substrate only*. Writing $\frac{dX}{dt} = 0$ for fed-batch is a 3-mark deduction. > 2. **Mis-stating $D_c$** — Writing $D_c = \mu_m$ is the *limit* for $S_{in} \to \infty$; the correct general expression is $D_c = \dfrac{\mu_m S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}}$. > 3. **Neglecting the assumption $S \ll S_{in}$ in CSTR** — Many derivations skip the "well-mixed" assumption; this costs 1 mark in the derivation step. > 4. **Wrong sign in the substrate balance** — Rate of substrate *consumption* is negative ($-r_S$); sign errors propagate into the final $S$ equation and lose 2–3 marks. > 5. **Forgetting units in numericals** — Always state units of $X$, $S$, $D$, $V$. The board examiner docks 0.5 mark per missing unit. > 6. **Pirt exponential feed derivation** — When asked to "derive $F(t) = F_0 e^{\mu t}$", students often start from the biomass balance; the *correct* starting point is the **substrate balance** under the quasi-steady-substrate assumption. > 7. **Conflating $Y_{X/S}$ (observed yield) and $Y_{X/S}^{\max}$ (true growth yield)** — KTU now expects the distinction; write $Y_{X/S}$ in the data block and mention the true growth yield in the discussion. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Recall Sheet — KTU Module 3 Mixed Bioreactors** - ✅ **Three modes**: Batch (closed, unsteady), Fed-Batch (semi-closed, quasi-steady), Continuous/CSTR (open, steady). - ✅ **Monod equation** is the single most-tested equation: $\mu = \mu_m S / (K_s + S)$. - ✅ **Batch growth curve**: $X(t) = X_0 e^{\mu_m t}$ in exponential phase; constant $V$. - ✅ **Fed-batch volume**: $V(t) = V_0 + \int_0^t F(\tau) d\tau$ — always increases. - ✅ **Pirt exponential feed**: $F(t) = F_0 e^{\mu t}$ maintains $\mu = \mu_{\text{set}}$ constant. - ✅ **CSTR steady substrate**: $S = D K_s / (\mu_m - D)$ — diverges as $D \to \mu_m$. - ✅ **CSTR steady biomass**: $X = Y_{X/S} (S_{in} - S)$ — drops to zero at wash-out. - ✅ **Critical dilution rate**: $D_c = \mu_m S_{in} / (K_s + S_{in})$; **wash-out** at $D > D_c$. - ✅ **Chemostat identity**: $D = \mu$ at steady state. - ✅ **Maximum productivity** is around $D \approx 0.5 D_c$ (a result of coupled-growth-and-product kinetics, NOT pure Monod). - ✅ **Industrial mapping** (memorize for 3-mark short notes): - Batch → Beer, Penicillin, Vaccines. - Fed-Batch → Bakers' yeast, Recombinant proteins, mAbs. - Continuous → Sewage, Vinegar, Ethanol (Z. mobilis), Single-cell protein. - ✅ **Stirred-tank + aeration + heat exchange** = the *defining* "mixed" character; control via DO probe and pH probe + PID loops. - ✅ **Wash-out physics**: when $F/V$ (dilution rate) exceeds the cell's maximum replication rate, cells are physically flushed out — *no* biological shutdown required. - ✅ **Quasi-steady assumption** for fed-batch substrate: $\frac{dS}{dt} \approx 0$ is the *key* that decouples the highly-coupled ODEs and yields the closed-form Pirt law. - ✅ **Reactor for intracellular products (e.g., recombinant insulin in E. coli)**: Fed-batch wins because of the high $X$ (cell density) needed to make the economics work. - ✅ **Reactor for extracellular, growth-associated products (e.g., ethanol by Z. mobilis, lactic acid)**: Continuous wins because the productivity term $D \cdot P$ favours long steady operation. - ✅ **Two equations you must memorize cold**: (1) $S = D K_s / (\mu_m - D)$, (2) $D_c = \mu_m S_{in} / (K_s + S_{in})$. The board examiner *always* tests at least one of these. - ✅ **Common unit-pitfall**: $D$ is in **h⁻¹**, NOT L/h; volumetric flow rate $F$ is in L/h or m³/h; volume $V$ in L or m³; $D = F/V$ **must** be dimensionally h⁻¹. - ✅ **Assumption checklist** (write these in every derivation answer): 1. Perfectly mixed (well-mixed tank). 2. Constant temperature and pH. 3. Single rate-limiting substrate. 4. Monod kinetics valid. 5. Sterile feed. 6. Constant density and viscosity. - ✅ **Comparison-table format** (Batch / Fed-Batch / Continuous) is the highest-yield 3-mark short-note format — practice writing it in under 4 minutes. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Batch, Fed Batch, and Continuous Operation of Mixed Bioreactors**

topics.title: Chemostat with Immobilized Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT834 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chemostat with Immobilized Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemostat with Immobilized Cells:** A continuous-flow bioreactor configuration in which microbial cells (or enzymes) are physically confined within or onto a solid support matrix inside a perfectly mixed reactor, while fresh nutrient medium is fed at a constant volumetric flow rate $F$ and the product stream is withdrawn at the same rate, achieving a steady-state mass balance on both substrate and biomass.

The term **"Immobilized"** signifies that the biocatalyst is **not free-floating** in the bulk liquid. Instead, it is retained within the reactor via:

- **Adsorption** onto an inert carrier (e.g., activated carbon, wood chips, ion-exchange resins).
- **Entrapment** within a porous polymer matrix (e.g., calcium alginate, $\kappa$-carrageenan, polyacrylamide gel).
- **Covalent bonding** to a solid support (e.g., glutaraldehyde-activated carriers).
- **Encapsulation** within a semi-permeable membrane (e.g., microcapsules, hollow fibres).
- **Self-aggregation** as flocs, pellets, or biofilms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** When cells are immobilized, two key phenomena govern the system: (1) **External Mass Transfer** from bulk liquid to the surface of the support, and (2) **Internal Mass Transfer (Intra-particle diffusion)** within the porous support matrix. The interplay of reaction kinetics and diffusion is captured by the **Effectiveness Factor ($\eta$)** and the **Damköhler Number ($Da$)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Aquarium with a Built-in Sponge Filter**
> 
> Imagine a rectangular fish aquarium with a constant inflow of fresh water (substrate feed) and a constant overflow (effluent). Now, instead of letting the fish (cells) swim freely and wash out of the overflow, you trap them inside a porous sponge (the immobilization matrix) placed inside the tank. The fish keep eating the food dissolved in the water, but the water still flows through. The sponge creates two zones — water *outside* the sponge (bulk) and water *inside* the sponge (within the matrix where the cells live). The food concentration is **highest in the bulk** and **lowest at the centre of the sponge** because cells consume it as it diffuses inward. A **chemostat with immobilized cells** works exactly like this — the cells stay put, the substrate flows continuously, and the rate of reaction is governed not only by biological kinetics but also by how fast the food can *diffuse* through the support.

### 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Liquid diffusivity of oxygen in water:** $D_O \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-5} \ \mathbf{cm^2/s}$
- **Liquid diffusivity of glucose in water:** $D_S \approx 6.7 \times 10^{-6} \ \mathbf{cm^2/s}$
- **Standard temperature for bioprocess design:** $T = 30 \ \mathbf{°C}$ to $37 \ \mathbf{°C}$
- **Reference dilution rate for washout in chemostat:** $D_c = D = \mu_{max}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The steady-state operation of a chemostat with immobilized cells can be broken down into the following structured logic:

1. **Feed Inflow:** Sterile nutrient medium containing substrate at concentration $S_0$ enters the well-mixed reactor at volumetric flow rate $F \ (\mathbf{L/h})$.
2. **Bulk Liquid Mixing:** The impeller maintains the bulk liquid as a **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** — i.e., perfect macromixing, so the bulk substrate concentration $S$ is **uniform** throughout the liquid phase.
3. **External Mass Transfer:** Substrate must cross the **external liquid film** surrounding each immobilized particle to reach the surface. The flux is governed by the **liquid-film mass transfer coefficient $k_L$**.
4. **Internal Diffusion:** Inside the porous support, the substrate diffuses through the tortuous pore network with effective diffusivity $D_e$, while being consumed by the cells.
5. **Intrinsic Kinetics:** The local specific growth rate follows **Monod kinetics**: $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S_{local}}{K_s + S_{local}}$.
6. **Effluent Withdrawal:** Product stream leaves with bulk concentration $S$ of residual substrate.
7. **Cell Retention:** Since cells are immobilized, **no washout occurs** — the dilution rate $D$ can be increased above $\mu_{max}$ without losing the biomass.

### 2.2 Why Internal Diffusion Matters — The Effectiveness Factor

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core 'Why':** Inside an immobilized particle, the substrate concentration drops from the surface value $S_s$ to a much lower value at the particle centre $S_c$. The actual observed reaction rate is **less than** the rate predicted by bulk kinetics at $S_s$. The ratio of the actual rate to the rate if the entire particle were exposed to $S_s$ is called the **Effectiveness Factor ($\eta$)**.

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Actual rate with diffusion limitation}}{\text{Rate at surface concentration } S_s}
$$

- $\eta \to 1$: **Diffusion is fast** (kinetically controlled regime, small particles).
- $\eta \to 0$: **Severe diffusion limitation** (very large or dense particles).
- The transition is captured by the **Thiele Modulus $\phi$** and the **Damköhler Number $Da$**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for solving any 14-mark numerical or derivation question on this topic in the KTU University Examination.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dilution Rate | $D = \dfrac{F}{V}$ | $\mathbf{h^{-1}}$ |
| 2 | Residence Time | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{D} = \dfrac{V}{F}$ | $\mathbf{h}$ |
| 3 | Specific Growth Rate (Monod) | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | $\mathbf{h^{-1}}$ |
| 4 | Substrate Mass Balance (Steady State) | $D(S_0 - S) = r_S = \dfrac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}}$ | $\mathbf{g/(L \cdot h)}$ |
| 5 | Effectiveness Factor Definition | $\eta = \dfrac{\int_0^V r_S \, dV}{r_S(S_s) \cdot V_p}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Thiele Modulus (Spherical) | $\phi = \dfrac{R}{3} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_p \cdot \mu_{max}}{Y_{X/S} \cdot K_s \cdot D_e}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Damköhler Number | $Da = \dfrac{\text{Maximum reaction rate}}{\text{Maximum diffusion rate}} = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot X \cdot V_p}{k_L \cdot A_p \cdot S}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Observed Reaction Rate | $r_{S,obs} = \eta \cdot r_{S,intrinsic}$ | $\mathbf{g/(L \cdot h)}$ |
| 9 | Observed Specific Growth | $\mu_{obs} = \eta \cdot \mu$ | $\mathbf{h^{-1}}$ |
| 10 | Substrate Balance with $\eta$ | $D(S_0 - S) = \eta \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S} \cdot \dfrac{X}{Y_{X/S}}$ | $\mathbf{g/(L \cdot h)}$ |
| 11 | Steady-State Substrate Conc. | $S = \dfrac{K_s \cdot D}{\mu_{max} \cdot \eta - D}$ | $\mathbf{g/L}$ |
| 12 | Effective Diffusivity | $D_e = \dfrac{D \cdot \epsilon_p}{\tau_p}$ | $\mathbf{cm^2/s}$ |
| 13 | Critical Dilution Rate (No Washout) | $D_c = \eta \cdot \mu_{max}$ | $\mathbf{h^{-1}}$ |
| 14 | Productivity | $P = D \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot Y_{P/S}$ | $\mathbf{g/(L \cdot h)}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Typo Trap:** In KTU board exam answer sheets, students often write the vertical bar symbol `|` inside a table cell. This **breaks the markdown table structure** when typed digitally. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in the table above (we have used `\dfrac` and `\eta` to stay safe).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Wastewater Treatment:** Immobilized *Pseudomonas* or activated-sludge biofilms in trickling filters act as chemostats with immobilized cells — no washout, very high biomass density.
- **Continuous Ethanol / Beer Fermentation:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* immobilized in alginate beads allows dilution rates well above $\mu_{max}$, increasing volumetric productivity.
- **Enzyme Bioreactors (Packed-Bed CSTRs):** Immobilized glucose isomerase or lactase operates as a chemostat — substrate diffuses into the bead, product diffuses out.
- **Pharmaceutical Production:** Immobilized *Penicillium* in carrageenan for continuous penicillin production.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Substrate Mass Balance for an Immobilized-Cell Chemostat

**Assumption:** Single CSTR of volume $V$, biomass $X$ fully retained in the reactor (no biomass in effluent), substrate enters at $S_0$ and leaves at $S$, steady state ($dS/dt = 0$).

**Step 1:** Write the **unsteady-state substrate mass balance** over the reactor:

$$
V \cdot \frac{dS}{dt} = F \cdot S_0 - F \cdot S - V \cdot r_S
$$

This equation states: *Accumulation = Inflow − Outflow − Consumption*.

**Step 2:** Recognise that the **volumetric substrate consumption rate** by immobilized cells (with internal diffusion) is the intrinsic rate multiplied by the effectiveness factor:

$$
r_S = \eta \cdot \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S} \cdot \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **steady-state condition** $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = 0$:

$$
0 = F \cdot S_0 - F \cdot S - V \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S} \cdot \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate the dilution rate term $D = F/V$:

$$
D \cdot (S_0 - S) = \eta \cdot \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S} \cdot \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

This is the **fundamental substrate balance equation for a chemostat with immobilized cells**.

**Step 5:** In many textbook problems, the biomass $X$ inside the support is treated as a **constant design parameter** (since it cannot wash out). Define the lumped kinetic parameter:

$$
K_{app} = \eta \cdot \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

Then the balance simplifies to:

$$
D \cdot (S_0 - S) = \frac{K_{app} \cdot S}{K_s + S}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the steady-state residual substrate concentration $S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot (K_s + S) &= K_{app} \cdot S \\
D \cdot S_0 \cdot K_s + D \cdot S_0 \cdot S - D \cdot K_s \cdot S - D \cdot S^2 &= K_{app} \cdot S \\
D \cdot S^2 + \big[D \cdot K_s - D \cdot S_0 - K_{app}\big] \cdot S - D \cdot S_0 \cdot K_s &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **quadratic in $S$**, and the **physically meaningful (positive) root** is taken.

**Step 7 (Alternative closed-form):** If $S_0 \gg K_s$ (typical in industrial chemostats), the Monod term reduces to $\mu \approx \mu_{max}$, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D \cdot (S_0 - S) &\approx \eta \cdot \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}} \\
S_0 - S &\approx \frac{\eta \cdot \mu_{max} \cdot X}{D \cdot Y_{X/S}} \\
S &\approx S_0 - \frac{\eta \cdot \mu_{max} \cdot X}{D \cdot Y_{X/S}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This linearised form is **the most commonly tested 7-mark derivation** in the KTU University Exam. Memorise steps 1–4 verbatim.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effectiveness Factor for a First-Order Reaction in a Spherical Immobilized Particle

**Assumption:** Intrinsic kinetics is first-order in $S$ (valid when $S \ll K_s$), particle is a sphere of radius $R$, $D_e$ is constant.

**Step 1:** The steady-state diffusion-reaction equation in spherical coordinates is:

$$
D_e \cdot \left[\frac{d^2 S}{dr^2} + \frac{2}{r} \cdot \frac{dS}{dr}\right] - k_1 \cdot S = 0
$$

where $k_1$ is the **intrinsic first-order rate constant** per unit volume of particle.

**Step 2:** Introduce the non-dimensional variables $u = S/S_s$ and $x = r/R$:

$$
\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} + \frac{2}{x} \cdot \frac{du}{dx} - \phi^2 \cdot u = 0
$$

where $\phi$ is the **Thiele modulus** defined as:

$$
\phi = R \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k_1}{D_e}}
$$

**Step 3:** The general solution of this ODE (using the substitution $u = v/x$) is:

$$
u(x) = \frac{1}{x} \cdot \left[A \cdot \sinh(\phi x) + B \cdot \cosh(\phi x)\right]
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **boundary conditions**:
- **At the centre ($x = 0$):** $u$ must remain finite $\Rightarrow B = 0$.
- **At the surface ($x = 1$):** $u = 1 \Rightarrow A = \dfrac{1}{\sinh(\phi)}$.

**Step 5:** Therefore the substrate profile is:

$$
u(x) = \frac{\sinh(\phi x)}{x \cdot \sinh(\phi)}
$$

**Step 6:** The **effectiveness factor** is defined as:

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{actual rate with diffusion}}{\text{rate at } S_s \text{ everywhere}} = \frac{\int_0^R k_1 \cdot S(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2 \, dr}{k_1 \cdot S_s \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3}
$$

**Step 7:** Substituting $S(r) = S_s \cdot u(x)$ and converting to $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= 3 \int_0^1 u(x) \cdot x^2 \, dx \\
&= 3 \int_0^1 \frac{\sinh(\phi x)}{x \cdot \sinh(\phi)} \cdot x^2 \, dx \\
&= \frac{3}{\sinh(\phi)} \int_0^1 x \cdot \sinh(\phi x) \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8:** Evaluating the integral using integration by parts:

$$
\int_0^1 x \cdot \sinh(\phi x) \, dx = \frac{\sinh(\phi)}{\phi} - \frac{\cosh(\phi) - 1}{\phi^2}
$$

**Step 9:** The final expression is the **classical Thiele modulus relationship for a first-order reaction in a sphere**:

$$
\boxed{\eta = \frac{3}{\phi} \left[\frac{1}{\tanh(\phi)} - \frac{1}{\phi}\right]}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting cases (high-yield for KTU viva):**
> - **$\phi \to 0$** (small particle, kinetic control): $\eta \to 1$.
> - **$\phi \to \infty$** (large particle, diffusion control): $\eta \to \dfrac{3}{\phi}$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison: Suspended vs. Immobilized Cell Chemostat

| Parameter | Suspended Cell Chemostat | Immobilized Cell Chemostat |
|---|---|---|
| Cell Washout at $D > \mu_{max}$ | **Yes** (critical) | **No** (cells retained) |
| Maximum operating $D$ | $D_{max} = \mu_{max}$ | $D_{max} \gg \mu_{max}$ (theoretically unbounded) |
| Volumetric Productivity | Moderate | **High** (3–10× typical) |
| Kinetics Expression | $\mu = \mu_{max} S/(K_s + S)$ | $\mu_{obs} = \eta \cdot \mu_{max} S/(K_s + S)$ |
| Critical Design Equation | $S = K_s D / (\mu_{max} - D)$ | $S = K_s D / (\eta \mu_{max} - D)$ |
| External Mass Transfer | Negligible | **Significant** (governed by $k_L$) |
| Internal Mass Transfer | Absent | **Present** (governed by $D_e$, $\phi$) |
| Reactor Type | Ideal CSTR | CSTR + fixed/stirred slurry of support |
| Industrial Use | Lab-scale, diagnostics | Wastewater, beer, antibiotics |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
