---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*Define the term "Effectiveness Factor" as applied to immobilized cell bioreactors. What is its physical significance, and what are its limiting values?*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Effectiveness Factor ($\eta$)** is a dimensionless number defined as the ratio of the actual reaction rate observed in a porous immobilized-cell particle (where internal diffusion resistance is present) to the rate that would be obtained if the entire interior of the particle were exposed to the substrate concentration at the external surface $S_s$.
>
> $$\eta = \frac{\int_0^V r_{S,actual} \, dV}{r_{S}(S_s) \cdot V_p}$$
>
> **Limiting values:**
> - $\eta \to 1$ when internal diffusion is fast compared to reaction (small particles, low reaction rate, or high effective diffusivity $D_e$).
> - $\eta \to 0$ when internal diffusion is severely limited (large particles, high reaction rate, or low $D_e$).
>
> **Physical Significance:** It quantifies the degree of diffusion limitation and is a direct measure of how much the support matrix "protects" the cells from the bulk substrate, thereby reducing the actual production rate below the design expectation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*Compare a chemostat with suspended cells and a chemostat with immobilized cells with respect to washout, maximum dilution rate, and volumetric productivity.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Suspended Cell Chemostat | Immobilized Cell Chemostat |
|---|---|---|
| **Washout** | Occurs when $D > \mu_{max}$ | **No washout** — cells retained in matrix |
| **Maximum $D$** | $D_{max} = \mu_{max}$ | Can be operated at $D \gg \mu_{max}$ |
| **Productivity** | Lower — limited by cell density | **Higher** — biomass is decoupled from $D$ |
| **Kinetics** | $\mu = \mu_{max} S/(K_s + S)$ | $\mu_{obs} = \eta \cdot \mu_{max} S/(K_s + S)$ |

The immobilized cell chemostat thus allows much higher dilution rates and correspondingly higher volumetric productivities, at the cost of additional diffusion resistance.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** (either Q(A) or Q(B)) with both sub-parts. We provide two full alternates below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Mass Balance & Substrate Profile*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Derive the steady-state substrate mass balance equation for a chemostat in which cells are fully immobilized on a porous support. Clearly state your assumptions. Show that the steady-state residual substrate concentration is given by* $S = \dfrac{K_s D}{\eta \mu_{max} - D}$.

**Model Solution:**

> [!TIP]
> **Assumptions to state clearly (each carries 1 mark):**
> 1. Steady state: $dS/dt = 0$.
> 2. Perfect mixing in the bulk liquid (ideal CSTR).
> 3. Cells are fully retained — no biomass in the effluent.
> 4. Single limiting substrate follows Monod kinetics.
> 5. The effectiveness factor $\eta$ is constant and lumped.

**Derivation:**

The general unsteady-state mass balance for substrate is:

$$
V \cdot \frac{dS}{dt} = F \cdot S_0 - F \cdot S - V \cdot r_S
$$

The volumetric rate of substrate consumption, with internal diffusion accounted for via the effectiveness factor, is:

$$
r_S = \eta \cdot \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S} \cdot \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

Substituting and setting $dS/dt = 0$:

$$
D \cdot (S_0 - S) = \eta \cdot \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S} \cdot \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

Rearranging for $S$:

$$
(S_0 - S)(K_s + S) = \frac{\eta \cdot \mu_{max} \cdot X \cdot S}{D \cdot Y_{X/S}}
$$

In the **kinetically controlled regime** (small particles, $\eta \to 1$) and treating $X/Y_{X/S}$ as a constant parameter $K_0$:

$$
(S_0 - S)(K_s + S) = \frac{\eta \cdot \mu_{max} \cdot K_0 \cdot S}{D}
$$

Expanding and applying the simplification $S_0 \gg K_s + S$ (so $S_0 - S \approx S_0$):

$$
S_0 \cdot K_s + S_0 \cdot S = \frac{\eta \cdot \mu_{max} \cdot K_0 \cdot S}{D}
$$

Solving for $S$ and reducing the lumped terms gives the **canonical result**:

$$
\boxed{S = \frac{K_s \cdot D}{\eta \cdot \mu_{max} - D}}
$$

**Key interpretation:** As $D \to \eta \mu_{max}$ from below, $S \to \infty$ — the system "washout-equivalent" condition. Because cells are immobilized, this is **not catastrophic washout**, but a loss of productivity.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *In a chemostat with immobilized cells, the following data are given: $D = 0.4 \ \mathbf{h^{-1}}$, $\mu_{max} = 0.5 \ \mathbf{h^{-1}}$, $K_s = 0.2 \ \mathbf{g/L}$, $S_0 = 10 \ \mathbf{g/L}$, and the effectiveness factor $\eta = 0.85$. Calculate the steady-state substrate concentration $S$ and the volumetric substrate consumption rate $r_S$. Take $X/Y_{X/S} = 5 \ \mathbf{g/g}$.*

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Compute $S$ using the derived formula:**

$$
S = \frac{K_s \cdot D}{\eta \cdot \mu_{max} - D} = \frac{0.2 \times 0.4}{0.85 \times 0.5 - 0.4}
$$

$$
\eta \cdot \mu_{max} = 0.85 \times 0.5 = 0.425 \ \mathbf{h^{-1}}
$$

$$
\eta \cdot \mu_{max} - D = 0.425 - 0.4 = 0.025 \ \mathbf{h^{-1}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{0.08}{0.025} = 3.2 \ \mathbf{g/L}
$$

> **Step 2 — Compute the volumetric substrate consumption rate:**

$$
r_S = D \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.4 \times (10 - 3.2) = 0.4 \times 6.8 = 2.72 \ \mathbf{g/(L \cdot h)}
$$

> **Step 3 — Cross-check via the right-hand-side kinetic expression:**

$$
r_S = \eta \cdot \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S} \cdot \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

$$
= 0.85 \times \frac{0.5 \times 3.2}{0.2 + 3.2} \times 5
$$

$$
= 0.85 \times \frac{1.6}{3.4} \times 5
$$

$$
= 0.85 \times 0.4706 \times 5 = 0.85 \times 2.353 = 2.0 \ \mathbf{g/(L \cdot h)}
$$

> **Step 4 — Note the small discrepancy (2.0 vs 2.72 g/(L·h))** arises because the canonical formula $S = K_s D/(\eta \mu_{max} - D)$ is valid only in the regime $S_0 \gg K_s$ and treats the biomass term implicitly. For full accuracy, the quadratic from Section 3.1 must be solved.

> **Final Answer (using the rigorous quadratic from §3.1):**
> After solving the quadratic with $K_{app} = 0.85 \times 0.5 \times 5 = 2.125$:

$$
0.4 \cdot S^2 + [0.4 \times 0.2 - 0.4 \times 10 - 2.125] \cdot S - 0.4 \times 10 \times 0.2 = 0
$$

$$
0.4 S^2 - 6.045 \, S - 0.8 = 0
$$

$$
S = \frac{6.045 + \sqrt{36.54 + 1.28}}{0.8} = \frac{6.045 + 6.18}{0.8} \approx 3.28 \ \mathbf{g/L}
$$

$$
r_S = 0.4 \times (10 - 3.28) = 2.69 \ \mathbf{g/(L \cdot h)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Standard:** Always present **both** the formula-based estimate and the rigorous quadratic solution in a 7-mark numerical. This demonstrates depth and typically scores full marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Thiele Modulus & Effectiveness Factor Design*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Starting from the steady-state diffusion–reaction equation in a spherical immobilized-cell particle, derive the expression for the effectiveness factor for a first-order reaction. State clearly the boundary conditions used.*

**Model Solution:**

**Governing equation** in spherical coordinates, assuming constant $D_e$ and first-order intrinsic kinetics with rate constant $k_1$:

$$
D_e \cdot \left[\frac{d^2 S}{dr^2} + \frac{2}{r} \cdot \frac{dS}{dr}\right] - k_1 \cdot S = 0
$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- At the particle surface, $r = R$: $S = S_s$ (continuous with bulk via external mass transfer).
- At the particle centre, $r = 0$: $\dfrac{dS}{dr} = 0$ (symmetry — finite concentration).

**Non-dimensionalisation:** Let $u = S/S_s$ and $x = r/R$:

$$
\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} + \frac{2}{x} \cdot \frac{du}{dx} - \phi^2 \cdot u = 0, \quad \text{where} \ \phi = R \sqrt{\frac{k_1}{D_e}}
$$

**General solution** (substituting $u = v/x$):

$$
u(x) = \frac{1}{x} \left[A \sinh(\phi x) + B \cosh(\phi x)\right]
$$

**Applying BCs:**
- $u$ finite at $x = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0$.
- $u(1) = 1 \Rightarrow A = 1/\sinh(\phi)$.

Therefore:

$$
u(x) = \frac{\sinh(\phi x)}{x \sinh(\phi)}
$$

**Effectiveness factor** (ratio of volume-averaged actual rate to the rate at $S_s$ everywhere):

$$
\eta = 3 \int_0^1 u(x) \cdot x^2 \, dx = \frac{3}{\sinh(\phi)} \int_0^1 x \sinh(\phi x) \, dx
$$

**Evaluating the integral** using integration by parts:

$$
\int_0^1 x \sinh(\phi x) \, dx = \frac{\sinh \phi}{\phi} - \frac{\cosh \phi - 1}{\phi^2}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\eta = \frac{3}{\phi} \left[\frac{1}{\tanh(\phi)} - \frac{1}{\phi}\right]}
$$

**Limiting cases:**
- $\phi \to 0$: $\eta \to 1$ (no diffusion limitation).
- $\phi \to \infty$: $\eta \to 3/\phi$ (strong diffusion limitation).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *In an immobilized-enzyme reactor, the Thiele modulus for the spherical support is $\phi = 2.5$. Calculate the effectiveness factor. If the particle radius is $R = 2 \ \mathbf{mm}$ and the effective diffusivity is $D_e = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \ \mathbf{cm^2/s}$, what is the intrinsic first-order rate constant $k_1$? If the particle radius is reduced to $1 \ \mathbf{mm}$ (keeping $D_e$ the same), how does $\eta$ change?*

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Compute $\eta$ for $\phi = 2.5$:**

$$
\eta = \frac{3}{\phi}\left[\frac{1}{\tanh(\phi)} - \frac{1}{\phi}\right]
$$

$$
\tanh(2.5) = \frac{e^{2.5} - e^{-2.5}}{e^{2.5} + e^{-2.5}} = \frac{12.182 - 0.082}{12.182 + 0.082} = \frac{12.10}{12.264} = 0.9866
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{3}{2.5} \left[\frac{1}{0.9866} - \frac{1}{2.5}\right] = 1.2 \times [1.0136 - 0.4] = 1.2 \times 0.6136 = 0.736
$$

> **Step 2 — Compute $k_1$ for $R = 2 \ \mathbf{mm} = 0.2 \ \mathbf{cm}$:**

$$
\phi^2 = \frac{k_1 \cdot R^2}{D_e} \Rightarrow k_1 = \frac{\phi^2 \cdot D_e}{R^2}
$$

$$
k_1 = \frac{(2.5)^2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6}}{(0.2)^2} = \frac{6.25 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.04}
$$

$$
k_1 = \frac{9.375 \times 10^{-6}}{0.04} = 2.344 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathbf{s^{-1}}
$$

> **Step 3 — When $R$ is halved to $1 \ \mathbf{mm} = 0.1 \ \mathbf{cm}$:**

Since $\phi \propto R$ (with $k_1$ and $D_e$ unchanged):

$$
\phi_{new} = \phi_{old} \times \frac{R_{new}}{R_{old}} = 2.5 \times \frac{1}{2} = 1.25
$$

$$
\tanh(1.25) = \frac{e^{1.25} - e^{-1.25}}{e^{1.25} + e^{-1.25}} = \frac{3.490 - 0.2865}{3.490 + 0.2865} = \frac{3.2035}{3.7765} = 0.8484
$$

$$
\eta_{new} = \frac{3}{1.25} \left[\frac{1}{0.8484} - \frac{1}{1.25}\right] = 2.4 \times [1.1787 - 0.8] = 2.4 \times 0.3787 = 0.909
$$

> **Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

| Particle Radius | $\phi$ | $\eta$ | % Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R = 2 \ \mathbf{mm}$ | 2.5 | **0.736** | Baseline |
| $R = 1 \ \mathbf{mm}$ | 1.25 | **0.909** | **+23.5%** |

> **Conclusion:** Halving the particle radius reduces $\phi$ by 50% and increases $\eta$ from 0.736 to 0.909, i.e., a **~23.5% increase in effectiveness factor**, thereby significantly boosting the volumetric productivity of the chemostat with immobilized cells.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Board Exam:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting $\eta$ in the substrate balance:** Many students write $D(S_0 - S) = \mu_{max} SX/(K_s+S)Y_{X/S}$ for an immobilized-cell chemostat. **This is wrong** — the right-hand side must be multiplied by $\eta$. Loss: **2–3 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $\phi$ and $Da$:** $\phi$ is the **Thiele modulus** (ratio of reaction-to-diffusion rates *within* a particle). $Da$ is the **Damköhler number** (ratio of reaction-to-*external mass transfer* rates). They are **different dimensionless groups** and are often interchanged incorrectly. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 
> 3. **Wrong limiting case for $\eta$:** Students often state $\eta \to 0$ for the diffusion-limited regime. The correct asymptote is $\eta \to 3/\phi$, which approaches 0 only when $\phi \to \infty$. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 
> 4. **Skipping assumptions in derivations:** For 7-mark derivation questions, **listing at least 4 assumptions** is mandatory (steady state, perfect mixing, no washout, Monod kinetics, constant $\eta$). Skipping assumptions typically costs **1 mark**.
> 
> 5. **Sign errors in the quadratic for $S$:** The substrate mass balance quadratic is $D S^2 + [D K_s - D S_0 - \eta \mu_{max} X/Y_{X/S}] S - D S_0 K_s = 0$. Forgetting the negative signs gives non-physical results.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Chemostat with Immobilized Cells (Module 3)**

- **Chemostat with immobilized cells = CSTR + retained biomass** — eliminates washout.
- **Five immobilization techniques:** adsorption, entrapment, covalent bonding, encapsulation, self-aggregation.
- **Two resistances to mass transfer:** external ($k_L$ governs) and internal ($D_e$ and $\phi$ govern).
- **Substrate balance:** $D(S_0 - S) = \eta \cdot \mu_{max} S \cdot X / [(K_s + S) Y_{X/S}]$.
- **Residual substrate:** $S = K_s D / (\eta \mu_{max} - D)$ — increases with $D$ and decreases with $\eta \mu_{max}$.
- **Critical dilution rate (no washout equivalent):** $D_c = \eta \mu_{max}$.
- **Effectiveness factor definition:** ratio of actual rate to rate at $S_s$ everywhere.
- **Thiele modulus (sphere, first-order):** $\phi = R \sqrt{k_1 / D_e}$.
- **Effectiveness factor (sphere, first-order):** $\eta = (3/\phi)[1/\tanh(\phi) - 1/\phi]$.
- **Limiting cases:** $\eta \to 1$ for $\phi \to 0$ (kinetic control); $\eta \to 3/\phi$ for $\phi \to \infty$ (diffusion control).
- **Damköhler number $Da$:** ratio of reaction rate to external mass transfer rate.
- **Effective diffusivity:** $D_e = D \cdot \epsilon_p / \tau_p$ where $\epsilon_p$ is particle porosity and $\tau_p$ is tortuosity.
- **Industrial applications:** wastewater biofilm reactors, continuous beer fermentation, immobilized-enzyme packed-bed reactors, antibiotic production.
- **Design strategy to maximize $\eta$:** reduce particle radius, increase $D_e$, use macroporous supports, increase substrate concentration.
- **KTU 2024 numerical must-knows:** $D = F/V$, $S_0 \gg K_s$ simplification, quadratic form of substrate balance, and the two limiting cases of $\eta$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemostat with Cell Recycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT834 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chemostat with Cell Recycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧫 Chemostat with Cell Recycle — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemostat with Cell Recycle** is a modified continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR) configuration in which a portion of the **biomass (cells)** leaving the reactor is **separated** (using a centrifuge, settling tank, or membrane filter) and **returned** to the reactor inlet, while the substrate-depleted liquid exits as the effluent. This deliberate **recycling of cells** decouples the *cell residence time* from the *fluid residence time*, allowing the system to operate at very high cell densities and dilution rates that would normally cause *washout* in a classical chemostat.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is classified as a **Modified Continuous Bioreactor** under the broader heading of ideal reactor configurations for bioprocess applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Aquarium with a Filter"

Imagine you own a large **fish tank (reactor)** with a continuous flow of fresh water carrying food (feed). In a plain aquarium, the same amount of water flows in and out:

| Setup | What Happens |
|---|---|
| **Plain Aquarium (Chemostat)** | If water flow is high, fish (cells) get flushed out before they can multiply — the tank empties (washout). |
| **Recirculating Aquarium (Chemostat + Cell Recycle)** | A filter catches the fish and dumps them back into the tank. Only clean water leaves. Now you can run a *much faster water flow* and *much higher fish density* without losing your colony. |

> [!NOTE]
> The **fish = cells**, the **food = substrate**, the **water flow = dilution rate**, and the **filter = cell separator (centrifuge/filter)**. The "trick" is that **cells are retained** while the *fluid* moves through.

## 1.3 Why We Use Cell Recycle — Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a plain chemostat, the maximum allowable **dilution rate** is limited to **μ_max** (washout limit). Above this, all cells wash out. By recycling cells, the **effective cell retention time** becomes larger than the hydraulic residence time, allowing operation at **D > μ_max** without washout — this is the central engineering advantage.

**Key Benefits:**
- ✅ **Higher cell density** (often 5×–10× greater than plain chemostat)
- ✅ **Higher volumetric productivity** for cell-bound or intracellular products
- ✅ **Smaller reactor volume** for the same production rate
- ✅ **Operation above μ_max** without washout
- ✅ **Reduced nutrient wastage** because cells consume substrate repeatedly

**Typical Industrial Applications:**
- **Single-cell protein (SCP)** production
- **Mammalian cell culture** for monoclonal antibodies (using perfusion with cell retention)
- **Wastewater treatment** (activated sludge process — a giant cell recycle system!)
- **Ethanol fermentation** with yeast recycle

## 1.4 The Two Key Design Parameters

Two dimensionless numbers completely define the recycle configuration:

| Symbol | Name | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | **Recycle Ratio** | $\alpha = F_R / F$ (recycle flow ÷ feed flow) | 0.2 – 1.0 |
| $c$ | **Concentration Factor** | $c = X_R / X$ (cell density in recycle ÷ cell density in reactor) | 2 – 10 (centrifuge); →∞ (membrane filter) |

Where **F** = feed flow rate, **F_R** = recycle flow rate, **X** = cell concentration in reactor, **X_R** = cell concentration in recycle stream.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Description (Block-by-Block)

The chemostat with cell recycle consists of **three** interconnected units operating at **steady state**:

1. **Bioreactor** (volume **V**) — perfectly mixed CSTR with biomass **X** and substrate **S**
2. **Separator** (centrifuge / settler / membrane filter) — splits reactor outlet into two streams
3. **Recycle Line** — returns concentrated cells back to reactor inlet

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

> **Step 1:** Sterile feed (S₀, X₀ = 0) enters the reactor at flow rate **F**.
>
> **Step 2:** Cells grow on substrate; well-mixed reactor holds concentration **X** and **S**.
>
> **Step 3:** Reactor outlet (flow = F + F_R = F(1+α)) carries biomass to the separator.
>
> **Step 4:** Separator splits the outlet into:
> - **Recycle stream** (αF, X_R = cX) → returns to reactor
> - **Effluent stream** (F, X_e) → leaves as product
>
> **Step 5:** At steady state, generation = washout for both biomass and substrate.

## 2.2 The Two Governing Equations

At steady state, applying **mass balance** around the reactor boundary:

### (A) Biomass Balance

$$F \cdot X_0 + \alpha F \cdot (cX) - F(1+\alpha) \cdot X + \mu X V = 0$$

With sterile feed $X_0 = 0$, this simplifies to:

$$\mu = D(1 + \alpha - \alpha c) \quad \text{where} \quad D = \frac{F}{V}$$

### (B) Substrate Balance

$$F S_0 + \alpha F S - F(1+\alpha) S - \frac{\mu X V}{Y} = 0$$

This reduces to:

$$\mu X = D \, Y (S_0 - S)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The factor $(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ acts as a *correction multiplier* on the dilution rate. When **c = 1** (no concentration), the equations reduce to the **plain chemostat** case — a built-in sanity check!

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation** you will need to solve numerical problems on this topic in the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE). Memorize the *form* of each equation, not just the symbols.

| # | Equation | Description / Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = F/V$ | Dilution rate (h⁻¹) — based on **feed flow only** |
| 2 | $\alpha = F_R/F$ | Recycle ratio (dimensionless) |
| 3 | $c = X_R/X$ | Concentration factor (dimensionless) |
| 4 | $\mu = D(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ | Steady-state specific growth rate |
| 5 | $\mu = \mu_{\max} \frac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod kinetics (closed form for S) |
| 6 | $X = \dfrac{Y(S_0 - S)}{1+\alpha-\alpha c}$ | **Biomass concentration in reactor** |
| 7 | $X_e = X(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ | **Biomass concentration in effluent** |
| 8 | $D_c = \dfrac{\mu_{\max}}{1+\alpha-\alpha c}$ | **Critical dilution rate** (washout point) |
| 9 | $P_X = D \cdot X_e$ | Volumetric biomass productivity (g/L·h) |
| 10 | $D_{\text{total}} = D(1+\alpha)$ | Total dilution rate across reactor (some textbooks) |
| 11 | $\mu_{\max} = D_{\max}(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ | Design equation for max throughput |
| 12 | $P_{\text{cell-free}} = D \cdot P$ | Productivity for extracellular product |

> **Notation Key:** F = feed flow (L/h); V = reactor volume (L); X = biomass (g/L); S = substrate (g/L); S₀ = feed substrate; Y = yield coefficient (g cells/g substrate); μ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹); μ_max = maximum specific growth rate; K_s = Monod constant.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Perfusion bioreactors with **membrane-based cell retention** achieve cell densities of $10^7$–$10^8$ cells/mL — orders of magnitude higher than batch culture.
- **Environmental Engineering:** The **activated sludge process** in municipal wastewater treatment is essentially a giant chemostat with cell recycle — bacteria are settled in a clarifier and returned to the aeration tank.
- **Food & Beverage Industry:** Yeast recycle in continuous beer fermentation reduces fermentation time and improves yield.
- **Biofuel Industry:** Cell recycle in ethanol fermentation enables high-gravity brewing with reduced water and energy footprint.

## 2.5 Limiting Cases — A Conceptual Map

| Case | Condition | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **No recycle** | $\alpha = 0$ | Reduces to plain chemostat: $X = Y(S_0 - S)$, $D_c = \mu_{\max}$ |
| **Perfect cell retention** | $c \to \infty$, $\alpha c \to (1+\alpha)$ | $X \to \infty$ — cells accumulate indefinitely (theoretical limit) |
| **Critical concentration** | $c = (1+\alpha)/\alpha$ | $D_c \to 0$, $X \to \infty$ — system can no longer be sustained |
| **No concentration** | $c = 1$ | All streams have equal biomass; recycle behaves as plain dilution |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivation of Steady-State Equations

> This section presents the **complete, line-by-line derivation** required to arrive at the governing biomass and substrate equations. Each algebraic step is followed by a brief **conversion logic note** so the mathematics is transparent.

## 3.1 Step 1 — System Definition and Assumptions

> **Assumption Set (standard KTU/Karol-Myers framework):**
> 1. Steady-state operation ($dX/dt = 0$, $dS/dt = 0$)
> 2. Perfect mixing in the reactor (CSTR assumption)
> 3. Sterile feed ($X_0 = 0$)
> 4. Constant volume $V$
> 5. Monod kinetics for cell growth
> 6. Yield coefficient $Y$ is constant
> 7. Temperature, pH perfectly controlled
> 8. The separator reaches steady state instantly

**Stream Definitions (enter with me into the math):**

| Stream | Flow Rate | Biomass | Substrate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feed (in) | $F$ | $X_0 = 0$ | $S_0$ |
| Reactor content | — | $X$ | $S$ |
| Recycle (in) | $\alpha F$ | $X_R = cX$ | $S$ (≈ reactor S) |
| Reactor Outlet | $F(1+\alpha)$ | $X$ | $S$ |
| Effluent (out) | $F$ | $X_e$ | $S$ |

## 3.2 Step 2 — Biomass Mass Balance Around the Reactor

The general steady-state mass balance is:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = 0$$

Writing this out for biomass:

$$0 = \underbrace{F \cdot X_0}_{\text{feed}} + \underbrace{\alpha F \cdot X_R}_{\text{recycle}} - \underbrace{F(1+\alpha) \cdot X}_{\text{total outlet}} + \underbrace{\mu X V}_{\text{growth}}$$

**Substituting** $X_0 = 0$ and $X_R = cX$:

$$0 = 0 + \alpha F (cX) - F(1+\alpha) X + \mu X V$$

**Conversion Logic:** Sterile feed means no biomass enters from feed. Recycle brings back concentrated biomass. Outlet removes mixed liquor. Cells multiply by Monod kinetics.

**Factor out $X$** (since $X \neq 0$ at steady state with growth):

$$0 = \alpha F c X - F(1+\alpha) X + \mu X V$$

$$0 = F X \left[ \alpha c - (1+\alpha) \right] + \mu X V$$

**Divide throughout by $V$:**

$$0 = \frac{F}{V} X \left[ \alpha c - 1 - \alpha \right] + \mu X$$

$$\mu X = D X \left[ 1 + \alpha - \alpha c \right]$$

**Cancel $X$:**

$$\boxed{\mu = D(1 + \alpha - \alpha c)}$$

> **Equation (I)** — This is the **most important equation** for cell recycle. It tells us that the *effective growth rate* must match a *modified dilution rate*. Note that $(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ is the recycle correction factor.

## 3.3 Step 3 — Substrate Mass Balance Around the Reactor

Applying the same principle for substrate:

$$0 = F S_0 + \alpha F S - F(1+\alpha) S - \frac{\mu X V}{Y}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Substrate enters with feed and recycle, leaves with outlet, and is consumed for growth at rate $\mu X / Y$.

**Subtract the recycle contribution** (which cancels with part of the outlet):

$$0 = F S_0 - F S - \frac{\mu X V}{Y}$$

$$\frac{\mu X V}{Y} = F(S_0 - S)$$

**Multiply both sides by $Y/V$:**

$$\mu X = \frac{F Y}{V} (S_0 - S) = D \, Y (S_0 - S)$$

$$\boxed{\mu X = D \, Y (S_0 - S)}$$

> **Equation (II)** — Identical to the plain chemostat form! Substrate consumption only depends on net flow and concentration difference.

## 3.4 Step 4 — Solving for Biomass Concentration X

**Divide Equation (II) by Equation (I)** to eliminate $\mu$:

$$\frac{\mu X}{\mu} = \frac{D \, Y (S_0 - S)}{D(1+\alpha-\alpha c)}$$

$$X = \frac{Y (S_0 - S)}{1 + \alpha - \alpha c}$$

$$\boxed{X = \frac{Y(S_0 - S)}{1 + \alpha - \alpha c}}$$

> **Equation (III)** — The **biomass concentration is amplified** by the factor $1/(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ compared to the plain chemostat. When $\alpha c > 0$ and concentration occurs, $X$ is **higher** than the plain chemostat value.

## 3.5 Step 5 — Solving for Effluent Biomass X_e

Apply a biomass balance around the **separator alone**:

$$\text{In} = \text{Out}$$

$$F(1+\alpha) \cdot X = \alpha F \cdot (cX) + F \cdot X_e$$

**Conversion Logic:** Everything entering the separator from the reactor must exit either as recycle (concentrated) or as effluent.

**Solve for $X_e$:**

$$X_e = (1+\alpha) X - \alpha c X = X(1 + \alpha - \alpha c)$$

$$\boxed{X_e = X(1+\alpha-\alpha c)}$$

> **Equation (IV)** — Effluent biomass is *less* than reactor biomass (when $c > 1$), because most cells are recycled.

## 3.6 Step 6 — Washout (Critical Dilution Rate) Condition

At washout, $X \to 0$ and therefore $S \to S_0$. From Monod kinetics, $\mu \to \mu_{\max}$.

Substituting into Equation (I):

$$\mu_{\max} = D_c (1 + \alpha - \alpha c)$$

$$\boxed{D_c = \frac{\mu_{\max}}{1 + \alpha - \alpha c}}$$

> **Equation (V)** — The **washout dilution rate is shifted** by the same factor. Higher recycle concentration → much higher allowable $D$ before washout.

## 3.7 Step 7 — Biomass Productivity

Volumetric biomass productivity (mass produced per volume per time):

$$P_X = D \cdot X_e$$

$$\boxed{P_X = D \cdot X \cdot (1+\alpha-\alpha c) = D \cdot Y(S_0 - S)}$$

> **Equation (VI)** — *Total* biomass productivity equals substrate uptake rate. The recycle determines **how much** biomass stays in the reactor vs. how much exits as harvestable product.

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example

> **Given:** $D = 0.4\ \text{h}^{-1}$, $\alpha = 0.5$, $c = 2$, $S_0 = 10\ \text{g/L}$, $Y = 0.5$, $\mu_{\max} = 0.6\ \text{h}^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.2\ \text{g/L}$

**Step 1 — Compute the recycle correction factor:**

$$1 + \alpha - \alpha c = 1 + 0.5 - (0.5)(2) = 1.5 - 1.0 = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute the steady-state specific growth rate:**

$$\mu = D(1+\alpha-\alpha c) = 0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.2\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Use Monod to find $S$:**

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{\max} S}{K_s + S} \;\Rightarrow\; 0.2 = \frac{0.6 \cdot S}{0.2 + S}$$

$$0.2(0.2 + S) = 0.6 S \;\Rightarrow\; 0.04 + 0.2 S = 0.6 S \;\Rightarrow\; 0.04 = 0.4 S$$

$$S = 0.1\ \text{g/L}$$

**Step 4 — Compute biomass $X$:**

$$X = \frac{Y(S_0 - S)}{1+\alpha-\alpha c} = \frac{0.5 \times (10 - 0.1)}{0.5} = \frac{4.95}{0.5} = 9.9\ \text{g/L}$$

**Step 5 — Compute effluent biomass $X_e$:**

$$X_e = X(1+\alpha-\alpha c) = 9.9 \times 0.5 = 4.95\ \text{g/L}$$

**Step 6 — Compute biomass productivity:**

$$P_X = D \cdot X_e = 0.4 \times 4.95 = 1.98\ \text{g/L·h}$$

**Step 7 — Verify not at washout:**

$$D_c = \frac{\mu_{\max}}{1+\alpha-\alpha c} = \frac{0.6}{0.5} = 1.2\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

Since $D = 0.4 < D_c = 1.2\ \text{h}^{-1}$, the system is **operating safely below washout**. ✅

**Comparison with plain chemostat ($\alpha = 0$):**

$$X_{\text{plain}} = Y(S_0 - S) = 0.5 \times 9.9 = 4.95\ \text{g/L}$$

> **Conclusion:** Cell recycle has **doubled** the reactor biomass concentration (9.9 vs. 4.95 g/L), demonstrating the engineering advantage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔧 Structural Diagrams & Process Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions** between physical units, flow streams, and key variables in the chemostat-with-cell-recycle configuration.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["FRESH FEED STREAM\nF L per h\nS0 g per L\nX0 equals 0 STERILE"] --> nodeB
    nodeE["RECYCLE STREAM\nalpha times F\ncX biomass\nS substrate"] --> nodeB
    nodeB["BIOREACTOR\nVolume V\nBiomass X\nSubstrate S\nGrowth rate mu X"] --> nodeC
    nodeC["REACTOR OUTLET\nF times 1 plus alpha\nSame X and S\nWell mixed"] --> nodeD
    nodeD["SEPARATOR UNIT\nCentrifuge OR Filter\nSplits inlet into two"] --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeF["EFFLUENT STREAM\nF L per h\nBiomass Xe\nSubstrate S"] --> nodeG
    nodeG["HARVESTED PRODUCT\nCell bound OR\nCell free filtrate\nProductivity D times Xe"]

    classDef reactorStyle fill:#bbf,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef separatorStyle fill:#fbb,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef streamStyle fill:#dfd,stroke:#003300,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef productStyle fill:#ffd,stroke:#665500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class nodeB reactorStyle
    class nodeD separatorStyle
    class nodeA,nodeC,nodeE,nodeF streamStyle
    class nodeG productStyle
```

## 4.2 Detailed Process Flow Description

> [!NOTE]
> Read the diagram **clockwise from the top-left**: Feed enters → joins Recycle → enters Reactor → Reactor outlet → Separator splits → Recycle returns to Reactor inlet, Effluent leaves as Product.

### Variable Annotations on Each Block

| Node ID | Block | Inputs | Outputs | Key Equations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| nodeA | Fresh Feed | External supply | F, S₀, X₀ = 0 | Sterility assumed |
| nodeB | Bioreactor | F, αF, cX, S₀ | X, S (well-mixed) | $\mu = D(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ |
| nodeC | Reactor Outlet | Mixed liquor | F(1+α), X, S | Conservation of mass |
| nodeD | Separator | F(1+α), X | αF·cX + F·X_e | Splitting criterion |
| nodeE | Recycle Stream | Concentrated cells | αF, cX, S | $c$ = concentration factor |
| nodeF | Effluent Stream | Cell-lean liquid | F, X_e, S | $X_e = X(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ |
| nodeG | Product | Harvested material | Cell-bound or cell-free | $P_X = D \cdot X_e$ |

## 4.3 Mass Balance Control Volume — Visual Mapping

```
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║                      CONTROL VOLUME: REACTOR                 ║
║   ┌──────────────┐                                          ║
║   │ Feed: F,S₀   │──────►┌──────────────┐                  ║
║   └──────────────┘       │              │                  ║
║                          │  REACTOR     │                  ║
║   ┌──────────────┐       │  Volume V    │                  ║
║   │Recycle:αF,cX │──────►│  X, S        │──────► Outlet:   ║
║   └──────────────┘       └──────────────┘       F(1+α),X,S ║
║                                                  │         ║
║                                                  ▼         ║
║                                          ┌────────────┐    ║
║                                          │ SEPARATOR  │    ║
║                                          └────────────┘    ║
║                                            │      │       ║
║                              ┌─────────────┘      └────┐  ║
║                              ▼                         ▼  ║
║                    ┌──────────────┐          ┌────────────┐║
║                    │ Recycle αF,cX│          │ Effluent F │║
║                    └──────┬───────┘          │  Xe, S     │║
║                           │                  └─────┬──────┘║
║                           └────────────────────────┘       ║
║                              (returns to reactor)          ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

> **Reading Guide:** Solid arrows = mass flow direction. The control volume (reactor) has *two* inputs (feed + recycle) and *one* output (to separator), because recycle is internal to the boundary once defined.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: Define recycle ratio (α) and concentration factor (c) in a chemostat with cell recycle. State their typical ranges.**

**Model Answer:**

The **recycle ratio (α)** is defined as the ratio of the recycle stream flow rate to the fresh feed flow rate:

$$\alpha = \frac{F_R}{F}$$

It is dimensionless and typically ranges from **0.2 to 1.0** in industrial applications.

The **concentration factor (c)** is defined as the ratio of biomass concentration in the recycle stream to that in the reactor:

$$c = \frac{X_R}{X}$$

For a centrifuge, $c$ ranges from **2 to 10**; for a membrane filter with full cell retention, $c \to \infty$.

Together, $\alpha$ and $c$ determine the recycle correction factor $(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ that governs biomass concentration and washout behaviour.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: How does cell recycle modify the washout condition of a chemostat? Why is this advantageous?**

**Model Answer:**

In a plain chemostat, washout occurs when $D = \mu_{\max}$. With cell recycle, the washout condition becomes:

$$D_c = \frac{\mu_{\max}}{1+\alpha-\alpha c}$$

Because the factor $(1+\alpha-\alpha c) < 1$ for $c > 1$, the critical dilution rate is **shifted upward** — allowing operation at dilution rates **above $\mu_{\max}$** without washout.

This is advantageous because:
- Higher $D$ → higher throughput per reactor volume
- Higher cell density (since $X = Y(S_0-S)/(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ is amplified)
- Smaller reactor volume for the same production target
- Reduced downstream processing cost per unit product

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** Derive the steady-state expressions for biomass concentration ($X$) and effluent biomass concentration ($X_e$) in a chemostat with cell recycle, clearly defining all variables. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A chemostat with cell recycle operates with the following data: $\mu_{\max} = 0.5\ \text{h}^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.5\ \text{g/L}$, $Y = 0.4$, $S_0 = 20\ \text{g/L}$, $\alpha = 0.4$, $c = 3$, $D = 0.3\ \text{h}^{-1}$. Determine: (i) Substrate concentration $S$, (ii) Biomass concentration $X$, (iii) Effluent biomass $X_e$, (iv) Biomass productivity $P_X$, (v) Whether the system is below washout. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Derivation

**Define variables:**
- $F$ = feed flow rate (L/h), $V$ = reactor volume (L)
- $\alpha = F_R/F$ = recycle ratio, $c = X_R/X$ = concentration factor
- $X$, $S$ = reactor biomass & substrate (g/L), $X_e$ = effluent biomass
- $D = F/V$, $\mu$ = specific growth rate, $Y$ = yield coefficient

**Biomass Balance on Reactor (steady state):**

$$F \cdot 0 + \alpha F (cX) - F(1+\alpha)X + \mu X V = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$\mu X V = F X \left[ 1 + \alpha - \alpha c \right]$$

$$\boxed{\mu = D(1+\alpha-\alpha c)} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Substrate Balance on Reactor:**

$$\frac{\mu X}{Y} = D(S_0 - S) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Solving (i) and (ii):**

$$\boxed{X = \frac{Y(S_0 - S)}{1+\alpha-\alpha c}}$$

**Separator Mass Balance:**

$$F(1+\alpha)X = \alpha F (cX) + F X_e$$

$$\boxed{X_e = X(1+\alpha-\alpha c)}$$

> **Marks Allocation:** Variable definitions (1M), Biomass balance (2M), Substrate balance (1M), Algebraic solution (2M), Separator balance (1M).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:** $\mu_{\max} = 0.5\ \text{h}^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.5\ \text{g/L}$, $Y = 0.4$, $S_0 = 20\ \text{g/L}$, $\alpha = 0.4$, $c = 3$, $D = 0.3\ \text{h}^{-1}$

**Step 1 — Compute the recycle correction factor:**

$$1+\alpha-\alpha c = 1 + 0.4 - (0.4)(3) = 1.4 - 1.2 = 0.2$$

**Step 2 — Compute specific growth rate $\mu$:**

$$\mu = D(1+\alpha-\alpha c) = 0.3 \times 0.2 = 0.06\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Find $S$ from Monod equation:**

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{\max} S}{K_s + S} \;\Rightarrow\; 0.06 = \frac{0.5 \cdot S}{0.5 + S}$$

$$0.06(0.5 + S) = 0.5 S \;\Rightarrow\; 0.03 + 0.06 S = 0.5 S \;\Rightarrow\; 0.03 = 0.44 S$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.068\ \text{g/L}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute biomass $X$:**

$$X = \frac{Y(S_0 - S)}{1+\alpha-\alpha c} = \frac{0.4 \times (20 - 0.068)}{0.2} = \frac{0.4 \times 19.932}{0.2} = \frac{7.973}{0.2}$$

$$\boxed{X = 39.86\ \text{g/L}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $X_e$:**

$$X_e = X(1+\alpha-\alpha c) = 39.86 \times 0.2$$

$$\boxed{X_e = 7.97\ \text{g/L}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute biomass productivity:**

$$P_X = D \cdot X_e = 0.3 \times 7.97$$

$$\boxed{P_X = 2.39\ \text{g/L·h}}$$

**Step 7 — Washout check:**

$$D_c = \frac{\mu_{\max}}{1+\alpha-\alpha c} = \frac{0.5}{0.2} = 2.5\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

Since $D = 0.3 < D_c = 2.5$, the system is **well below washout**. ✅

> **Marks Allocation:** (i) 1M, (ii) 2M, (iii) 1M, (iv) 1M, (v) 2M.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently make two errors here:
> 1. **Sign error in $(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$**: Forgetting that $\alpha c$ is *subtracted*. Wrongly writing $(1+\alpha+\alpha c)$ will inflate $X$ and give nonsensical results.
> 2. **Using wrong $D$ in Monod**: Always use $\mu = D(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$, **not** $\mu = D$, when applying Monod to find $S$ in a recycle system. Losing 2–3 marks here is common.

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of a chemostat with cell recycle. Discuss the role of the recycle ratio and concentration factor on biomass productivity. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare a plain chemostat with a chemostat with cell recycle in terms of (i) biomass concentration $X$, (ii) washout dilution rate $D_c$, (iii) operational stability, and (iv) industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Schematic + Discussion

**Working Principle (schematic shown in SECTION 4.1):**

A chemostat with cell recycle consists of:
- A **bioreactor** (CSTR, volume V) where cells grow on substrate
- A **separator** (centrifuge, microfilter, or settler) that divides the reactor outlet
- A **recycle line** that returns concentrated cells to the reactor inlet
- An **effluent line** that discharges substrate-depleted product

**Working Steps:**
1. Sterile feed enters reactor at flow rate $F$ with substrate $S_0$
2. Recycle stream (αF) carrying concentrated cells ($cX$) rejoins feed at reactor inlet
3. Cells grow on substrate; reactor holds biomass $X$ and residual substrate $S$
4. Outlet ($F(1+\alpha)$) goes to separator
5. Separator: cells are concentrated; concentrated stream returns as recycle
6. Effluent (F) exits with biomass $X_e$ and substrate $S$

**Role of $\alpha$ and $c$ on Biomass Productivity:**

Increasing either $\alpha$ or $c$ reduces the recycle correction factor $(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$:

- **Higher $\alpha$** → more concentrated cells returned → lower $X_e$ but higher $X$
- **Higher $c$** → more concentrated recycle stream → much higher $X$
- When $c \to \infty$ (membrane filter), $X$ becomes very large but $X_e \to 0$ (all cells retained)

The **volumetric productivity** $P_X = D \cdot X_e$ remains tied to substrate consumption: $P_X = D \cdot Y(S_0-S)$. However, **cell density** in the reactor and **specific productivity** (per cell) for intracellular products increases dramatically.

> **Marks Allocation:** Schematic (2M), Working explanation (2M), Role of α (1.5M), Role of c (1.5M).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Comparative Analysis

| S.No. | Parameter | Plain Chemostat | Chemostat with Cell Recycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Biomass $X$** | $X = Y(S_0 - S)$ | $X = \dfrac{Y(S_0 - S)}{1+\alpha-\alpha c}$ — **higher** by factor $1/(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ |
| (ii) | **Washout $D_c$** | $D_c = \mu_{\max}$ | $D_c = \dfrac{\mu_{\max}}{1+\alpha-\alpha c}$ — **higher**, allowing $D > \mu_{\max}$ operation |
| (iii) | **Operational Stability** | Sensitive to perturbations near $D_c$; washout at $D > \mu_{\max}$ | More robust; wider operating window; cell reservoir buffers shocks |
| (iv) | **Industrial Applications** | Microbial growth studies, single-pass fermentations, SCP | Perfusion mammalian cell culture, wastewater treatment (activated sludge), high-density yeast fermentation, ethanol from high-gravity brewing |

**Key Engineering Takeaway:**

> Cell recycle is **preferable** when:
> - The product is **cell-associated** (intracellular, biomass-bound)
> - **High cell density** is desired for downstream extraction
> - **Long-term continuous operation** is needed without washout risk
>
> Plain chemostat is **preferable** when:
> - The product is **extracellular** and cell-free filtrate is desired
> - Simplicity and low CAPEX are priorities
> - Single-pass kinetics are being studied

> **Marks Allocation:** Table structure (2M), Correct formulas for both systems (2M), Correct comparison statements (2M), Industrial applications (1M).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:** Common errors include:
> 1. Drawing the schematic without clearly marking the **recycle loop** (loses 1 mark immediately)
> 2. Writing $D_c = \mu_{\max}(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ instead of dividing — sign/inversion mistake
> 3. Listing *applications without justification* — always pair an application with the *property* that makes it suitable
> 4. Confusing "biomass productivity" with "cell density" — they are related but different quantities

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Chemostat with Cell Recycle**

### Must-Know Equations (5 formulas)

- ✅ $\mu = D(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ — **specific growth rate at steady state**
- ✅ $X = \dfrac{Y(S_0 - S)}{1+\alpha-\alpha c}$ — **reactor biomass**
- ✅ $X_e = X(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ — **effluent biomass**
- ✅ $D_c = \dfrac{\mu_{\max}}{1+\alpha-\alpha c}$ — **washout dilution rate**
- ✅ $P_X = D \cdot X_e = D \cdot Y(S_0-S)$ — **biomass productivity**

### Must-Know Definitions (4 terms)

- ✅ **Recycle ratio $\alpha$**: $F_R / F$
- ✅ **Concentration factor $c$**: $X_R / X$
- ✅ **Washout**: condition when $X \to 0$ and $S \to S_0$
- ✅ **Perfusion**: industrial term for chemostat-with-cell-recycle operation

### Must-Know Limiting Cases (3 limits)

- ✅ $\alpha = 0$ → reduces to **plain chemostat**
- ✅ $c = 1$ → recycle behaves as plain dilution (no benefit)
- ✅ $c = (1+\alpha)/\alpha$ → $D_c \to 0$, system infeasible (cells accumulate unboundedly)

### Must-Know Real-World Examples

- ✅ **Activated sludge process** (wastewater treatment) — the world's largest cell recycle system
- ✅ **Mammalian cell perfusion bioreactors** for monoclonal antibodies
- ✅ **High-gravity brewing** with yeast recycle in ethanol plants
- ✅ **Continuous beer fermentation** with cone-bottom settling tanks

### Must-Know Common Mistakes

- ❌ Using $\mu = D$ instead of $\mu = D(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$ when solving Monod for $S$
- ❌ Forgetting sterile feed assumption ($X_0 = 0$)
- ❌ Confusing $D = F/V$ with $D_{\text{total}} = F(1+\alpha)/V$
- ❌ Sign error: writing $(1+\alpha+\alpha c)$ instead of $(1+\alpha-\alpha c)$

### One-Line Takeaway

> *"In a chemostat with cell recycle, the cells live longer than the fluid — this single decoupling is the source of every advantage the system offers."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Structured Models for Analysis of Various Bioprocess: Compartmental Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT834 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Study of Structured Models for Analysis of Various Bioprocess: Compartmental Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structured Models (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *structured model* is a mathematical representation of a microbial system in which the internal physiological state of the cell is explicitly described by one or more internal variables (such as age, mass, DNA content, RNA content, or metabolic enzyme levels). Unlike unstructured models that treat the cell as a single homogeneous lump, structured models preserve the heterogeneity of the cell population.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compartmental Models:**
> Compartmental models are a specific class of structured models in which the cell population is divided into a finite number of discrete compartments, each representing a distinct physiological or morphological state. Cells are assumed to be uniformly distributed within a compartment but behave differently between compartments. Transitions between compartments are governed by specific rate functions.

### 1.2 Classification of Bioprocess Models

| Model Type | Description | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| Unstructured, Unsegregated | Single lumped variable for biomass | Cell treated as a "black box" |
| Structured, Unsegregated | Internal state variables included | E.g., Two-compartment models |
| Unstructured, Segregated | Population treated as discrete entities | E.g., Particle-based models |
| **Structured, Segregated** | **Both internal state and individual cells considered** | **E.g., Compartmental models** |

> [!NOTE]
> Compartmental models lie at the intersection of **structured** and **segregated** modeling. The "structure" refers to the division into physiological states, while "segregation" refers to the discrete treatment of subpopulations.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **busy hospital emergency ward** during a pandemic:
- Patients arrive continuously (just as nutrients are fed into a bioreactor).
- Doctors classify incoming patients into discrete categories based on symptom severity: *Mild*, *Moderate*, *Critical*, *Recovering* — these are the **compartments**.
- Patients move from one compartment to another based on clinical rules: a Mild case may progress to Moderate after some time, or recover and be discharged.
- The number of patients in each compartment changes over time depending on inflow, outflow, and transitions between compartments.
- Critically, **two patients in the "Mild" compartment are treated identically** (uniformity within a compartment), but a Mild patient behaves differently from a Critical patient (heterogeneity between compartments).

This mirrors how compartmental models describe a microbial culture: **biomass flows between discrete physiological states, each governed by its own kinetics**, while cells within the same compartment are assumed biochemically equivalent.

### 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU Board Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Vocabulary (High Yield for 14-mark Questions):**
> - **Biomass Compartment:** A physiological state distinguished by a measurable internal variable (mass, age, DNA, etc.).
> - **Transition Rate (kᵢ):** The specific rate at which cells move from compartment *i* to compartment *i+1* (units: h⁻¹).
> - **Partitioning Function:** Describes how mother cells divide asymmetrically to produce daughter cells of different sizes/ages.
> - **Distribution Function:** Probability density describing the spread of cells across compartments.
> - **Steady-State Distribution:** Time-invariant profile of cells across compartments under continuous operation (chemostat).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Rationale: Why Compartmental Models?

The Monod-type unstructured models fail to predict several experimentally observed phenomena:

1. **Diauxic growth** — sequential consumption of two carbon sources.
2. **Overshoot phenomenon** — transient accumulation of intermediates.
3. **Storage compound dynamics** — e.g., polyhydroxybutyrate (PHB) accumulation in *Cupriavidus necator*.
4. **Recombinant protein instability** — plasmid loss and segregational instability in genetically modified organisms.
5. **Synchronous culture behavior** — synchronous division in batch cultures.
6. **Lag phase dynamics** — adaptation of inoculated cells to a new environment.

A compartmental model captures these by explicitly tracking **different physiological subpopulations** rather than averaging over them.

### 2.2 Classification of Compartmental Models

> [!NOTE]
> Compartmental models are categorized by the **internal variable** chosen to define the compartments.

**(a) Age-Dependent Compartmental Models**
- Cells are divided by **chronological age** (time since birth).
- Each compartment *i* contains cells aged between *(i−1)Δa* and *iΔa*.
- The classical **Sinko–Streifer** model is the most cited example.

**(b) Mass-Dependent Compartmental Models**
- Cells are divided by **cell mass** rather than age.
- Useful when growth is correlated with cell size (most prokaryotes and yeasts follow this rule).
- **Eakman–Fredrickson–Tsuchiya** model is the classical reference.

**(c) Morphologically Structured Models**
- Cells divided by morphological features: e.g., budding yeast (G1, S, G2/M phases).
- Useful for **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** cultures.

**(d) Internally Structured Models**
- Cells divided by intracellular composition: DNA, RNA, protein, carbohydrate content.
- E.g., **Ramkrishna's Cybernetic Models**.

**(e) Single-Cell Models / Cybernetic Models**
- Cells divided by metabolic regime: e.g., *fast-growing* vs *slow-growing* compartments.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Equations for Compartmental Model Analysis (High-Yield for Board Exam)**

| # | Equation | Description / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dN_i}{dt} = k_{i-1} N_{i-1} - k_i N_i$ | Mass-balance for compartment *i* (no growth) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dX_i}{dt} = (k_{i-1} - k_i - \mu_i) X_i$ | Mass-balance with growth and transitions |
| 3 | $f(m) \, dm = $ number of cells with mass in $[m, m+dm]$ | Mass distribution function |
| 4 | $\mu = \int_0^{\infty} r(m) \, f(m) \, dm$ | Overall specific growth rate (population average) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (r(m) \, n)}{\partial m} = - \Gamma(m) \, n$ | Population Balance Equation (PBE) |
| 6 | $r(m) = \mu_{\max} \left(1 - \dfrac{m}{m^*}\right)$ | Eakman growth rate vs cell mass |
| 7 | $\dfrac{dn_i}{dt} = (k_{i-1} - k_i) n_i - \Gamma_i \, n_i$ | Compartment-level PBE with division |
| 8 | $\Gamma(m) = 2 \, p(2m) \, r(2m)$ | Division rate function (binary fission) |
| 9 | $X_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i$ | Total biomass from all compartments |
| 10 | $\mu_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sum_i \mu_i X_i}{X_{\text{total}}}$ | Weighted average specific growth rate |

> [!NOTE]
> In the equations above, $k_i$ has units of **h⁻¹**, biomass $X_i$ has units of **g L⁻¹**, mass $m$ has units of **pg**, and $\mu$ has units of **h⁻¹**.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Compartmental Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Bioprocess Engineer Cares:**
> 1. **Optimizing fed-batch cultures** — predicting overflow metabolism and acetate formation in *E. coli*.
> 2. **Designing induction strategies** — for recombinant protein production where plasmid-bearing cells differ from plasmid-free cells.
> 3. **Predicting diauxic lag** — important in industrial fermentations with mixed-substrate feed.
> 4. **Scale-up decisions** — segregated models predict non-ideal mixing effects on subpopulation dynamics.
> 5. **Control of synchronous cultures** — used in biopharmaceutical production for enhanced product uniformity.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Age-Dependent Compartmental Model (Sinko–Streifer Framework)

We begin with the **population balance equation** for an age-structured microbial population.

**Step 1 — Define the Age Distribution Function.**

Let $n(a,t)$ be the number density of cells of age $a$ at time $t$. By definition, $n(a,t) \, da$ gives the number of cells aged between $a$ and $a+da$ per unit volume.

**Step 2 — Account for Cells Aging Out of an Age Class.**

In time $dt$, cells in the age interval $[a, a+da]$ age into $[a+da, a+2\,da]$. By a simple Taylor expansion:

$$n(a+da, t+dt) = n(a,t) + \dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t} dt + \dfrac{\partial n}{\partial a} da$$

**Step 3 — Introduce Growth and Division.**

Cells grow at a size-dependent rate $r(m)$ and divide at a division rate $\Gamma(a)$. After accounting for cells lost to division and those added by division of older cells, the **partial differential equation (PDE)** becomes:

$$\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial n}{\partial a} + \Gamma(a) \, n(a,t) = 2 \, \Gamma(2a) \, n(2a, t)$$

> This is the **Sinko–Streifer age-structured population balance equation**, and it is the foundation for the cell-cycle model.

**Step 4 — Transform to Steady-State Chemostat.**

Under steady state in a chemostat with dilution rate $D$:

$$D \, n(a) + \dfrac{d n(a)}{da} + \Gamma(a) \, n(a) = 2 \, \Gamma(2a) \, n(2a)$$

**Step 5 — Solve via Laplace Transform.**

Multiplying both sides by $e^{D a} \, da$ and integrating:

$$n(a) = 2 \int_0^{a} \Gamma(\tau) \, n(\tau) \, e^{-D(a-\tau)} \, d\tau \cdot e^{-D a}$$

This integral equation can be discretized into the **compartmental form**:

$$\dfrac{d n_i}{dt} = k_{i-1} \, n_{i-1} - k_i \, n_i - \Gamma_i \, n_i + \text{division source}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Solution for a Two-Compartment Model

> A **two-compartment model** is the simplest and most KTU-board-relevant structured model. It splits biomass into an "active" fraction $X_a$ and a "storage" fraction $X_s$.

**Step 1 — Write Mass Balances for Both Compartments.**

For the active compartment (growing, consuming substrate):

$$\dfrac{dX_a}{dt} = -k_a \, X_a + \mu_a \, X_a - D \, X_a$$

For the storage compartment (accumulating product, slower turnover):

$$\dfrac{dX_s}{dt} = k_a \, X_a + \mu_s \, X_s - k_s \, X_s - D \, X_s$$

**Step 2 — Apply Steady-State Condition ($\frac{d}{dt} = 0$).**

For $X_a$:

$$0 = (\mu_a - k_a - D) X_a \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mu_a = k_a + D$$

For $X_s$:

$$0 = k_a \, X_a + (\mu_s - k_s - D) X_s \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{X_s}{X_a} = \dfrac{k_a}{D + k_s - \mu_s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Biomass.**

$$X_{\text{total}} = X_a + X_s = X_a \left[ 1 + \dfrac{k_a}{D + k_s - \mu_s} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Compute Specific Growth Rate (Population Average).**

$$\mu_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\mu_a X_a + \mu_s X_s}{X_{\text{total}}}$$

Substituting the ratio from Step 2:

$$\mu_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\mu_a + \mu_s \, \left( \dfrac{k_a}{D + k_s - \mu_s} \right)}{1 + \left( \dfrac{k_a}{D + k_s - \mu_s} \right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This expression shows that the **overall growth rate of the culture is a weighted harmonic-like average** of the compartment-specific rates, weighted by biomass fractions. This is the central insight: structured models predict that the overall $\mu$ is **not** simply Monod-like but depends on the distribution of cells across physiological states.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Compartmental Model for a Bioprocess

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is exam-ready: students should be able to justify their choice of compartmental framework for any given bioprocess.

| Bioprocess | Recommended Compartmental Variable | Number of Compartments | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Baker's yeast (fed-batch) | Cell mass | 3 (small / medium / large) | Size governs budding cycle |
| Recombinant *E. coli* | Plasmid content | 2 (plasmid-bearing / plasmid-free) | Segregational instability is critical |
| PHB production in *C. necator* | Internal PHB content | 2 (active / PHB-rich) | Storage compound drives dynamics |
| Synchronous CHO cell culture | Cell-cycle phase | 4 (G1 / S / G2 / M) | Phase determines productivity |
| Diauxic growth on glucose/lactose | Substrate uptake regime | 2 (glucose-consuming / lactose-consuming) | Catabolite repression governs switch |
| Antibiotic production (*Penicillium*) | Morphological form | 3 (vegetative / producing / degenerated) | Trophophase–idiophase transition |
| Activated sludge (wastewater) | Floc size | Multiple | Dispersed growth across floc sizes |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A recombinant *E. coli* culture is modeled as a two-compartment system. Plasmid-bearing cells ($X^+$) and plasmid-free cells ($X^-$) undergo the following transitions in a chemostat operated at dilution rate $D = 0.3$ h⁻¹:
> - Specific growth rate of plasmid-bearing cells: $\mu^+ = 0.55$ h⁻¹
> - Specific growth rate of plasmid-free cells: $\mu^- = 0.60$ h⁻¹
> - Plasmid loss rate: $k_{\text{loss}} = 0.02$ h⁻¹
> - Mutation rate (plasmid-free → plasmid-bearing): negligible.
>
> **Find the steady-state fraction of plasmid-bearing cells.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass balance for plasmid-bearing cells $X^+$:**

$$\dfrac{dX^+}{dt} = \mu^+ X^+ - k_{\text{loss}} X^+ - D \, X^+$$

At steady state:

$$0 = (\mu^+ - k_{\text{loss}} - D) X^+$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance for plasmid-free cells $X^-$:**

$$\dfrac{dX^-}{dt} = \mu^- X^- + k_{\text{loss}} X^+ - D \, X^-$$

At steady state:

$$0 = \mu^- X^- + k_{\text{loss}} X^+ - D \, X^-$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the ratio $X^- / X^+$:**

$$\mu^- X^- + k_{\text{loss}} X^+ = D \, X^-$$

$$(D - \mu^-) X^- = k_{\text{loss}} X^+$$

$$\dfrac{X^-}{X^+} = \dfrac{k_{\text{loss}}}{D - \mu^-} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.3 - 0.6} = \dfrac{0.02}{-0.3} = -0.0667$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **negative ratio** is physically impossible — this signals the model assumption is inconsistent! Specifically, $\mu^- > D$ means plasmid-free cells **grow faster than the washout threshold**, so they dominate the culture and ultimately displace plasmid-bearing cells.

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

This is precisely the **segregational instability** phenomenon: even a small plasmid loss rate ($k_{\text{loss}} = 0.02$ h⁻¹) causes complete takeover by plasmid-free cells when their intrinsic growth advantage ($\mu^- > \mu^+$) exceeds the dilution rate. The two-compartment model **quantitatively explains** why long-term continuous cultures of recombinant strains are unstable.

> [!NOTE]
> Industrial solution: operate at $D > \mu^-$ (high dilution), but this also risks washout of the entire population. The optimum is to operate at $D \approx \mu^- + 5\%$ to selectively retain plasmid-bearing cells.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B -->|"k2 transition"| C["Compartment 3\nMature Cells\nLarge Mass"]
    C -->|"k3 division"| D["Compartment 1\nDaughter Cell 1"]
    C -->|"k3 division"| E["Compartment 1\nDaughter Cell 2"]
    F["Substrate S_in"] -->|"uptake r1"| A
    F -->|"uptake r2"| B
    F -->|"uptake r3"| C
    A -->|"growth mu1"| P1["Biomass Increase"]
    B -->|"growth mu2"| P2["Biomass Increase"]
    C -->|"growth mu3"| P3["Biomass Increase"]
    P1 --> Q["Product Formation"]
    P2 --> Q
    P3 --> Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above: $k_i$ are first-order transition rate constants (h⁻¹), $r_i$ are substrate uptake rates (g L⁻¹ h⁻¹), and $\mu_i$ are compartment-specific growth rates (h⁻¹). Binary fission (cell division) is shown returning two daughter cells to Compartment 1.

### 4.3 Cell-Cycle Compartmental Architecture (Eukaryotic Cells)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1["Compartment G1\nGap Phase 1\nCell Grows\nNon-Dividing"] -->|"passage through R"| S["Compartment S\nDNA Synthesis\nChromosomes Replicate"]
    S -->|"completion of DNA replication"| G2["Compartment G2\nGap Phase 2\nPrepares for Mitosis"]
    G2 -->|"entry into mitosis"| M["Compartment M\nMitosis\nCell Division"]

    M -->|"cytokinesis"| D2["Daughter Cell 2\nreturns to G1"]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> In CHO cell cultures for monoclonal antibody production, **most recombinant protein is secreted during G1**, while **cell division occurs in M**. By synchronizing the population (using thymidine block or serum starvation), engineers can boost productivity 2–3 fold. The compartmental model formalizes this strategy.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Distinguish between unstructured and structured models in bioprocess engineering. Give two examples where unstructured models fail to predict observed behavior.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Unstructured Models:** Treat the cell as a homogeneous "black box" with no internal variables. A single biomass concentration $X$ describes the entire population. Examples: Monod kinetics, Logistic growth.

> **Structured Models:** Explicitly describe internal cellular state using one or more variables (mass, age, DNA, RNA, enzyme levels). The cell population is divided into compartments representing distinct physiological states.

> **Failures of unstructured models (any two):**
> 1. **Diauxic growth** — sequential utilization of two carbon sources (glucose then lactose) cannot be captured by a single Monod equation.
> 2. **Overshoot phenomenon** — transient intermediate accumulation during substrate pulse experiments.
> 3. **Storage polymer dynamics** — e.g., PHB accumulation in *C. necator* under nitrogen limitation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is a compartmental model? Why are cells assumed to be uniformly distributed within a compartment but heterogeneous between compartments?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **compartmental model** is a structured model that divides the cell population into a finite number of discrete compartments, each defined by a specific value (or range) of an internal variable such as age, mass, or DNA content.

> **Uniformity within a compartment:** Cells in the same compartment are assumed to share identical physiological properties (e.g., growth rate, division probability). This is a *modeling simplification* that keeps the equations tractable.

> **Heterogeneity between compartments:** Different compartments represent genuinely distinct physiological states (e.g., G1 vs S phase cells, plasmid-bearing vs plasmid-free cells). This heterogeneity is what gives the model its predictive power.

> Cells transition from one compartment to another at a rate governed by kinetics (often first-order), and only upon division does a "reset" event occur, returning daughter cells to the initial compartment.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

> **Module-Level Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper for 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, derive the governing equations for a **two-compartment chemostat model** in which biomass is split into an "active" fraction ($X_a$) and a "storage" fraction ($X_s$). Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous stirred-tank bioreactor operates at dilution rate $D = 0.25$ h⁻¹. The two compartments have parameters:
- $\mu_a = 0.45$ h⁻¹, $k_a = 0.08$ h⁻¹ (transition from active to storage)
- $\mu_s = 0.05$ h⁻¹, $k_s = 0.03$ h⁻¹ (transition from storage to active)

Calculate the steady-state ratio $X_s / X_a$ and the overall specific growth rate $\mu_{\text{avg}}$. Comment on whether the culture is dominated by active or storage biomass. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The cell population is divided into two discrete compartments.
2. Cells within a compartment are physiologically identical.
3. Cell transitions between compartments follow first-order kinetics with respect to biomass.
4. The chemostat operates at steady state.
5. No mutation between compartments (only physiological adaptation).

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    XA["Compartment Xa\nActive Biomass\nmu_a = 0.45 per h"] -->|"k_a Xa"| XS["Compartment Xs\nStorage Biomass\nmu_s = 0.05 per h"]
    XS -->|"k_s Xs"| XA
    S_in["Substrate S_in"] -->|"uptake"| XA
    S_in -->|"uptake"| XS
    XA -->|"D Xa"| W["Washout"]
    XS -->|"D Xs"| W
```

**Mass Balance Equations (unsteady state):**

For active compartment:
$$\dfrac{dX_a}{dt} = -k_a X_a + k_s X_s + \mu_a X_a - D X_a$$

For storage compartment:
$$\dfrac{dX_s}{dt} = k_a X_a - k_s X_s + \mu_s X_s - D X_s$$

**Steady-State Form (set $\frac{d}{dt} = 0$):**

$$0 = (\mu_a - k_a - D) X_a + k_s X_s$$

$$0 = k_a X_a + (\mu_s - k_s - D) X_s$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Apply steady-state to $X_a$ equation:**

$$0 = (0.45 - 0.08 - 0.25) X_a + k_s X_s$$

$$0 = 0.12 \, X_a + k_s X_s$$

$$X_s = -\dfrac{0.12}{k_s} X_a$$

**Step 2 — Apply steady-state to $X_s$ equation:**

$$0 = k_a X_a + (\mu_s - k_s - D) X_s$$

$$0 = 0.08 \, X_a + (0.05 - 0.03 - 0.25) X_s$$

$$0 = 0.08 \, X_a - 0.23 \, X_s$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system simultaneously:**

From equation (a): $X_s = -\dfrac{0.12}{0.03} X_a = -4 X_a$

But this yields a **negative ratio**, indicating inconsistency. Re-examining equation (a):

$$0.12 X_a + 0.03 X_s = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad X_s = -4 X_a$$

> [!WARNING]
> A negative biomass ratio is **physically impossible**. This means our parameter set violates one of the steady-state feasibility conditions.

**Step 4 — Re-examine assumptions and feasibility:**

The condition for feasible steady state requires:

$$\mu_a - k_a - D < 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \mu_s - k_s - D < 0$$

Check: $\mu_a - k_a - D = 0.45 - 0.08 - 0.25 = +0.12$ (positive — washout-prone)

This means active biomass alone cannot reach steady state; it requires inflow from the storage compartment to balance washout. The only feasible solution requires $X_s < 0$, which is unphysical.

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**

The given parameter set describes a culture where **active cells grow faster than they are washed out**, but the **transition rate $k_a$ exceeds the net retention of active biomass**. To make the system feasible, either:
- Reduce $D$ below $0.12$ h⁻¹, or
- Reduce $k_a$ below $0.08$ h⁻¹, or
- Increase substrate supply.

**Corrected Feasibility Check (modified parameter: $D = 0.10$ h⁻¹):**

$$0 = (0.45 - 0.08 - 0.10) X_a + 0.03 X_s = 0.27 X_a + 0.03 X_s$$
$$0 = 0.08 X_a + (0.05 - 0.03 - 0.10) X_s = 0.08 X_a - 0.08 X_s$$

From the second equation: $X_s = X_a$.

> **Therefore, at $D = 0.10$ h⁻¹, the steady-state ratio is $X_s / X_a = 1$, and the overall growth rate $\mu_{\text{avg}} = 0.25$ h⁻¹.**

> The culture is **equally split** between active and storage biomass at this operating condition. Increasing $D$ further tips the balance toward washout; decreasing $D$ allows more accumulation.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Alternative Model for 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Describe the **Sinko–Streifer age-structured model**. Derive its governing equation starting from the population balance principle and explain the role of the **division rate function** $\Gamma(a)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain how compartmental models have been applied to model the **trophophase–idiophase transition** in antibiotic-producing fungal cultures (*Penicillium chrysogenum*). Discuss the engineering implications for fed-batch penicillin production. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Sinko–Streifer Model Derivation:**

**Conceptual Framework:**

The Sinko–Streifer model (1969–1971) is the classical age-structured population balance model for microbial cultures. Cells are characterized solely by their **chronological age** $a$ (time elapsed since the last division).

**Define:** $n(a, t) \, da$ = number of cells (per unit volume) of age in $[a, a+da]$ at time $t$.

**Population Balance Over an Age Interval:**

In a small time interval $dt$, cells in $[a, a+da]$ age into $[a+dt, a+da+dt]$. The number is conserved except for division events. Each cell that divides produces **two daughter cells of age zero**, which then re-enter the population at $a = 0$.

**Differential Balance:**

The change in number of cells aged $a$ = inflow from age $(a-dt)$ − outflow to age $(a+dt)$ − cells lost by division + cells added by division of cells aged $2a$ (a mother of age $a$ was born when its mother divided, so a cell dividing now was born at age $0$ and now divides to give two daughters of age $0$).

Applying the limit $dt \to 0$:

$$\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial n}{\partial a} = -\Gamma(a) \, n(a, t) + 2 \, \Gamma(2a) \, n(2a, t)$$

This is the **Sinko–Streifer equation** for an age-structured population.

**Role of $\Gamma(a)$:**

The function $\Gamma(a)$ is the **age-specific division rate** (units: h⁻¹). It describes the probability per unit time that a cell of age $a$ will divide. Typical forms:

- **Step function:** $\Gamma(a) = 0$ for $a < a_d$, and $\Gamma(a) = k_d$ for $a \geq a_d$, where $a_d$ is the minimum division age.
- **Exponential form:** $\Gamma(a) = k_d \, e^{\alpha a}$.
- **Gaussian form:** centered at the mean division age $a_{\text{mean}}$.

> The division rate function governs **cell-cycle synchronization**: a sharp peak in $\Gamma(a)$ produces synchronized cultures; a broad $\Gamma(a)$ produces asynchronous cultures.

**Boundary Condition:**

At $a = 0$ (newborn cells):

$$n(0, t) = 2 \int_0^{\infty} \Gamma(a) \, n(a, t) \, da$$

This states that the inflow of newborn cells equals twice the total division rate.

---

**Part (b) — Compartmental Modeling of Antibiotic Production:**

**Trophophase–Idiophase Concept:**

In *Penicillium chrysogenum* (the industrial penicillin producer), growth and product formation are **temporally separated**:

| Phase | Nutrient Status | Primary Activity | Compartment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trophophase** | Excess carbon, balanced nutrients | Rapid biomass growth | Compartment 1: *Vegetative Cells* |
| **Idiophase** | Nutrient limited (especially N or P) | Secondary metabolite (penicillin) production | Compartment 2: *Producing Cells* |

**Three-Compartment Model Proposed by Various Researchers:**

1. **Compartment 1:** Vegetative, actively growing hyphae.
2. **Compartment 2:** Differentiated, penicillin-producing cells.
3. **Compartment 3:** Degenerated, non-producing cells (autolysis).

**Mass Balances:**

$$\dfrac{dX_1}{dt} = \mu_1 X_1 - k_{12} X_1 - D X_1$$

$$\dfrac{dX_2}{dt} = k_{12} X_1 - k_{23} X_2 - D X_2$$

$$\dfrac{dX_3}{dt} = k_{23} X_2 - D X_3$$

Penicillin production is associated **only** with Compartment 2:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dt} = q_P \, X_2 - D \, P$$

where $q_P$ is the specific penicillin production rate (mg P per g cells per h).

**Engineering Implications for Fed-Batch Penicillin Production:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** To maximize penicillin, the operator must **drive the culture from Compartment 1 → Compartment 2 as fast as possible** while **minimizing transition to Compartment 3 (autolysis).**

| Strategy | Effect on Compartments | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Rapid glucose depletion after initial growth | $X_1 \to X_2$ accelerates | Higher penicillin titer |
| Slow glucose feed | Maintains $X_1$ too long | Lower penicillin, higher biomass |
| Excessive depletion | $X_2 \to X_3$ accelerates | Autolysis, penicillin degradation by penicillase |
| Optimal fed-batch profile | $X_2$ peaks at $\sim 60\%$ of total biomass | Maximum penicillin yield |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Industrial operators sometimes overfeed glucose to "boost growth," but this **reverses the trophophase–idiophase transition**, returning cells to Compartment 1 and **shutting down penicillin synthesis**. The compartmental model formalizes this trade-off.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing structured with segregated** — Structured refers to internal state; segregated refers to discrete individual cells. Compartmental models are **both** structured AND segregated.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — Always write $k_i$ in h⁻¹ and $X$ in g L⁻¹. Losing units costs 0.5–1 mark per question.
> 3. **Ignoring the chemostat dilution term $D X_i$** — Many students forget washout in mass balances; this is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 4. **Treating compartments as parallel processes** — They are **sequential/cyclic**: cells return to Compartment 1 after division. This is the most common conceptual error.
> 5. **Misapplying the division source term** — The factor of **2** in $2 \, \Gamma(2a) \, n(2a, t)$ comes from binary fission; do not omit it.
> 6. **Not checking feasibility** — A correct algebraic solution that yields a negative biomass is **incorrect** by physical reasoning; always cross-check signs.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Module 4 — Compartmental Models: High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Definition Core:** A compartmental model divides a cell population into **discrete physiological compartments** based on an internal variable (age, mass, DNA, plasmid content, morphology).

- **Why It Exists:** Unstructured models (Monod) fail for **diauxie, overshoot, storage, segregational instability, and synchronous cultures**.

- **Two Big Families:** (1) **Age-structured** (Sinko–Streifer) and (2) **Mass-structured** (Eakman–Fredrickson–Tsuchiya). Eukaryotic cultures often use **morphologically structured** models.

- **Master Equation (Age-Structured):** $\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial n}{\partial a} = -\Gamma(a) n(a,t) + 2 \Gamma(2a) n(2a,t)$ — memorize this.

- **Boundary Condition:** Newborns at $a = 0$ satisfy $n(0,t) = 2 \int_0^{\infty} \Gamma(a) n(a,t) \, da$.

- **Two-Compartment Chemostat Steady State:** Solve the linear system $\mu_a - k_a - D$ and $\mu_s - k_s - D$; check feasibility before accepting the algebraic answer.

- **Recombinant Strain Model:** $X^+$ (plasmid-bearing) and $X^-$ (plasmid-free) with loss rate $k_{\text{loss}}$. Predicts **segregational instability** quantitatively.

- **Industrial Applications:**
  - Recombinant protein (plasmid stability).
  - PHB biopolymer (active vs storage compartments).
  - Penicillin (trophophase vs idiophase).
  - CHO cell cultures (G1/S/G2/M phases).
  - Baker's yeast (budding cycle compartments).
  - Activated sludge (floc-size segregation).

- **Key Insight to Quote in Exams:** *"A compartmental model captures population heterogeneity by recognizing that the overall growth rate is a weighted average of compartment-specific rates — and that subpopulation dynamics (not just bulk kinetics) govern product formation."*

- **Equation Symbols (don't mix up):**
  - $k_i$ = transition rate constant (h⁻¹).
  - $\Gamma(a)$ = age-specific division rate (h⁻¹).
  - $\mu_i$ = compartment-specific growth rate (h⁻¹).
  - $D$ = dilution rate (h⁻¹).
  - $n(a,t)$ = age density function (cells L⁻¹ h⁻¹ when integrated over $a$).
  - $X_i$ = biomass concentration (g L⁻¹).

- **Sanity Checks for Board Exam Solutions:**
  - All biomass must be $\geq 0$.
  - All specific rates must be positive.
  - At steady state, dilution rate $D$ must be less than the **maximum compartment-specific $\mu$**, otherwise washout occurs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models of Cellular Energetics and Metabolism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT834 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Models of Cellular Energetics and Metabolism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Models of Cellular Energetics and Metabolism — KTU Module 4

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cellular Energetics** is the quantitative branch of bioengineering that describes energy transformations, material flows, and thermodynamic efficiency of living cells using **mathematical models** that couple stoichiometry, thermodynamics, and kinetics.

In KTU bioprocess engineering context, a **Model of Cellular Energetics** is a set of coupled algebraic/differential equations that quantitatively relate:

- **Substrate uptake** → **Biomass generation** → **Product formation** → **Energy (ATP) turnover**

The three classical model families demanded by the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

| Model Type | Mathematical Form | Question it Answers |
|------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Black-Box (Stoichiometric) Model** | Linear balance equations | *How much biomass can theoretically form per gram of substrate?* |
| **Thermodynamic (Energetic) Model** | Gibbs energy + ATP balance | *How much ATP is needed/regenerated per gram of biomass?* |
| **Cybernetic / Structured Model** | ODEs with regulatory variables | *How does the cell *decide* which pathway to use?* |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Cell as a Small Chemical Factory

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Microbial Cell = A Living Power Plant + Sugar Mill**

Imagine a **sugar mill** (your microbial cell):

| Factory Element | Cellular Counterpart | Engineering Quantity |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Coal/Sugar intake | Glucose substrate uptake (qS) | **mmol g⁻¹ h⁻¹** |
| Electricity generated | ATP turnover | **mmol ATP gDCW⁻¹ h⁻¹** |
| Maintenance crew salaries | Maintenance metabolism (mS) | **mmol g⁻¹ h⁻¹** |
| Finished goods (bricks/cars) | New biomass produced (rx) | **gDCW L⁻¹ h⁻¹** |
| Wasted heat (smoke) | Heat dissipated by catabolism | **ΔH (kJ/mol)** |
| Plant expansion (more workers) | Growth-associated processes | **YG, μ (h⁻¹)** |
| Overhead (lights on, idle machinery) | Non-growth-associated maintenance | **mATP, mO** |

Just as a factory manager must know: *"How much coal per brick?"* and *"How much electricity per machine per hour?"*, the bioprocess engineer must know the analogous **yield coefficients (Y)** and **maintenance coefficients (m)**.

---

## 1.3 Why Cellular Energetics Matters (KTU Industry Relevance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important number in fermentation design is the **theoretical maximum biomass yield Y*ₓₛ** — it sets the **upper bound** on every fermentation process. A KTU student who cannot calculate Y*ₓₛ from stoichiometry cannot design a commercial bioreactor.

- Pharmaceutical industry: Recombinant protein yield depends on **ATP availability per cell**.
- Biofuel industry: Ethanol yield from glucose is capped by the **electron balance** (γ).
- Wastewater treatment: Oxygen demand is dictated by **substrate degree of reduction**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Cellular Energetics

### Pillar 1 — Thermodynamic Foundation (Gibbs Energy in Cells)

Life obeys the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**: any spontaneous reaction has **ΔG < 0**.
