For a biochemical reaction at cellular pH 7:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ'} + RT \ln(Q)$$

where:
- $\Delta G^{\circ'}$ = standard Gibbs energy at pH 7, **T = 298 K**, **1 M** (water = 55.5 M)
- $R$ = **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** (Universal gas constant)
- $Q$ = mass-action ratio of products/reactants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key biological reference: ATP hydrolysis**
> $\text{ATP}^{4-} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{ADP}^{3-} + \text{HPO}_4^{2-} + \text{H}^+$
> $\Delta G^{\circ'}_{ATP} = -30.5 \text{ kJ/mol} = -7.3 \text{ kcal/mol}$

This **30.5 kJ/mol** is the universal "energy currency" of the cell — every biosynthetic reaction is paid for in ATP multiples of this value.

---

### Pillar 2 — Adenylate Energy Charge (Atkinson, 1977)

Cells regulate their metabolism by sensing the ratio of charged vs. uncharged adenine nucleotides:

$$EC = \frac{[ATP] + 0.5\,[ADP]}{[ATP] + [ADP] + [AMP]}$$

| EC Value | Cell State |
|----------|------------|
| **0.95 – 1.00** | Starved / dormant / spores |
| **0.70 – 0.95** | Healthy growing cells (typical) |
| **< 0.50** | Senescent / dying cells |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Atkinson showed that **most regulatory enzymes are inhibited when EC deviates from ~0.85**. This single equation explains why cells do not run glycolysis and gluconeogenesis simultaneously.

---

### Pillar 3 — Yield Coefficients (The Engineering Balances)

The **biomass yield on substrate** is the most quoted number in biotechnology:

$$Y_{X/S} = \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S} \quad \text{(gDCW produced per g substrate consumed)}$$

Common siblings:

- $Y_{X/O}$ — biomass yield on oxygen (gDCW per g O₂)
- $Y_{P/S}$ — product yield on substrate (g product per g substrate)
- $Y_{X/P}$ — biomass-to-product ratio

---

## 2.2 The Maintenance Concept & Pirt's Equation

Herbert (1958) and Pirt (1965) showed that substrate is consumed for **two** purposes: **growth** AND **maintenance of existing biomass**.

> **Pirt's Equation (KTU-favorite formula):**
> $$q_S = \frac{\mu}{Y_G} + m_S$$
>
> where:
> - $q_S$ = specific substrate uptake rate (g substrate gDCW⁻¹ h⁻¹)
> - $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹)
> - $Y_G$ = *true* growth yield (excludes maintenance) — dimensionless (g/g)
> - $m_S$ = maintenance coefficient (g substrate gDCW⁻¹ h⁻¹)

Graphically, plotting $q_S$ vs $\mu$ gives a straight line of slope $1/Y_G$ and intercept $m_S$ — this is the standard KTU lab experiment.

---

## 2.3 Degree of Reduction (γ) and the Electron Balance

> [!NOTE]
> The **degree of reduction (γ)** is the number of electrons available per carbon atom when the substrate is fully oxidized to CO₂.

General formula (Minkevich, 1973):

$$\gamma_{\text{substrate}} = \frac{4n_C + n_H - 2n_O - 3n_N}{\,n_C\,}$$

| Substrate | Formula | $\gamma$ |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| Glucose | C₆H₁₂O₆ | 4 |
| Ethanol | C₂H₆O | 6 |
| Methanol | CH₄O | 6 |
| Methane | CH₄ | 8 |
| Acetic acid | C₂H₄O₂ | 4 |
| Ammonia | NH₃ | 3 (per N) |
| O₂ (electron acceptor) | O₂ | 4 (per O₂, but -4 per e⁻) |
| **Biomass (typical)** | CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂ | ~4.2 |

> **Maximum theoretical biomass yield:**
> $$Y_{X/S}^{\max} = \frac{\gamma_S \cdot M_X}{\gamma_X \cdot M_S}$$
> where $M_X \approx 24.6$ g/mol per carbon in biomass; for glucose ($M_S = 30$ gC/mol): $Y^{\max}_{X/S} \approx 0.82$ g/g-glucose — but realistic values are **0.45–0.50 g/g** because ~half the substrate is oxidized for ATP, not biomass.

---

## 2.4 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the **high-yield formula set** for KTU Module 4. Memorize the symbols; they will appear verbatim in university questions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT\ln Q$ | Gibbs free energy in cell | kJ/mol |
| 2 | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{ATP} = -30.5$ kJ/mol | ATP hydrolysis energy | kJ/mol |
| 3 | $EC = \dfrac{[ATP]+0.5[ADP]}{[ATP]+[ADP]+[AMP]}$ | Adenylate energy charge | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | Biomass yield on substrate | g/g |
| 5 | $q_S = \dfrac{\mu}{Y_G} + m_S$ | Pirt's equation | g g⁻¹ h⁻¹ |
| 6 | $q_S = \dfrac{1}{Y_G}\mu + m_S$ | Same as Pirt (alternate form) | g g⁻¹ h⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\mu = \mu_{\max}\dfrac{S}{K_S+S}\dfrac{C^*}{C^*+K_C}$ | Monod with Crabtree/oxygen limitation | h⁻¹ |
| 8 | $Y_{X/S}^{\max} = \dfrac{\gamma_S M_X}{\gamma_X M_S}$ | Theoretical biomass yield | g/g |
| 9 | $\gamma = \dfrac{4n_C + n_H - 2n_O - 3n_N}{n_C}$ | Degree of reduction | e⁻/C |
| 10 | $\sigma_S = \dfrac{m_S}{\dfrac{\mu}{Y_G}+m_S}$ | Maintenance fraction (Pirt) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $r_{X} = Y_{X/S}\,r_S$ | Growth-coupled rate | g L⁻¹ h⁻¹ |
| 12 | $r_{O_2} = \dfrac{\mu}{Y_{X/O}}$ | Oxygen demand for growth | g L⁻¹ h⁻¹ |
| 13 | $r_{ATP} = \dfrac{\mu}{Y_{X/ATP}} + m_{ATP}$ | Cellular ATP demand | mol gDCW⁻¹ h⁻¹ |
| 14 | $C_x H_y O_z N_w = aC_aH_bO_c + \dots$ | Stoichiometric biomass eqn. | — |
| 15 | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu X - k_d X$ | Net growth (with death) | g L⁻¹ h⁻¹ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where do these formulas earn money?**
>
> - **Insulin / mAb production:** CHO cells produce ~5 pg/cell/day of antibody; engineers compute the **ATP budget** and feed glucose + glutamine accordingly.
> - **Bioethanol:** Yeast converts glucose → ethanol with theoretical $Y_{P/S} = 0.51$ g/g (Pasteur equation); deviations from this signal contamination or stress.
> - **Single Cell Protein (Pruteen, ICI):** Methylococcus on methanol — $Y^{\max}_{X/S} \approx 0.68$ g/g-methanol; production rate dictated by **oxygen transfer rate (OTR)** which is set by $q_{O_2}$ and $\mu$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Derivation of Adenylate Energy Charge

We begin from the **adenylate kinase** equilibrium that interconverts ATP, ADP, AMP inside every cell:

$$\text{ATP} + \text{AMP} \rightleftharpoons 2\,\text{ADP} \quad\quad K_{eq} = \frac{[\text{ADP}]^2}{[\text{ATP}][\text{AMP}]} \approx 1 \;\text{at}\; 25^{\circ}\text{C}$$

Step 1 — Let the total adenine pool be conserved:

$$A_T = [ATP] + [ADP] + [AMP]$$

Step 2 — From equilibrium, set $[ADP]^2 = [ATP][AMP]$. Define $x = [ATP]/A_T$, $y = [ADP]/A_T$, $z = [AMP]/A_T$.

Step 3 — Substitute $y^2 = xz$ together with $x+y+z = 1$ and solve.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{From } y^2 &= xz \\
z &= \dfrac{y^2}{x} \\
\text{Substitute into } x + y + z &= 1: \\
x + y + \dfrac{y^2}{x} &= 1 \\
\text{Multiply by } x: \quad x^2 + xy + y^2 &= x \\
\text{Complete the square: }\quad (x + 0.5y)^2 + 0.75y^2 &= x
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — The **energy charge** is defined as the fraction of phosphoanhydride bonds available. ATP has 2 bonds, ADP has 1, AMP has 0.

$$EC = \frac{2[ATP] + 1[ADP]}{2[ATP] + 2[ADP] + 2[AMP]} = \frac{[ATP] + 0.5[ADP]}{A_T}$$

Step 5 — **Verification of range:** If only ATP present → $EC = 1$. If only AMP present → $EC = 0$. If equimolar ATP/ADP/AMP → $EC = (1 + 0.5)/3 = 0.5$. ✅ Matches the Atkinson range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pirt's Equation from Differential Balance

Start with the macroscopic substrate balance on a chemostat:

$$r_S = q_S \, X \quad\quad \text{(g substrate L⁻¹ h⁻¹)}$$

Decompose substrate consumption into two mutually exclusive terms:

$$q_S = q_S^{\text{growth}} + q_S^{\text{maintenance}}$$

Step 1 — **Growth-associated consumption** is proportional to growth rate $\mu$ with proportionality constant $1/Y_G$:

$$q_S^{\text{growth}} = \frac{\mu}{Y_G}$$

Step 2 — **Maintenance consumption** is independent of $\mu$ (cells must respire even when not growing):

$$q_S^{\text{maintenance}} = m_S$$

Step 3 — **Combine:**

$$\begin{aligned}
q_S &= \frac{\mu}{Y_G} + m_S \\
\text{or equivalently:} \quad \frac{1}{Y_{obs}} &= \frac{1}{Y_G} + \frac{m_S}{\mu} \\
\text{where } Y_{obs} &= \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}\;\text{(observed yield)}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — **Asymptotic behavior check:**
- At $\mu \to 0$ (zero growth, only maintenance): $q_S = m_S$ ✅
- At $\mu \to \mu_{\max}$: $q_S = \mu_{\max}/Y_G + m_S$ ✅
- $Y_{obs}$ decreases at low $\mu$ — consistent with "wash-out" experiments ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Maximum Yield from Degree of Reduction

**Starting point:** The electron balance. Each mole of substrate provides $\gamma_S n_C$ electrons; each mole of biomass incorporates $\gamma_X n_{C,X}$ electrons; the rest go to the terminal electron acceptor (O₂).

$$Y_{X/S}^{\max} = \frac{(\text{electrons in biomass})}{(\text{electrons in substrate})}$$

Step 1 — Moles of carbon in 1 g biomass (formula CH₁.₈O₀.₅N₀.₂, $M_X = 24.6$ g per mol C):

$$n_{C,X} = \frac{1 \text{ g}}{24.6 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.0406 \text{ mol C per gDCW}$$

Step 2 — For glucose ($M_S = 30$ g/mol per C, $\gamma_S = 4$):

$$n_{C,S} = \frac{1 \text{ g}}{30 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.0333 \text{ mol C per g-glucose}$$

Step 3 — Plug into:

$$Y^{\max}_{X/S} = \frac{\gamma_S}{\gamma_X} \cdot \frac{M_X}{M_S}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Y^{\max}_{X/S} &= \frac{4}{4.2} \cdot \frac{24.6}{30} \\
&= 0.9524 \cdot 0.8200 \\
&= 0.781 \text{ gDCW per g-glucose}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — **Realistic yield:** Accounting for ATP maintenance (typically 5–10 mmol ATP gDCW⁻¹ h⁻¹) and oxidative phosphorylation efficiency, the **observed** $Y_{X/S}$ for aerobic yeast on glucose is **0.45–0.50 g/g** — about **60%** of theoretical.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Model Hierarchy

| Property | Black-Box Model | Thermodynamic Model | Cybernetic / MFA Model |
|----------|----------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Mathematical basis** | Linear algebra (mass balance) | Gibbs energy + ATP stoichiometry | ODEs + optimization (FBA) |
| **Variables** | $X, S, P, O_2$ | Adds $EC$, $[ATP]$ | Adds intracellular flux vector $\mathbf{v}$ |
| **Parameters** | $Y_{X/S}$, $\mu_{\max}$ | $Y_{X/ATP}$, $m_{ATP}$ | $\sim 100$–$1000$ fluxes |
| **Predicts enzyme regulation?** | ❌ No | ⚠️ Indirect (via EC) | ✅ Yes |
| **Computational cost** | Low | Medium | High (linear programming) |
| **KTU exam weightage** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Industry use** | Fermentation control | Metabolic engineering | Systems biology |

---

## 3.5 Full Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1:** A chemostat culture of *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* has $\mu = 0.30$ h⁻¹ at a dilution rate. The true growth yield is $Y_G = 0.55$ g/g and the maintenance coefficient is $m_S = 0.05$ g substrate gDCW⁻¹ h⁻¹. Find (a) the specific substrate uptake rate and (b) the observed yield.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
q_S &= \frac{\mu}{Y_G} + m_S \\
&= \frac{0.30}{0.55} + 0.05 \\
&= 0.5455 + 0.05 \\
&= 0.5955 \text{ g substrate gDCW}^{-1} \text{h}^{-1} \\
\boxed{q_S &\approx 0.60 \text{ g g}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

Observed yield:

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{obs} &= \frac{1}{q_S / \mu} = \frac{\mu}{q_S} \\
&= \frac{0.30}{0.5955} \\
\boxed{Y_{obs} &\approx 0.504 \text{ g/g}}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** At higher $\mu$, $Y_{obs} \to Y_G$. At lower $\mu$ (e.g., $\mu = 0.05$ h⁻¹), $Y_{obs}$ drops because more substrate is burned for maintenance — this is exactly what is observed in slow-growing continuous cultures.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2:** Aerobic batch fermentation of *E. coli* on glucose. Initial $[S]_0 = 10$ g/L, final $[S] = 0.4$ g/L, final biomass $X = 4.2$ g/L. Calculate $Y_{X/S}$ and the maintenance fraction $\sigma_S$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta S &= 10 - 0.4 = 9.6 \text{ g/L} \\
Y_{X/S} &= \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S} = \frac{4.2}{9.6} \\
\boxed{Y_{X/S} &= 0.4375 \text{ g/g} \approx 0.44 \text{ g/g}}
\end{aligned}$$

Comparison with theoretical maximum ($0.78$ g/g) shows **56%** efficiency — typical for industrial *E. coli*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Cellular Energy Economy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Substrate Input]
        GLUC[Glucose C6H12O6]
        O2IN[O2 acceptor]
    end

    subgraph CATAB[Catabolism Glycolysis TCA ETC]
        GLY[Embden Meyerhof Pathway]
        TCA[TCA Cycle]
        ETC[Oxidative Phosphorylation]
    end

    subgraph CURRENCY[Energy Currency]
        ATP[ATP pool]
        ECnode[Energy Charge EC]
    end

    subgraph ANAB[Anabolism Biosynthesis]
        PROT[Protein synthesis]
        LIPID[Lipid synthesis]
        NA[DNA RNA]
        CELLWALL[Cell wall]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Outputs]
        XNODE[Biomass X]
        PNODE[Product P]
        CO2NODE[CO2 + Heat]
    end

    GLUC --> GLY
    O2IN --> ETC
    GLY --> TCA
    TCA --> ETC
    ETC -->|generates| ATP
    ATP -->|powers| ANAB
    ATP -->|maintained by| ECnode
    ECnode -->|regulates| GLY
    ECnode -->|regulates| TCA
    ANAB --> XNODE
    GLY --> PNODE
    TCA --> CO2NODE
    ETC --> CO2NODE

    style ATP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style ECnode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style XNODE fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Gold nodes** = energy currency (ATP)
- **Green node** = regulatory thermostat (Energy Charge)
- **Blue node** = final product of interest (Biomass)
- All anabolic branches consume ATP; all catabolic branches regenerate ATP.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Pirt's Two-Component Substrate Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Total qS g per g per h] --> GRO[Growth branch mu divided by YG]
    Q --> MAIN[Maintenance branch mS independent of mu]
    GRO -->|consumes| ATPG[ATP for biosynthesis]
    MAIN -->|consumes| ATPM[ATP for homeostasis ion gradients motility]
    ATPG --> RX[Biomass formation rX]
    ATPM --> HEAT[Dissipation as heat and turnover]

    style Q fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style GRO fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style MAIN fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style RX fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style HEAT fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Model Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Cellular Energetics Models] --> B[Level 1 Black Box Stoichiometric]
    A --> C[Level 2 Thermodynamic Energetic]
    A --> D[Level 3 Structured Cybernetic]

    B --> B1[Mass balance only]
    B --> B2[Yields YXS YXP YXO]
    B --> B3[No internal states]

    C --> C1[Adds ATP balance]
    C --> C2[Adds Gibbs energy]
    C --> C3[Energy Charge EC]

    D --> D1[ODEs with internal metabolites]
    D --> D2[Regulatory enzymes]
    D --> D3[Metabolic Flux Analysis FBA]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Block Architecture — A Bioreactor Energy Budget

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED[Feed Stream]
        S0[S substrate concentration]
    end

    subgraph RX[Reactor Vessel Volume V]
        BIOMASS[X biomass concentration]
        qSNODE[qS uptake]
        ATPBAL[ATP balance dATPdt]
    end

    subgraph EFF[Effluent]
        SF[S final]
        XF[X final]
        PF[P product]
    end

    S0 -->|F in| RX
    RX --> EFF

    RX -.->|tracked by| MON[Online sensors DO pH CO2]
    MON -.->|control loop| CTRL[PID controller]
    CTRL -.->|adjusts| F[F feed rate]
    CTRL -.->|adjusts| AGIT[Agitation rpm]

    style ATPBAL fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style MON fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style CTRL fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
```

**Functional explanation:**
The **gold ATP balance node** is the heart of any energetics model — every other variable ultimately flows from and into this single pool. The **green sensor node** is what makes it observable; the **red controller** is what makes the process industrial.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank Style]**

### Question A.1 (3 Marks)

**Q:** Define **Adenylate Energy Charge (EC)** and explain its significance in cellular metabolism.

**Model Answer:**
The Adenylate Energy Charge, proposed by Daniel E. Atkinson in 1977, is a dimensionless measure of the cell's energetic state defined as:

$$EC = \frac{[ATP] + 0.5\,[ADP]}{[ATP] + [ADP] + [AMP]}$$

EC ranges from **0** (all AMP) to **1** (all ATP). In healthy growing microbes, EC is maintained between **0.70 and 0.95** by adenylate kinase. Most regulatory enzymes — glycolytic kinases and biosynthetic enzymes — are sensitive to EC. When EC is high, ATP-generating catabolism is inhibited and ATP-consuming anabolism is stimulated; the converse happens at low EC. EC thus acts as the cell's **internal thermostat**, preventing simultaneous operation of antagonistic pathways.

---

### Question A.2 (3 Marks)

**Q:** Differentiate between **observed yield (Y_obs)** and **true growth yield (Y_G)**. Why does $Y_{obs}$ depend on $\mu$?

**Model Answer:**
$Y_{obs} = \Delta X / \Delta S$ is the experimentally measured ratio in a real culture. $Y_G$ is the yield **extrapolated** to zero maintenance requirement — i.e., the fraction of substrate that *would* go to biomass if there were no maintenance metabolism. From Pirt's equation, $1/Y_{obs} = 1/Y_G + m_S/\mu$. At low $\mu$, the maintenance term $m_S/\mu$ dominates and $Y_{obs}$ drops sharply. At high $\mu$ (near $\mu_{max}$), $m_S/\mu \to 0$ and $Y_{obs} \to Y_G$. Hence observed yield is growth-rate dependent.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

---

### 🔹 Question B-A (14 Marks) — OPTION A

**Q:** Derive **Pirt's Equation** for substrate consumption in a microbial culture. An aerobic culture of *E. coli* on glucose shows: $Y_G = 0.48$ gDCW/g-glucose, $m_S = 0.03$ g-glucose gDCW⁻¹ h⁻¹. If the specific growth rate is $\mu = 0.40$ h⁻¹, calculate:

**(a)** [7 Marks] The specific substrate uptake rate $q_S$ and the **observed yield** $Y_{obs}$.
**(b)** [7 Marks] The **ATP demand** for growth if $Y_{X/ATP} = 10$ gDCW/mol ATP and the maintenance ATP coefficient is $m_{ATP} = 1.5$ mmol ATP gDCW⁻¹ h⁻¹. Comment on the engineering significance of the maintenance fraction.

---

#### Model Solution to B-A(a):

Applying Pirt's equation directly:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_S &= \frac{\mu}{Y_G} + m_S \\
&= \frac{0.40}{0.48} + 0.03 \\
&= 0.8333 + 0.03 \\
\boxed{q_S &= 0.8633 \text{ g g}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

The **observed yield**:

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{obs} &= \frac{\mu}{q_S} = \frac{0.40}{0.8633} \\
\boxed{Y_{obs} &\approx 0.4633 \text{ g/g}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison:** $Y_{obs}$ (0.4633) < $Y_G$ (0.48) due to maintenance loss. Difference = $0.017$ g/g ≈ **3.5%** substrate lost to maintenance.

---

#### Model Solution to B-A(b):

Cellular ATP demand follows the **same Pirt form**:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{ATP} &= \frac{\mu}{Y_{X/ATP}} + m_{ATP} \\
&= \frac{0.40}{10\,\text{g/mol}} + 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol g}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1} \\
&= 0.0400 + 0.0015 \\
\boxed{q_{ATP} &= 0.0415 \text{ mol ATP gDCW}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Maintenance fraction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{ATP} &= \frac{m_{ATP}}{q_{ATP}} = \frac{0.0015}{0.0415} \\
\boxed{\sigma_{ATP} &\approx 0.0362 = 3.62\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering significance:**
- At low growth rates (chemostat, $\mu \approx 0.05$ h⁻¹), $\sigma_{ATP}$ rises dramatically (often 30–50%) — explains why **fed-batch and high-$\mu$ strategies** are preferred industrially.
- Maintenance ATP drives: (i) PMF-driven solute transport, (ii) futile cycles, (iii) macromolecular turnover.
- Reducing $m_{ATP}$ via strain engineering (e.g., knocking out proteases) directly **increases $Y_{obs}$** without increasing $Y_G$.

---

### 🔹 Question B-B (14 Marks) — OPTION B (Internal Choice)

**Q:** Define **Degree of Reduction (γ)** for a biological substrate. For the following substrates — (i) glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆), (ii) ethanol (C₂H₆O), (iii) methanol (CH₄O), (iv) methane (CH₄) — calculate γ and discuss their implications on **maximum biomass yield** and **oxygen demand**.

**(a)** [7 Marks] Calculate γ for all four substrates and rank them by theoretical electron density per carbon.
**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the engineering implications of high-γ substrates on bioreactor design (oxygen transfer rate, heat removal, cooling).

---

#### Model Solution to B-B(a):

Using the general formula $\gamma = (4n_C + n_H - 2n_O - 3n_N) / n_C$:

**Glucose C₆H₁₂O₆:**
$$\gamma = \frac{4(6) + 12 - 2(6) - 0}{6} = \frac{24 + 12 - 12}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4$$

**Ethanol C₂H₆O:**
$$\gamma = \frac{4(2) + 6 - 2(1) - 0}{2} = \frac{8 + 6 - 2}{2} = \frac{12}{2} = 6$$

**Methanol CH₄O:**
$$\gamma = \frac{4(1) + 4 - 2(1) - 0}{1} = \frac{4 + 4 - 2}{1} = 6$$

**Methane CH₄:**
$$\gamma = \frac{4(1) + 4 - 0 - 0}{1} = 8$$

**Ranking (γ values):**

$$\boxed{\text{Methane (8)} > \text{Ethanol (6)} = \text{Methanol (6)} > \text{Glucose (4)}}$$

**Theoretical $Y_{X/S}^{\max}$ (using $Y^{\max}_{X/S} = \gamma_S M_X / (\gamma_X M_S)$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Methane:}\quad Y^{\max}_{X/S} &= \frac{8 \cdot 24.6}{4.2 \cdot 16} = \frac{196.8}{67.2} = 2.93 \text{ g/g (wet!)} \\
\text{Methanol:}\quad Y^{\max}_{X/S} &= \frac{6 \cdot 24.6}{4.2 \cdot 32} = \frac{147.6}{134.4} = 1.10 \text{ g/g} \\
\text{Ethanol:}\quad Y^{\max}_{X/S} &= \frac{6 \cdot 24.6}{4.2 \cdot 23} = \frac{147.6}{96.6} = 1.53 \text{ g/g} \\
\text{Glucose:}\quad Y^{\max}_{X/S} &= \frac{4 \cdot 24.6}{4.2 \cdot 30} = \frac{98.4}{126} = 0.78 \text{ g/g}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Observation:** Substrates with **higher γ** yield more biomass per gram because more electrons are available for biosynthesis.

---

#### Model Solution to B-B(b):

**Oxygen demand:** Since all electrons from the substrate must eventually go to the terminal acceptor (O₂ for aerobic culture), higher γ implies **larger O₂ requirement**:

$$\frac{\text{g O}_2}{\text{g substrate}} = \frac{\gamma_S}{4} \cdot \frac{32}{M_S}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Methane:} \quad O_2/S &= \frac{8}{4} \cdot \frac{32}{16} = 4.0 \text{ g O}_2 / \text{g CH}_4 \\
\text{Methanol:} \quad O_2/S &= \frac{6}{4} \cdot \frac{32}{32} = 1.5 \\
\text{Ethanol:} \quad O_2/S &= \frac{6}{4} \cdot \frac{32}{23} = 2.09 \\
\text{Glucose:} \quad O_2/S &= \frac{4}{4} \cdot \frac{32}{30} = 1.07
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering implications (this is what KTU examiners want):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher γ ⇒ larger heat release and higher OTR requirement.**

1. **Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR):** Methane fermentation requires OTR **~3.7× higher** than glucose. Reactor must have high $k_La$ (10–20× more than glucose reactor).
2. **Heat removal:** Heat of combustion of substrate ≈ $115\,\gamma$ kJ per e⁻ equivalent. For methane, this is enormous — cooling jacket alone insufficient; **external heat exchangers** mandatory.
3. **Explosion hazard:** Methane–air mixtures are explosive between 5–15% v/v; **closed pressurized reactors** with N₂ blanketing needed.
4. **Substrate solubility:** Methanol and ethanol are water-miscible; methane is **virtually insoluble** (~22 mg/L at 25°C) — requires **pressurized gas-fed reactors** up to 50 bar.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on this topic (READ BEFORE EXAM):**
>
> 1. **Sign errors in Pirt's equation:** Writing $q_S = Y_G \mu - m_S$ instead of $q_S = \mu/Y_G + m_S$. → Lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $Y_{obs}$ and $Y_G$:** Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you forget that $Y_{obs} \leq Y_G$ always.
> 3. **Forgetting N in $\gamma$ formula:** When the substrate contains nitrogen (e.g., urea, amino acids), the term $-3n_N$ **must** appear. Common error: writing $-2n_N$ (incorrect sign convention).
> 4. **Wrong unit of $m_S$:** Examiners look for **g g⁻¹ h⁻¹**, not g/L/h. Easy mark loss.
> 5. **Not commenting on EC regulatory role in energy metabolism:** When asked about ATP, mention **EC** and **Atkinson**, not just ΔG.
> 6. **Skipping the derivation in long-answer questions:** Even if you solve the numerical part correctly, KTU valuation key requires **explicit intermediate algebraic steps**; jumping from $q_S = 0.30/0.55$ to the final answer without writing $\mu/Y_G + m_S$ will lose 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Mixing $\gamma_X$ with $\gamma_S$ in $Y_{X/S}^{\max}$ formula:** Always state both — the numerator uses $\gamma_S$ (substrate) and denominator uses $\gamma_X$ (biomass).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **MUST-MEMORIZE Bullet Points for Last-Night Revision:**

- **ATP hydrolysis ΔG°' = −30.5 kJ/mol** is the universal energy "price" in biology.
- **Energy Charge EC** is dimensionless, bounded in [0, 1], healthy at **0.70–0.95**.
- **Pirt's equation:** $q_S = \mu/Y_G + m_S$ — straight line in $q_S$ vs $\mu$ plot with slope $1/Y_G$ and intercept $m_S$.
- **Degree of reduction γ:** Glucose = 4, Ethanol = 6, Methanol = 6, Methane = 8, Biomass ≈ 4.2.
- **Maximum theoretical yield on glucose = 0.78 g/g** (assume $M_X = 24.6$, $M_S = 30$, $\gamma_X = 4.2$).
- **Observed yield < True yield** always; difference = maintenance loss.
- **Three classical models:** Black-box (algebraic), Thermodynamic (ATP + EC), Structured/Cybernetic (ODEs + FBA).
- **Yobs → YG as μ → μmax** — high-growth = high efficiency.
- **Oxygen demand per gram substrate** scales linearly with γ.
- **Maintenance ATP** dominates at low μ — drives bioreactor design for fed-batch & high-cell-density cultures.
- **Methanol and methane** are high-γ substrates requiring high OTR, high pressure, and robust heat removal.
- **Adenylate kinase equilibrium:** $K_{eq} = [ADP]^2 / ([ATP][AMP]) \approx 1$ — used to derive EC.
- **Atkinson's regulatory rule:** Catabolic enzymes are active at low EC; anabolic enzymes at high EC.
- **Industrial yeast:** $Y_{X/S} \approx 0.45$ g/g-glucose (aerobic), $Y_{X/S} \approx 0.10$ g/g (anaerobic, due to ethanol excretion).

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 4 — MODELS OF CELLULAR ENERGETICS AND METABOLISM**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Cell Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT834 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Single Cell Models ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Bioprocess Engineering, **Single Cell Models** refer to mathematical frameworks developed to describe the quantitative behavior of microbial populations (bacteria, yeast, mammalian cells) at the level of an individual "average" cell. These models mathematically correlate three critical process variables: - **Biomass concentration** ($X$) — measured typically in **g/L** or **g(dry weight)/L** - **Substrate concentration** ($S$) — the carbon/energy source, measured in **g/L** - **Product concentration** ($P$) — the desired metabolite, measured in **g/L** or **U/mL** The classification of single cell models is conventionally divided along **two orthogonal axes**, producing four foundational combinations: | Axis | Category | Description | |---|---|---| | **Structure** | **Unstructured** | Cell treated as a "black box"; no internal components considered | | **Structure** | **Structured** | Cell is compartmentalized into biochemical pools (RNA, protein, DNA, etc.) | | **Segregation** | **Segregated** | Accounts for individual cell-to-cell variations in a population | | **Segregation** | **Unsegregated** | Population treated as a homogeneous lumped average | The most commonly taught "single cell model" in KTU undergraduate bioprocess courses is the **unstructured–unsegregated model**, which assumes a single, uniform, internally featureless cell representing the entire population. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The Module 4 emphasis is on **unstructured single cell models** of microbial growth, substrate utilization, and product formation. Structured and segregated models are typically introduced as conceptual extensions only. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Worker" Model Imagine a single office worker representing an entire company: - **Unstructured Model Analogy**: The boss only tracks the worker's *inputs* (coffee = substrate) and *outputs* (reports = biomass, ideas = product). The boss does **not** know what happens inside the brain. This is the **unstructured** view — input-output, no internals. - **Structured Model Analogy**: The boss actually knows the worker's brain has different chemicals (RNA = "thinking material", Proteins = "writing material", ATP = "energy currency"). The model tracks these internal pools. This is the **structured** view. - **Segregated Model Analogy**: Instead of treating all 500 workers as identical, the boss recognizes that some workers are faster, some slower, some young, some experienced. Each individual has a different "state". This is the **segregated** view. - **Unsegregated Model Analogy**: All 500 workers are averaged into a single "typical worker" whose behavior represents everyone. This is the **unsegregated** view — and combined with unstructured, it gives us the **classic single cell model** studied in this module. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** Single cell models are *predictive tools*. Given initial substrate and biomass, they predict how much product you will get after time $t$ in a fermenter — the foundation of bioreactor design and scale-up. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics The standard biological constant used throughout these models is the **specific growth rate** $\mu$ (mu), measured in **h⁻¹** (per hour). For most industrial microorganisms under optimum conditions, $\mu_{max}$ ranges between **0.1 h⁻¹ and 1.0 h⁻¹**, corresponding to doubling times of approximately **0.7 h to 7 h**. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification Architecture — The Four Quadrants The single cell model space can be visualized as a 2×2 matrix: ``` SEGREGATED UNSEGREGATED ┌──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┐ │ Individual cells │ Population treated │ STRUCTURED │ distinguished; │ as lumped average; │ │ internal pools │ internal pools │ │ tracked │ tracked │ ├──────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┤ UNSTRUCTURED │ Individual cells │ ★ CLASSIC SINGLE │ │ distinguished; │ CELL MODEL │ │ no internal pools │ (Most common in KTU) │ │ tracked │ │ └──────────────────────┴──────────────────────────┘ ``` ### 2.2 The Unstructured-Unsegregated Single Cell Model — Core Logic The model rests on **three coupled rate equations** representing mass balances on biomass, substrate, and product. #### Step 1: Biomass Growth Rate The rate of biomass accumulation is proportional to the existing biomass: $$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X$$ where $\mu$ is the **specific growth rate**. This is **first-order** with respect to biomass — cells divide, producing more cells, which divide again (autocatalytic process). #### Step 2: Substrate Consumption Rate Substrate is consumed for both **growth** (building new cells) and **maintenance** (keeping existing cells alive): $$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\left(\frac{\mu}{Y_{X/S}} + m\right) \cdot X$$ where: - $Y_{X/S}$ = **yield coefficient of biomass on substrate** (g cells / g substrate) - $m$ = **maintenance coefficient** (g substrate / g cells · h) #### Step 3: Product Formation Rate Product formation kinetics are described by the **Luedeking–Piret equation**: $$\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \mu \cdot X + \beta \cdot X$$ - $\alpha$ = growth-associated product formation constant (g product / g cells) - $\beta$ = non-growth-associated product formation constant (g product / g cells · h) The two terms represent: 1. **$\alpha \mu X$** — product made *during* active growth (e.g., ethanol in yeast) 2. **$\beta X$** — product made *regardless* of growth rate (e.g., secondary metabolites like antibiotics) ### 2.3 Monod Kinetics — The Heart of Single Cell Models Jacques Monod (1942) proposed that the specific growth rate $\mu$ depends on substrate concentration $S$ analogously to the Michaelis–Menten equation in enzyme kinetics: $$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S}$$ where: - $\mu_{max}$ = maximum specific growth rate (h⁻¹) - $K_S$ = substrate saturation constant (g/L) — the substrate concentration at which $\mu = \frac{\mu_{max}}{2}$ > [!NOTE] > **Limiting Cases of Monod Equation:** > - When $S \gg K_S$: $\mu \approx \mu_{max}$ (zero-order in $S$, growth is maximal) > - When $S \ll K_S$: $\mu \approx \frac{\mu_{max}}{K_S} \cdot S$ (first-order in $S$, growth is substrate-starved) ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Biomass growth | $\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X$ | $X$ (g/L), $\mu$ (h⁻¹) | Predicting cell density vs. time | | 2 | Monod kinetics | $\mu = \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S+S}$ | $\mu_{max}$ (h⁻¹), $K_S$ (g/L) | Linking growth rate to substrate | | 3 | Substrate consumption | $-\frac{dS}{dt}=\left(\frac{\mu}{Y_{X/S}}+m\right)X$ | $Y_{X/S}$ (g/g), $m$ (g/g·h) | Estimating nutrient feed rate | | 4 | Luedeking–Piret | $\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha\mu X + \beta X$ | $\alpha$ (g/g), $\beta$ (g/g·h) | Predicting product titre | | 5 | Logistic equation | $\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max}\left(1-\frac{X}{X_{max}}\right)X$ | $X_{max}$ (g/L) | Modeling batch growth with carrying capacity | | 6 | Yield coefficient | $Y_{X/S} = \frac{\Delta X}{\vert -\Delta S\vert}$ | (g/g) | Mass balance closure check | | 7 | Substrate inhibition (Andrews) | $\mu = \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S+S+\frac{S^2}{K_I}}$ | $K_I$ (g/L) | High-substrate inhibition modeling | | 8 | Product inhibition | $\mu = \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S+S}\left(1-\frac{P}{P_{max}}\right)$ | $P_{max}$ (g/L) | Ethanol, lactic acid inhibition | | 9 | Tessier kinetics | $\mu = \mu_{max}\left[1-\exp(-S/K)\right]$ | $K$ (g/L) | Alternative to Monod | | 10 | Moser kinetics | $\mu = \mu_{max}\frac{S^n}{K_S+S^n}$ | $n$ (dimensionless) | Generalized Monod form | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Questions typically ask you to **(a)** derive the coupled ODE system, **(b)** integrate analytically for batch operation (assuming $\mu \approx \mu_{max}$ during exponential phase), and **(c)** interpret parameters physically. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Single cell models form the **mathematical backbone** of: - **Industrial fermentation design** — penicillin, ethanol, citric acid, recombinant protein production - **Wastewater treatment** — activated sludge process design - **Scale-up calculations** — translating lab results to 100,000 L reactors - **Process control** — designing feedback loops for substrate feed (fed-batch) - **Bioprocess economics** — optimizing yield $Y_{X/S}$ minimizes raw material costs ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Analytical Solution for Exponential Growth Phase **Problem Setup:** In a batch bioreactor during the early exponential phase, substrate $S \gg K_S$, so $\mu \approx \mu_{max}$ (constant). Initial biomass $X_0$ at time $t = 0$. Find $X(t)$. **Starting Equation:** $$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max} \cdot X$$ **Step 1 — Separation of variables.** Move all $X$ terms to one side and $t$ to the other: $$\frac{dX}{X} = \mu_{max} \, dt$$ **Step 2 — Apply integration limits.** Integrate from $t = 0$ (where $X = X_0$) to $t = t$ (where $X = X$): $$\int_{X_0}^{X} \frac{dX}{X} = \mu_{max} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ **Step 3 — Perform the integrations.** $$\ln(X) - \ln(X_0) = \mu_{max} \cdot t$$ **Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides.** $$X = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu_{max} \cdot t}$$ **Step 5 — Introduce doubling time** $t_d$ (time for $X$ to double, i.e., $X = 2X_0$): $$2X_0 = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu_{max} \cdot t_d}$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $t_d$.** $$2 = e^{\mu_{max} \cdot t_d}$$ $$\ln(2) = \mu_{max} \cdot t_d$$ $$\boxed{t_d = \frac{\ln(2)}{\mu_{max}} = \frac{0.693}{\mu_{max}}}$$ **Interpretation:** This is the **exponential growth law** for microbial cultures. For $\mu_{max} = 0.5$ h⁻¹ (typical for *E. coli*), $t_d = 1.386$ h, meaning the population doubles roughly every 83 minutes. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Monod-Based Substrate Depletion in Batch **Problem Setup:** Under Monod kinetics with constant $Y_{X/S}$ and negligible maintenance, derive $S$ as a function of $X$. **Starting Equations:** $$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S+S} \cdot X \quad \text{...(A)}$$ $$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{\mu_{max}}{Y_{X/S}}\cdot\frac{S}{K_S+S}\cdot X \quad \text{...(B)}$$ **Step 1 — Form the ratio** $\frac{dS}{dX}$ by dividing (B) by (A): $$\frac{dS}{dX} = \frac{-\frac{\mu_{max}}{Y_{X/S}}\cdot\frac{S}{K_S+S}\cdot X}{\mu_{max}\cdot\frac{S}{K_S+S}\cdot X}$$ **Step 2 — Cancel common terms.** The $\mu_{max}$, the $\frac{S}{K_S+S}$, and $X$ all cancel: $$\frac{dS}{dX} = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}}$$ **Step 3 — Integrate.** With initial conditions $X(0) = X_0$ and $S(0) = S_0$: $$\int_{S_0}^{S} dS = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}}\int_{X_0}^{X} dX$$ **Step 4 — Solve.** $$S - S_0 = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}}(X - X_0)$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange to express $S$ in terms of $X$.** $$\boxed{S = S_0 - \frac{X - X_0}{Y_{X/S}}}$$ **Interpretation:** This linear relation is the **mass balance identity** — every gram of new biomass formed consumes exactly $\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}}$ grams of substrate. It is used as a **cross-check** in fermentation experiments: plot measured $S$ vs. $X$, and the slope should equal $-\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}}$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Logistic Equation for Stationary Phase The Monod model fails when $S$ is depleted because it predicts $\mu \to 0$ smoothly, but in reality cells enter stationary phase abruptly due to multiple limitations (substrate, oxygen, pH, space). The **logistic equation** captures this using a "carrying capacity" $X_{max}$: $$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max}\left(1 - \frac{X}{X_{max}}\right) X$$ **Step 1 — Define carrying capacity factor.** Let $f = 1 - \frac{X}{X_{max}}$. When $X \ll X_{max}$, $f \approx 1$ and growth is exponential. When $X \to X_{max}$, $f \to 0$ and growth stops. **Step 2 — Separate variables.** $$\frac{dX}{X(X_{max}-X)/X_{max}} = \mu_{max}\, dt$$ **Step 3 — Use partial fractions.** $$\frac{1}{X(1-X/X_{max})} = \frac{1}{X} + \frac{1}{X_{max}-X}$$ **Step 4 — Integrate from $X_0$ to $X$.** $$\int_{X_0}^{X}\left[\frac{1}{X} + \frac{1}{X_{max}-X}\right] dX = \mu_{max}\int_0^t dt$$ **Step 5 — Solve.** $$\ln\left(\frac{X}{X_0}\right) - \ln\left(\frac{X_{max}-X}{X_{max}-X_0}\right) = \mu_{max}\, t$$ **Step 6 — Combine logs and solve for $X$.** $$\frac{X(X_{max}-X_0)}{X_0(X_{max}-X)} = e^{\mu_{max}\, t}$$ **Step 7 — Final logistic form.** $$\boxed{X(t) = \frac{X_{max}}{1 + \left(\frac{X_{max}-X_0}{X_0}\right)e^{-\mu_{max}\, t}}}$$ **Verification:** - At $t = 0$: $X = \frac{X_{max}}{1+\frac{X_{max}-X_0}{X_0}} = \frac{X_0 \cdot X_{max}}{X_{max}} = X_0$ ✓ - At $t \to \infty$: $X \to X_{max}$ ✓ - When $X \ll X_{max}$: $X \approx X_0 \cdot e^{\mu_{max} t}$ (recovers exponential growth) ✓ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Specific Growth Rate from Batch Data **Problem:** Given experimental biomass data $(t_i, X_i)$ from a batch run, derive $\mu$ using linearization. **Step 1 — Take the log of the exponential growth equation** $X = X_0 e^{\mu t}$: $$\ln(X) = \ln(X_0) + \mu t$$ **Step 2 — Identify linear form.** Plotting $\ln(X)$ vs. $t$ should yield a straight line with: - **Slope** = $\mu$ - **y-intercept** = $\ln(X_0)$ **Step 3 — Compute slope from two data points** $(t_1, X_1)$ and $(t_2, X_2)$: $$\mu = \frac{\ln(X_2) - \ln(X_1)}{t_2 - t_1}$$ This is the **standard experimental method** to determine $\mu_{max}$ from fermentation data — a question KTU examiners frequently ask. ### 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Kinetics Models | Model | Equation | Best For | Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | Monod | $\mu = \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S+S}$ | General bacterial growth on single substrate | Fails at high $S$ if inhibition occurs | | Andrews (Substrate inhibition) | $\mu = \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K_S+S+S^2/K_I}$ | Phenol, methanol, high-sugar fermentations | Two parameters to fit | | Tessier | $\mu = \mu_{max}(1-e^{-S/K})$ | Empirical fits | Lacks mechanistic interpretation | | Moser | $\mu = \mu_{max}\frac{S^n}{K_S+S^n}$ | Generalized fitting | Extra parameter $n$ | | Logistic | $\mu = \mu_{max}(1-X/X_{max})$ | Stationary phase modeling | Empirically derived | | Contois | $\mu = \mu_{max}\frac{S}{K \cdot X + S}$ | Substrate-to-biomass ratio control | Couples $\mu$ to $X$ | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Single Cell Model Information Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Substrate Input
S g per L] --> B[Microbial Cell
Black Box
Unstructured Model] B --> C[Biomass Output
dX by dt equals mu X] B --> D[Product Output
dP by dt equals alpha mu X plus beta X] B --> E[Heat and CO2
Maintenance Energy] F[Monod Regulator
mu equals mu max S over Ks plus S] --> B G[Luedeking Piret Regulator
alpha and beta constants] --> D ``` ### 4.2 Flowchart — Model Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start: Define Bioprocess Goal] --> Q1{Internal
Cell Detail
Required?} Q1 -- Yes --> Structured[Structured Model
Tracks RNA Protein Pools] Q1 -- No --> Unstructured[Unstructured Model
Black Box Approach] Structured --> Q2{Cell to Cell
Variation
Important?} Unstructured --> Q2 Q2 -- Yes --> Segregated[Segregated Model
Population Balance Equations] Q2 -- No --> Unsegregated[Unsegregated Model
Lumped Average Cell] Unsegregated --> KTU[★ KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus
Monod plus Luedeking Piret] Segregated --> Advanced[Advanced Topic
Beyond KTU Scope] Structured --> Advanced ``` ### 4.3 Batch Growth Curve — Typical Phases ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Phase I: Lag Phase
t equals 0 to t lag
dX by dt is near zero] --> B[Phase II: Exponential Phase
mu equals mu max constant
X equals X0 e to power mu max t] B --> C[Phase III: Stationary Phase
Logistic Equation Governs
X approaches X max] C --> D[Phase IV: Death Phase
dX by dt is negative
Cells Lyse] D --> E[End of Batch Run] ``` ### 4.4 Mass Balance Block Diagram for a CSTR Bioreactor ```mermaid flowchart LR In[F_in S_in X_in P_in] --> Reactor[Reactor Vessel
Volume V
Operating Volume] Reactor --> Out[F_out S_out X_out P_out] Reactor -.-> R1[Reaction 1
Biomass Growth
Rate mu X] Reactor -.-> R2[Reaction 2
Substrate Uptake
Rate mu X over Y] Reactor -.-> R3[Reaction 3
Product Formation
Rate alpha mu X plus beta X] R1 --> Reactor R2 --> Reactor R3 --> Reactor ``` ### 4.5 Coupled ODE System — Schematic Relationship ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Substrate S] -- mu depends on S --> mu[Specific Growth Rate mu] mu -- Monod --> X[Biomass X] X -- proportional to X --> dXdt[dX by dt equals mu X] X -- coupled via Y X over S --> dSdt[dS by dt] S -- coupled via Y X over S --> dSdt mu -- alpha mu X term --> dPdt[dP by dt] X -- beta X term --> dPdt dXdt --> X dSdt --> S dPdt --> P[Product P] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each arrow represents a **mathematical coupling**. The three ODEs $dX/dt$, $dS/dt$, $dP/dt$ are *not* independent — they share variables. This is why they must be **solved simultaneously**, either analytically (for simplified cases) or numerically (for full Monod kinetics using Euler's method or Runge–Kutta). ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between structured and unstructured models in bioprocess engineering. Give one example of each.** > **Model Answer:** > - **Unstructured Model:** Treats the cell as a homogeneous "black box" with no internal differentiation. Only bulk variables $X$, $S$, $P$ are tracked. *Example:* Monod model. > - **Structured Model:** Divides the cell into biochemical compartments (RNA, protein, carbohydrate, lipid pools) and tracks each separately. *Example:* Williams' structured model for *E. coli*. > **Key Distinction:** Unstructured models ignore internal cell composition changes; structured models capture them at the cost of higher mathematical complexity. **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Explain the physical significance of the Monod constant $K_S$.** > **Model Answer:** > The Monod constant $K_S$ (g/L) is the **substrate concentration at which the specific growth rate $\mu$ equals half of $\mu_{max}$**, i.e., $\mu = \frac{\mu_{max}}{2}$ when $S = K_S$. > - It is **analogous to the Michaelis constant** $K_M$ in enzyme kinetics. > - It represents the **affinity** of the organism for the substrate: **low $K_S$ means high affinity** (efficient uptake at low substrate levels). > - Typical values: $K_S = 1$ to $10$ mg/L for *E. coli* on glucose. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **[INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B]** --- #### ★ QUESTION A (14 Marks) ★ **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the Monod equation for microbial growth. Explain its limiting cases and how it compares to the Michaelis–Menten equation for enzyme kinetics. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The Luedeking–Piret model describes product formation in many fermentations. Derive the equation and classify the following industrial products as growth-associated, non-growth-associated, or mixed: **(i)** ethanol from *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, **(ii)** penicillin from *Penicillium chrysogenum*, **(iii)** lactic acid from *Lactobacillus* spp. Justify each classification. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Derivation of Monod Equation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Empirical observation (1 mark).** Monod (1942) observed that microbial specific growth rate $\mu$ depends on limiting substrate concentration $S$ in a hyperbolic fashion, mirroring enzyme kinetics. **Step 2 — Mathematical form (1 mark).** $$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S}$$ **Step 3 — Derivation via analogy (2 marks).** By analogy with Michaelis–Menten, $\mu$ corresponds to reaction rate $v$, $\mu_{max}$ corresponds to $V_{max}$, and $K_S$ corresponds to $K_M$. The form arises from a quasi-steady-state assumption on an internal transport intermediate $S_{in}$: $$\mu = k \cdot S_{in}, \quad S_{in} = \frac{S}{K_S + S}$$ **Step 4 — Limiting cases (2 marks).** - **$S \gg K_S$:** $\mu \approx \mu_{max}$ (zero-order; growth limited by intracellular machinery, not substrate) - **$S \ll K_S$:** $\mu \approx \frac{\mu_{max}}{K_S} \cdot S$ (first-order; growth limited by substrate availability) **Step 5 — Comparison with Michaelis–Menten (1 mark).** Both have identical mathematical form. However, Monod's $\mu$ describes *population-level* growth over hours, whereas Michaelis–Menten describes *single-enzyme* rates over seconds. Monod's $K_S$ is typically 100–1000× larger than $K_M$. ##### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Luedeking–Piret Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Empirical form (1 mark).** Luedeking and Piret (1959) proposed: $$\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \mu \cdot X + \beta \cdot X$$ **Step 2 — Term-by-term meaning (2 marks).** - **First term $\alpha \mu X$:** Product synthesized in proportion to growth rate (e.g., primary metabolites tied to biomass synthesis). - **Second term $\beta X$:** Product synthesized by existing biomass independent of growth (e.g., secondary metabolites during stationary phase). **Step 3 — Classifications (4 marks).** | Product | Type | Justification | |---|---|---| | **(i) Ethanol** | **Mixed (mostly growth-associated)** | Ethanol is produced by glycolysis, which peaks during exponential growth; $\alpha > 0$, $\beta > 0$ but $\alpha \gg \beta$. | | **(ii) Penicillin** | **Non-growth-associated** | Penicillin is a secondary metabolite produced *after* exponential phase (idiophase); $\beta > 0$, $\alpha \approx 0$. | | **(iii) Lactic acid** | **Growth-associated** | Lactic acid is a primary metabolite from pyruvate reduction, directly coupled to growth; $\alpha > 0$, $\beta \approx 0$. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose 2–3 marks on **Part (b)** by failing to **explicitly justify each classification with numerical reasoning** (e.g., "$\alpha \gg \beta$ implies growth-associated"). A bare classification without justification will receive *partial credit only*. Always state the relative magnitudes of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ for each product. --- #### ★ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE ★ **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** A batch bioreactor is inoculated with $X_0 = 0.5$ g/L of *E. coli*. The maximum specific growth rate is $\mu_{max} = 0.6$ h⁻¹. Assuming exponential growth conditions, calculate: **(i)** the biomass concentration after 5 hours, **(ii)** the doubling time, and **(iii)** the time required to reach $X = 50$ g/L. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the logistic equation for microbial growth in a batch system and explain why it is more realistic than pure exponential growth for modeling complete fermentation cycles. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Numerical Batch Growth (7 marks) **Given:** $X_0 = 0.5$ g/L, $\mu_{max} = 0.6$ h⁻¹, $X(t) = X_0 \cdot e^{\mu_{max} t}$ **Part (i): Biomass at $t = 5$ h (3 marks)** $$X(5) = 0.5 \cdot e^{0.6 \times 5} = 0.5 \cdot e^{3.0}$$ $$e^{3.0} = 20.085$$ $$\boxed{X(5) = 0.5 \times 20.085 = 10.04 \text{ g/L}}$$ **Part (ii): Doubling time (2 marks)** $$t_d = \frac{\ln(2)}{\mu_{max}} = \frac{0.693}{0.6}$$ $$\boxed{t_d = 1.155 \text{ h} \approx 69.3 \text{ minutes}}$$ **Part (iii): Time to reach $X = 50$ g/L (2 marks)** $$50 = 0.5 \cdot e^{0.6 t}$$ $$\frac{50}{0.5} = e^{0.6 t}$$ $$100 = e^{0.6 t}$$ $$\ln(100) = 0.6 t$$ $$t = \frac{\ln(100)}{0.6} = \frac{4.605}{0.6}$$ $$\boxed{t = 7.68 \text{ h}}$$ ##### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Logistic Equation Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Monod limitation (1 mark).** Pure Monod-based growth assumes unlimited substrate; in reality, $X$ saturates due to substrate depletion, space constraints, and waste accumulation. **Step 2 — Define carrying capacity (1 mark).** Introduce $X_{max}$ — the maximum achievable biomass concentration determined by initial substrate, volume, and other limitations. **Step 3 — Modified rate equation (2 marks).** Replace the Monod term $\frac{S}{K_S+S}$ with an empirical saturation factor $\left(1 - \frac{X}{X_{max}}\right)$: $$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu_{max}\left(1 - \frac{X}{X_{max}}\right) X$$ **Step 4 — Integrate to obtain S-shaped curve (2 marks).** $$X(t) = \frac{X_{max}}{1 + \left(\frac{X_{max} - X_0}{X_0}\right) e^{-\mu_{max} t}}$$ **Step 5 — Why more realistic (1 mark).** The logistic equation captures the **complete fermentation profile**: exponential early growth, deceleration as nutrients deplete, and asymptotic approach to $X_{max}$ — matching experimental observations across lag, exponential, stationary, and decline phases. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Numerical Questions:** For **Q.B(a)**, students lose marks by: > 1. **Rounding $e^{3.0}$ incorrectly** — must show the actual computation or use $e^{3} \approx 20.086$. > 2. **Unit omission** — always state the unit (g/L, h, minutes) with every numerical answer. > 3. **Algebra errors in logarithm manipulation** — when solving $X = X_0 e^{\mu t}$ for $t$, write $\ln(X/X_0) = \mu t$ explicitly before dividing. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — Single Cell Models** - [x] **Four model classes:** Structured/Unstructured × Segregated/Unsegregated → 4 combinations; KTU focuses on **Unstructured + Unsegregated**. - [x] **Core ODE triplet:** Always write all three coupled equations ($dX/dt$, $dS/dt$, $dP/dt$) together — they are the *defining feature* of single cell models. - [x] **Monod equation** is the heart of single cell models: $\mu = \mu_{max} \frac{S}{K_S + S}$. - [x] **$K_S$ interpretation:** Substrate concentration at which $\mu = \mu_{max}/2$; low $K_S$ = high substrate affinity. - [x] **$Y_{X/S}$** = grams of biomass per gram of substrate consumed — the most-used yield coefficient. - [x] **Luedeking–Piret** distinguishes growth-associated ($\alpha$) vs. non-growth-associated ($\beta$) product formation. - [x] **Exponential phase formula:** $X = X_0 e^{\mu_{max} t}$ and doubling time $t_d = \ln(2)/\mu_{max}$. - [x] **Logistic equation** introduces $X_{max}$ (carrying capacity) and models the *complete* batch cycle, including stationary phase. - [x] **Substrate inhibition (Andrews):** Add $S^2/K_I$ term in denominator for high-substrate cases (e.g., phenol degradation, acetic acid toxicity). - [x] **Linearization trick:** Plot $\ln(X)$ vs. $t$ to extract $\mu_{max}$ experimentally — the slope of the straight line in the exponential phase. - [x] **Common industrial mappings:** - Yeast ethanol → growth-associated - Penicillin → non-growth-associated (secondary metabolite) - Lactic acid → growth-associated - Citric acid (Aspergillus) → mixed - [x] **KTU exam pattern:** Always show **Step 1, Step 2, ...** for derivations; always include **units** in numerical answers; always provide **physical interpretation** of mathematical results.

topics.title: Plasmid Replication and Plasmid Stability Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT834 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plasmid Replication and Plasmid Stability Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plasmid Replication — Formal Definition

In **Bioprocess Engineering**, a **plasmid** is a small, double-stranded, circular DNA molecule (typically 1–300 kbp) that exists and replicates independently of the host chromosome inside a bacterial cell (most commonly *Escherichia coli*). **Plasmid replication** is the enzymatically controlled, semi-autonomous duplication process by which a plasmid copies its own DNA using the host's replication machinery, regulated by plasmid-encoded genes such as the *origin of replication* (oriV) and copy-number control elements (e.g., *ColE1-type* iterons, *RNA I/RNA II* antisense pairing system).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> Plasmid replication is the *in vivo* DNA synthesis process wherein plasmid copy number per cell is maintained at a characteristic value (the **copy number**, *n*) determined by the origin of replication sequence, plasmid-encoded regulators (Rom protein, antisense RNAs), and host cell division cycle.

## 1.2 Plasmid Stability — Formal Definition

**Plasmid stability** is defined as the ability of a plasmid to be retained within a microbial population over successive generations in a bioreactor, without being lost, structurally rearranged, or diluted out by faster-growing plasmid-free cells. Mathematically, it is expressed as the **fraction of plasmid-bearing cells (X⁺) to the total cell population (X_total = X⁺ + X⁻)** at any given time.

$$\text{Plasmid Stability } (S) = \frac{X^+}{X^+ + X^-} = \frac{X^+}{X_{total}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Plasmid Stability (KTU Syllabus Mandate)**
> 1. **Segregational Stability** — faithful distribution of plasmid copies into daughter cells at division.
> 2. **Structural Stability** — absence of deletions, insertions, or rearrangements in the plasmid DNA.
> 3. **Host Cell Viability / Plasmid-Host Interaction** — plasmid does not impose a fatal metabolic burden on the host.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Library Photocopying Booth"

Imagine a **library** where every visitor is given a personal **photocopying booth** (the plasmid) that can duplicate a chosen book (your recombinant gene). Each booth can make, say, 50–100 copies (the **copy number**). Every time the visitor (host cell) splits into two (cell division), the booths must be split between the two daughter visitors.

- **If the booth is "well-behaved"**: copies are made and split evenly → both daughters retain the booth → **stable inheritance**.
- **If by random chance one daughter gets zero copies** → that daughter is now a "homeless visitor" who grows faster (no booth to carry) → **segregational plasmid loss**.
- **If the booth is damaged during copying** (a page torn out) → **structural instability**.
- **If carrying the booth makes the visitor tired/slow** → slower growth → **metabolic burden (plasmid cost)**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of plasmid copy number (*n*) as **"insurance"** — more copies per cell mean a statistically smaller chance that any daughter cell inherits *zero* plasmids.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (E. coli) |
|---|---|---|
| Plasmid copy number | *n* | 1 (low copy, e.g., pSC101) — 500+ (high copy, e.g., pUC) |
| Specific growth rate of plasmid-bearing cells | *μ⁺* | 0.3 – 1.0 h⁻¹ |
| Specific growth rate of plasmid-free cells | *μ⁻* | 0.3 – 1.2 h⁻¹ |
| Segregational loss probability | *P₀* | 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻² per generation |
| Metabolic burden coefficient | *α* | 0.05 – 0.30 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Physical Constants / Standard Metrics**
> - **Copy number (*n*)** is the most critical design parameter — it directly controls recombinant protein yield **per cell** and inversely affects the **metabolic burden**.
> - **Dilution rate (*D*)** in chemostat = **specific growth rate (*μ*)** at steady state, a key operational link.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Plasmid Replication

Plasmids replicate using one of **three canonical mechanisms**, each dictated by the origin of replication and replication proteins:

### A. Theta (θ) Replication
- Most common in *E. coli* (e.g., *ColE1*, *pBR322*, *pUC* plasmids).
- Replication forks proceed in **opposite directions** around the circular plasmid, forming a structure resembling the Greek letter θ.
- Requires **RNA primer (RNA II)** for initiation.
- **Copy number control** is mediated by antisense RNA I binding to RNA II, blocking primer maturation.
- *Why it matters:* Predictable, high-fidelity, suitable for industrial recombinant protein production.

### B. Rolling Circle Replication (RCR)
- Common in Gram-positive plasmids (e.g., *pT181* in *Staphylococcus*).
- One strand is nicked, and the 3′-OH end is extended around the circle, displacing the parental strand.
- Generates **single-stranded intermediates** — useful for phage-based cloning (M13).

### C. Strand Displacement Replication
- Seen in plasmids of *Streptococcus* and some broad-host-range plasmids (e.g., *IncQ*, RSF1010).
- A leading strand is synthesized continuously, displacing the parental strand, which is later replicated as a lagging strand.

## 2.2 Why Plasmid Loss Occurs — The "Why" Behind the Phenomenon

Plasmid loss is governed by a **random statistical process at cell division**:

1. A mother cell contains *n* plasmid copies distributed randomly between two daughter cells.
2. If a daughter receives *zero* copies (rare but possible when *n* is small), it becomes plasmid-free (X⁻).
3. The plasmid-free cell has **no metabolic burden** of carrying/replicating the plasmid → it grows faster.
4. In a chemostat, the faster-growing X⁻ cells **wash out** the X⁺ cells progressively.
5. Over time, the entire culture becomes plasmid-free, halting product formation.

## 2.3 Mathematical Model of Plasmid Stability (KTU Favourite)

### Model Assumptions (Standard Moser / Stewart-Levin Framework)
- Chemostat at steady state.
- Two populations: plasmid-bearing (X⁺) and plasmid-free (X⁻).
- Plasmid-free cells arise **only** by segregational loss from X⁺ at rate *P₀*.
- Plasmid loss is a one-way event (X⁻ → X⁺ reversion neglected).
- Both sub-populations obey Monod kinetics with potentially different parameters.

### Key Differential Equations (Chemostat)

For **plasmid-bearing cells (X⁺):**
$$\frac{dX^+}{dt} = (1 - P_0)\,\mu^+ X^+ - D X^+$$

For **plasmid-free cells (X⁻):**
$$\frac{dX^-}{dt} = P_0\, \mu^+ X^+ + \mu^- X^- - D X^-$$

Where:
- *P₀* = probability of forming a plasmid-free daughter per division.
- *μ⁺, μ⁻* = specific growth rates of X⁺ and X⁻ cells.
- *D* = dilution rate (h⁻¹).

### Probability of Zero-Plasmid Daughter (Statistical Foundation)

If *n* copies are distributed randomly between two daughter cells, the probability of a daughter receiving *zero* copies follows a **binomial / Poisson approximation**:

$$P_0 = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n-1} \quad \text{(for perfectly random partitioning)}$$

A more general form (used in the **proportional model**, Noack–Roth model):
$$P_0 = 2^{1-n}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for this topic. Master it.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_0 = 2^{1-n}$ | Probability of plasmid-free daughter per division (random partition) | Segregational loss calculation |
| 2 | $P_0 = (1-p)^{n-1}$ where $p$ is fractional partition error | More general loss model | Non-random partitioning |
| 3 | $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod growth equation | Both X⁺ and X⁻ growth |
| 4 | $D = \mu$ | Chemostat steady state condition | Linking growth and dilution |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dX^+}{dt} = (1-P_0)\mu^+ X^+ - D X^+$ | Plasmid-bearing cell balance | Dynamic modelling |
| 6 | $\dfrac{dX^-}{dt} = P_0 \mu^+ X^+ + \mu^- X^- - D X^-$ | Plasmid-free cell balance | Dynamic modelling |
| 7 | $S_{X^+} = \dfrac{X^+}{X^+ + X^-}$ | Fractional plasmid stability | Stability reporting |
| 8 | $\mu^- = \mu^+ (1 + \beta)$ where $\beta$ = burden coefficient | Faster growth of X⁻ cells | Burden quantification |
| 9 | $\text{Burden} (\%) = \dfrac{\mu^- - \mu^+}{\mu^-} \times 100$ | Metabolic burden (%) | Engineering diagnostics |
| 10 | $\tau_{gen} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | Generation / doubling time | Time-scale of loss |
| 11 | $\Delta t_{50\%} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{P_0 \cdot \mu^+}$ | Time for 50% culture to become plasmid-free | Stability lifetime |
| 12 | $D_{crit} = \mu^+ (1 - P_0)$ | Critical dilution rate below which X⁺ survives | Chemostat design |
| 13 | $q_P = Y_{P/X} \cdot \mu^+$ | Specific product formation rate | Recombinant protein titre |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU exams, **do not confuse** the symbols *P₀* (probability, dimensionless) with *P* (product). Always read the question stem carefully.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Plasmid Stability |
|---|---|
| **Recombinant insulin (E. coli)** | Plasmid stability >95% across 60-h fed-batch is essential for yield and FDA compliance. |
| **Vaccine antigen production** | Loss of plasmid = loss of antigen expression; critical for batch consistency. |
| **Bioremediation (e.g., *Pseudomonas* with degradative plasmids)** | Field-scale stability under non-selective conditions determines efficacy. |
| **Gene therapy vectors** | Stability of viral plasmid intermediates governs manufacturing yield. |
| **Continuous biomanufacturing** | Chemostat operation requires sustained stability over weeks — a major engineering challenge. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Probability of Plasmid-Free Daughter Cell (P₀)

### Starting Point
Consider a mother cell with *n* identical plasmid copies about to divide. Each plasmid is independently and randomly assigned to one of the two daughter cells with probability ½. We seek the probability *P₀* that a given daughter receives **zero** plasmids.

### Step 1 — Binomial Probability Framework
For one plasmid, the probability of going to daughter A is *p = 1/2*, and to daughter B is *q = 1/2*. The events are independent across all *n* plasmids.

### Step 2 — Probability of Daughter A Receiving Zero Plasmids
Using the **binomial distribution**, the probability of *k* successes (plasmid goes to daughter A) in *n* trials, with success probability *p = 1/2*, is:

$$P(k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$$

For daughter A to receive **zero** plasmids, set *k = 0*:

$$P_0 = \binom{n}{0} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^0 \left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right)^n = 1 \cdot 1 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$$

### Step 3 — Symmetry Correction (Daughter A OR Daughter B)
The probability that *either* daughter receives zero plasmids (the **segregational loss event**) is twice that of one specific daughter (since the two events are mutually exclusive only if *n ≥ 2*; for *n = 1*, the events are identical):

$$P_0 = 2 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n = 2^{1-n}$$

> **Note:** When *n = 1*, *P₀ = 1* (certain loss) — physically consistent with single-copy plasmids being lost in every division. When *n = 100*, *P₀ ≈ 10⁻³⁰* (essentially zero loss).

### Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{P_0 = 2^{1-n}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Plasmid Stability in a Chemostat

### Starting Point — At Steady State
At steady state in a chemostat, *dX⁺/dt = 0* and *dX⁻/dt = 0*, so both sub-populations grow at the dilution rate:

$$\mu^+ = D, \quad \mu^- = D$$

But X⁺ cells lose plasmid at rate *P₀* per division. The net effective growth rate of X⁺ is:

$$r^+ = (1 - P_0)\,\mu^+$$

### Step 1 — Steady-State Mass Balance for X⁺
Setting *dX⁺/dt = 0* in the X⁺ equation:

$$(1 - P_0)\,\mu^+ X^+ = D\, X^+$$

Dividing both sides by *X⁺*:

$$(1 - P_0)\,\mu^+ = D \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu^+ = \frac{D}{1 - P_0}$$

### Step 2 — Steady-State Mass Balance for X⁻
Setting *dX⁻/dt = 0*:

$$P_0\,\mu^+ X^+ + \mu^- X^- = D\, X^-$$

Substituting *D = μ⁻* (steady-state for X⁻):

$$P_0\,\mu^+ X^+ + \mu^- X^- = \mu^- X^-$$

### Step 3 — Simplification
The *μ⁻ X⁻* terms cancel:

$$P_0\,\mu^+ X^+ = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X^+ = 0$$

> **Critical Insight:** This shows that in a chemostat with **non-zero** *P₀* and *μ⁻ = D*, the only true steady state is **complete washout of plasmid-bearing cells** — a profoundly important industrial result!

### Step 4 — Apparent Steady State (Practical Engineering View)
In practice, *X⁺* is replenished continuously by feed and never reaches absolute zero. The **apparent steady-state ratio** of X⁺ to X⁻ is derived by re-introducing the inlet feed term *D X_in⁺*:

$$\frac{X^+}{X^-} = \frac{D\, X_{in}^+}{P_0\, \mu^+ X^+ - (\mu^- - D) X^-}$$

For *D = μ⁻* (true X⁻ steady state):

$$\frac{X^+}{X^-} = \frac{D\, X_{in}^+}{P_0\, \mu^+ X^+}$$

This shows that *X⁺/X⁻* is **proportional to 1/P₀** — confirming the **dominant role of copy number** in industrial stability engineering.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time for 50% Culture Loss (Δt₅₀%) in Batch Culture

### Starting Point
In a **batch culture** (no washout, no feed), plasmid-free cells appear by segregational loss. The fraction of plasmid-bearing cells decays with each generation.

### Step 1 — Define Stability Decay Per Generation
After one generation (one cell division), the fraction of X⁺ cells that produce at least one X⁻ daughter is *P₀*. For a small *P₀*, the fractional loss per generation ≈ *P₀*.

### Step 2 — Continuous-Time Approximation
Treating generations as continuous in time, the X⁺ fraction decays **exponentially**:

$$\frac{X^+(t)}{X^+(0)} = (1 - P_0)^{N(t)}$$

where *N(t) = t / τ_gen* is the number of generations elapsed in time *t*.

### Step 3 — Number of Generations to Reach 50% Loss
Setting *X⁺(t) / X⁺(0) = 0.5*:

$$(1 - P_0)^{N_{50}} = 0.5$$

Taking the natural log:

$$N_{50} \cdot \ln(1 - P_0) = \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2$$

$$N_{50} = \frac{\ln 2}{-\ln(1 - P_0)} = \frac{\ln 2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - P_0}\right)}$$

### Step 4 — Convert Generations to Real Time
Using *N₅₀ = t₅₀ / τ_gen = t₅₀ · μ⁺ / ln 2*:

$$t_{50} = \frac{\tau_{gen} \cdot \ln 2}{-\ln(1 - P_0)} = \frac{(\ln 2)^2}{\mu^+ \cdot [-\ln(1 - P_0)]}$$

### Step 5 — Small-P₀ Approximation
For *P₀ << 1*, *−ln(1 − P₀) ≈ P₀*:

$$\boxed{\Delta t_{50\%} \approx \frac{\ln 2}{P_0 \cdot \mu^+}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **KFU/KTU favourite equation** — the "half-life" of plasmid stability in batch culture. Example: For *P₀ = 10⁻⁵* and *μ⁺ = 0.5 h⁻¹*, *t₅₀ ≈ 1386 hours* (~58 days) — justifying why high-copy-number plasmids are essential for long industrial fed-batch runs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Parameters

| Parameter | Increased → Effect on P₀ | Effect on Stability | Industrial Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Copy number *n* | ↓ (exponentially) | ↑↑ | Use high-copy plasmids (e.g., pUC) |
| Growth rate *μ⁺* | Unchanged directly | ↓ (faster generations → more loss events) | Slower feed = better stability |
| Metabolic burden *β* | Indirectly ↑ (more X⁻) | ↓ | Avoid over-expressing toxic genes |
| Dilution rate *D* in chemostat | Unchanged | ↓↓ (washout) | Avoid chemostat for unstable plasmids |
| Temperature ↑ | ↑ (replication errors) | ↓ | Use optimal 37 °C, not higher |
| Selective pressure (antibiotic) | Unchanged | ↑↑ (kills X⁻) | Essential in lab, costly at scale |
| Plasmid size ↑ | ↑ (replication stress) | ↓ | Use minimal plasmid backbone |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Modes of Plasmid Replication (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plasmid Replication] --> B[Theta Mode]
    A --> C[Rolling Circle Mode]
    A --> D[Strand Displacement Mode]
    B --> B1[Two replication forks move in opposite directions]
    B --> B2[Origin: ColE1 type]
    B --> B3[Control: RNA I / RNA II antisense]
    B --> B4[Host: Mainly E. coli]
    C --> C1[Single nick on one strand]
    C --> C2[3 prime end extends around circle]
    C --> C3[Origin: pT181, pC194 type]
    C --> C4[Host: Gram positive bacteria]
    D --> D1[Leading strand continuous synthesis]
    D --> D2[Parental strand displaced]
    D --> D3[Origin: RSF1010, IncQ type]
    D --> D4[Host: Broad host range]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Plasmid Stability Model (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Feed[Inlet Feed X_plus_in] --> CSTR[Chemostat CSTR Reactor]
    CSTR --> Xplus[Plasmid Bearing Cells X_plus]
    CSTR --> Xminus[Plasmid Free Cells X_minus]
    Xplus -->|Segregational loss with probability P_zero| Xminus
    Xminus -->|Faster growth mu_minus greater than mu_plus| CSTR
    Xplus -->|Slower growth with burden beta| CSTR
    CSTR -->|Outflow at dilution rate D| Outlet[Reactor Outlet]
    Xplus --> MonX[Monod Kinetics mu_plus equals mu_max_plus S over Ks_plus plus S]
    Xminus --> MonY[Monod Kinetics mu_minus equals mu_max_minus S over Ks_minus plus S]
    MonX --> CSTR
    MonY --> CSTR
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Cell Division and Random Partitioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Mother Cell with n plasmids] --> D1[Daughter 1]
    M --> D2[Daughter 2]
    D1 -->|Random assignment| P1[Plasmid count k1]
    D2 -->|Complementary count| P2[Plasmid count n minus k1]
    P1 -->|If k1 equals 0| Loss[Plasmid Free X_minus]
    P1 -->|If k1 greater than 0| Keep1[Plasmid Bearing X_plus]
    P2 -->|If n minus k1 equals 0| Loss
    P2 -->|If n minus k1 greater than 0| Keep2[Plasmid Bearing X_plus]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Stability Over Generations (Decay Curve Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Generation 0: 100 percent X_plus] --> T1[Generation 1: 100 into 1 minus P_zero to power 1]
    T1 --> T2[Generation 2: 100 into 1 minus P_zero to power 2]
    T2 --> TN[Generation N: 100 into 1 minus P_zero to power N]
    TN --> Calc{Stability above 95 percent?}
    Calc -->|Yes| Stable[Suitable for industrial batch]
    Calc -->|No| Unstable[Reject or redesign high copy number]
```
